- Documentation: https://docs.projectdiscovery.io/tools/nuclei/overview
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/nuclei/

### 5.8 httpx

#### 1. Tool Introduction

httpx is a fast and multi-purpose HTTP toolkit developed by **ProjectDiscovery**, written in Go, designed to probe large lists of hosts and confirm which are running live HTTP/S services. Beyond simple liveness checks, httpx extracts a wealth of metadata per host: status codes, page titles, content length, technology stack (via built-in Wappalyzer-style fingerprinting), TLS certificate details, response headers, redirect chains, and more. It is built for speed at scale — capable of probing tens of thousands of hosts per minute — and is the standard "second stage" tool in most ProjectDiscovery-based recon pipelines, sitting between subdomain enumeration and vulnerability scanning (Nuclei).

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux
sudo apt update
sudo apt install httpx-toolkit -y   # Kali packages it as httpx-toolkit to avoid conflict with python httpx
httpx -version

# Or install via Go
go install -v github.com/projectdiscovery/httpx/cmd/httpx@latest
```

> **Note:** On Kali, the binary may be installed as `httpx-toolkit` to avoid a naming collision with the unrelated Python `httpx` HTTP client package. Verify with `which httpx-toolkit` or `which httpx`.

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
httpx -u <target> [options]
cat targets.txt | httpx [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options (Key Flags)

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, -target <url>` | Single target URL/host |
| `-l, -list <file>` | File containing list of hosts |
| `-silent` | Show only results, suppress banner |
| `-title` | Display page title |
| `-status-code` | Display HTTP status code |
| `-content-length` | Display response content length |
| `-tech-detect` | Display detected technologies |
| `-web-server` | Display server header |
| `-location` | Display redirect location |
| `-follow-redirects` | Follow HTTP redirects |
| `-follow-host-redirects` | Follow redirects only within the same host |
| `-tls-grab` | Grab TLS certificate details |
| `-cdn` | Detect CDN/WAF provider |
| `-ip` | Display resolved IP address |
| `-cname` | Display CNAME record |
| `-asn` | Display ASN information |
| `-ports <list>` | Probe specific ports (e.g., `80,443,8080`) |
| `-threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default 50) |
| `-rate-limit <n>` | Max requests per second |
| `-timeout <secs>` | Request timeout |
| `-retries <n>` | Number of retries |
| `-mc, -match-code <codes>` | Match only specific status codes |
| `-fc, -filter-code <codes>` | Filter out specific status codes |
| `-mr, -match-regex <regex>` | Match responses via regex |
| `-o, -output <file>` | Write output to file |
| `-json` | Output results in JSON format |
| `-screenshot` | Capture screenshot (headless mode) |
| `-probe` | Display probe status (successful/failed) |
| `-random-agent` | Use a random User-Agent per request |
| `-H, -header <header>` | Custom HTTP header |
| `-x, -method <verb>` | HTTP method (GET, POST, HEAD, etc.) |
| `-proxy <url>` | Route requests through a proxy |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | httpx -silent
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Probe with status code, title, and tech detection**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | httpx -silent -status-code -title -tech-detect
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com [200] [Home of Acunetix Art] [Apache,PHP]
```

**Example 2 — Bulk probing a subdomain list**

```bash
$ cat subdomains.txt | httpx -silent -o live-hosts.txt
```
Output:
```
http://api.example.com
https://www.example.com
https://mail.example.com
[+] 3/50 hosts responded, saved to live-hosts.txt
```

**Example 3 — Filter only 200 OK responses**

```bash
$ cat subdomains.txt | httpx -silent -mc 200
```
Output:
```
https://www.example.com
https://blog.example.com
```

**Example 4 — Probe specific ports across hosts**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | httpx -silent -ports 80,443,8080,8443
```
Output:
```
http://192.168.1.10:8080
https://192.168.1.11:8443
```

**Example 5 — JSON output with full metadata**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | httpx -silent -json | jq .
```
Output:
```json
{
  "url": "https://example.com",
  "status_code": 200,
  "title": "Example Domain",
  "webserver": "cloudflare",
  "tech": ["Cloudflare", "HSTS"],
  "content_length": 1256
}
```

**Example 6 — TLS certificate grabbing**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | httpx -silent -tls-grab -json | jq '.tls'
```
Output:
```json
{
  "issuer_cn": "R3",
  "subject_cn": "example.com",
  "not_after": "2026-10-12T00:00:00Z"
}
```

**Example 7 — Detect CDN/WAF provider**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | httpx -silent -cdn
```
Output:
```
https://example.com [cloudflare]
```

**Example 8 — Screenshot capture**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | httpx -silent -screenshot -srd screenshots/
```
Output:
```
[+] Screenshot saved: screenshots/example.com.png
```

**Example 9 — Chain with subfinder and Nuclei (full pipeline)**

```bash
$ subfinder -d example.com -silent | httpx -silent | nuclei -silent -severity high,critical
```
Output:
```
[CVE-2023-XXXX] [http] [high] https://legacy.example.com
```

**Example 10 — Custom threading and rate limiting for large scans**

```bash
$ cat large-list.txt | httpx -silent -threads 100 -rate-limit 200 -o results.txt
```
Output:
```
[INF] Probing 10000 hosts with 100 threads at 200 req/s
[+] 843 live hosts found, saved to results.txt
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Confirming which subdomains/hosts from a large enumeration list are actually live.
- Enriching host lists with titles, status codes, and technology fingerprints before deeper scanning.
- Detecting CDN/WAF presence at scale prior to running active scanners.
- Screenshotting large numbers of live hosts for visual triage.
- Serving as the connective "liveness + enrichment" stage between subdomain discovery and vulnerability scanning tools like Nuclei.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# httpx-enrich.sh — probe a subdomain list and produce an enriched CSV report

INPUT="subdomains.txt"
OUTPUT="httpx-enriched.json"

cat "$INPUT" | httpx -silent -status-code -title -tech-detect -content-length \
    -tls-grab -cdn -json -o "$OUTPUT"

echo "[*] Building CSV summary..."
jq -r '[.url, .status_code, .title, (.tech // [] | join(";"))] | @csv' "$OUTPUT" > httpx-summary.csv

echo "[+] Done. Summary saved to httpx-summary.csv"
column -t -s, httpx-summary.csv | head -20
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always pipe httpx output into Nuclei/other tools rather than re-resolving hosts manually — it's designed for pipeline chaining.
- Use `-mc`/`-fc` to filter noise (e.g., excluding 404s) before passing results downstream.
- Enable `-tech-detect` early to help decide which Nuclei template tags to run next.
- Tune `-threads` and `-rate-limit` based on target sensitivity — internal assets can handle higher concurrency than public production systems.
- Use `-json` output for all automation; human-readable output is for manual review only.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No output at all | Hosts unreachable or all filtered by `-mc`/`-fc` | Remove filters and re-test connectivity |
| Command not found | Kali package name mismatch | Try `httpx-toolkit` instead of `httpx` |
| Screenshot feature fails | Missing headless browser dependency | Install Chromium: `sudo apt install chromium` |
| Very slow on large lists | Low thread count | Increase `-threads` (test target tolerance first) |
| False tech-detect results | Fingerprint DB outdated | Update httpx binary to latest release |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/projectdiscovery/httpx
- Documentation: https://docs.projectdiscovery.io/tools/httpx/overview
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/httpx/

### 5.9 Aquatone

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Aquatone is a Go-based tool created by **Michael Henriksen (michenriksen)** for visual reconnaissance of large numbers of websites. It takes a list of hosts, discovers open HTTP/S ports, captures a screenshot of each responding web service using a headless Chrome/Chromium instance, and compiles everything into a single, browsable HTML report clustered by page similarity. This lets a tester quickly scan hundreds or thousands of hosts visually — spotting login panels, default install pages, error pages, and unusual applications — far faster than manually visiting each URL.

#### 2. How to Install

Aquatone is not always pre-installed on Kali; install via the Go toolchain or precompiled binary:

```bash
# Install via Go
go install github.com/michenriksen/aquatone@latest

# Or download precompiled binary release
wget https://github.com/michenriksen/aquatone/releases/download/v1.7.0/aquatone_linux_amd64_1.7.0.zip
unzip aquatone_linux_amd64_1.7.0.zip -d aquatone
sudo mv aquatone/aquatone /usr/local/bin/
aquatone -version
```

Chromium is required for screenshotting:

```bash
sudo apt install chromium -y
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
cat hosts.txt | aquatone [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-out <dir>` | Output directory for report and screenshots (default: `.`) |
| `-ports <list>` | Ports to scan (default: common web ports; use `xlarge`, `large`, `medium` presets) |
| `-threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads |
| `-http-timeout <ms>` | HTTP request timeout in milliseconds |
| `-scan-timeout <ms>` | Port scan timeout in milliseconds |
| `-screenshot-timeout <ms>` | Timeout for screenshot capture |
| `-resolution <WxH>` | Screenshot resolution (default `1440,900`) |
| `-chrome-path <path>` | Path to Chrome/Chromium binary |
| `-save-body` | Save raw HTTP response bodies |
| `-nmap` | Parse input as Nmap XML instead of plain host list |
| `-session <file>` | Load a previous Aquatone session file to resume/re-render |
| `-proxy <url>` | Route HTTP requests through a proxy |
| `-silent` | Suppress banner and progress output |
| `-template-path <path>` | Custom HTML report template |
| `-version` | Show Aquatone version |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -out ./aquatone-report
```
Output:

```
aquatone v1.7.0 started at 2026-07-19T10:30:00+05:00
----------------------------------------------------
Targets    : 25
Threads    : 10
Ports      : 80, 443, 8000, 8080, 8443
Output dir : ./aquatone-report

http://192.168.1.10 => 200 OK (Apache httpd)
https://192.168.1.11 => 200 OK (nginx)
http://192.168.1.12:8080 => 401 Unauthorized (Jetty)

Wrote HTML report to: aquatone-report/aquatone_report.html
Session data written to: aquatone-report/aquatone_session.json

aquatone is done: 25 hosts scanned in 42.3 seconds
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Basic scan of a small host list**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -out results/
```
Output:
```
Targets: 10, Threads: 10
Wrote HTML report to: results/aquatone_report.html
```

**Example 2 — Scan with an extended port list**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -ports xlarge -out results-full/
```
Output:
```
Ports: 80,81,300,443,591,593,832,981,1010,1311,2082,2087,2095,...(4400+ ports)
Scanning 10 hosts across 20 ports each...
```

**Example 3 — Parse Nmap XML output directly**

```bash
$ nmap -p 80,443,8080 -oX scan.xml 192.168.1.0/24
$ cat scan.xml | aquatone -nmap -out nmap-aquatone/
```
Output:
```
Parsed 254 hosts from Nmap XML
32 hosts had open web ports
Wrote HTML report to: nmap-aquatone/aquatone_report.html
```

**Example 4 — Increase thread count for faster scanning**

```bash
$ cat large-hosts.txt | aquatone -threads 30 -out fast-scan/
```
Output:
```
Threads: 30
1000 hosts scanned in 3m18s
```

**Example 5 — Route through Burp proxy for combined recon**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080 -out proxied-scan/
```
Output:
```
[+] Using proxy 127.0.0.1:8080 for all requests
Wrote HTML report to: proxied-scan/aquatone_report.html
```

**Example 6 — Save raw response bodies for later grep analysis**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -save-body -out with-bodies/
$ grep -rl "admin panel" with-bodies/html/
```
Output:
```
with-bodies/html/192.168.1.15__443.html
```

**Example 7 — Custom screenshot resolution**

```bash
$ cat hosts.txt | aquatone -resolution 1920,1080 -out hd-screens/
```
Output:
```
Screenshot resolution set to 1920x1080
Wrote HTML report to: hd-screens/aquatone_report.html
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Visual triage of hundreds/thousands of discovered hosts after a large subdomain enumeration.
- Rapid identification of login panels, admin interfaces, and default installation pages across an internal network.
- Clustering visually similar pages to detect widespread misconfigurations across many hosts.
- Combining Nmap port scan results directly into a visual report via `-nmap`.
- Providing screenshot evidence for penetration test reports.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# aquatone-pipeline.sh — subdomain enum -> httpx liveness -> aquatone screenshots

DOMAIN="$1"
OUTDIR="aquatone-recon-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Enumerating subdomains..."
subfinder -d "$DOMAIN" -silent -o "$OUTDIR/subdomains.txt"

echo "[*] Confirming live hosts..."
cat "$OUTDIR/subdomains.txt" | httpx -silent -o "$OUTDIR/live.txt"

echo "[*] Running Aquatone for visual recon..."
cat "$OUTDIR/live.txt" | aquatone -out "$OUTDIR/aquatone" -threads 20

echo "[+] Report ready at $OUTDIR/aquatone/aquatone_report.html"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Feed Aquatone a pre-filtered live-host list (via httpx) instead of raw subdomains to save scan time.
- Use the `-nmap` flag when you already have detailed Nmap scan data to avoid redundant port scanning.
- Open the generated HTML report in a browser — it groups screenshots by visual similarity, making anomalies easy to spot.
- Increase `-threads` cautiously; too high can overload the local Chromium instance or the target network.
- Use `-save-body` when you need to grep response content later without re-requesting each host.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Screenshots all blank/black | Chromium sandboxing issue | Run with `--no-sandbox` Chromium flag or as non-root user |
| `chrome not found` error | Chromium not installed or wrong path | Install Chromium and set `-chrome-path` explicitly |
| Very slow scanning | High port count (`xlarge`) on many hosts | Use `medium`/`large` preset or reduce host list size |
| Report shows 0 screenshots | All hosts returned non-HTTP responses | Verify target list with `httpx` first |
| Out-of-memory on very large scans | Too many concurrent Chromium instances | Lower `-threads` value |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/michenriksen/aquatone
- Releases page: https://github.com/michenriksen/aquatone/releases
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/aquatone/

### 5.10 EyeWitness

#### 1. Tool Introduction

EyeWitness is a Python-based reconnaissance tool developed by the **FortyNorth Security** team, designed to take screenshots of websites, gather server header information, and identify default credentials where possible. It supports HTTP, HTTPS, and RDP services, and can process input from Nmap XML/Nessus files as well as plain text host lists. Unlike Aquatone (Go/Chromium-based), EyeWitness uses Selenium with a headless Firefox or Chrome driver, and produces a self-contained HTML report with clickable thumbnails, source-code preview, and a "Category" view that groups hosts by default-creds signatures, high-value targets, and non-responsive hosts.

#### 2. How to Install

Pre-installed on Kali Linux. To verify or reinstall:

```bash
which eyewitness
sudo apt update
sudo apt install eyewitness -y
eyewitness --version
```

Install from source (for latest features):

```bash
git clone https://github.com/FortyNorthSecurity/EyeWitness.git
cd EyeWitness/Python/setup
sudo ./setup.sh
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
eyewitness [--web | --rdp | --headless] -f <targets_file> [options]
eyewitness --web --single <url> [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--web` | Screenshot HTTP/HTTPS services |
| `--rdp` | Screenshot RDP services |
| `--headless` | Screenshot only, no protocol-specific parsing |
| `-f <file>` | File containing list of URLs/hosts |
| `--single <url>` | Screenshot a single URL |
| `-x <file>` | Parse Nmap XML file as input |
| `--nessus <file>` | Parse Nessus scan file as input |
| `-d <dir>` | Output directory for report |
| `--timeout <secs>` | Max time to wait for a response (default 7) |
| `--threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads |
| `--max-retries <n>` | Retry attempts on failed connections |
| `--resolve` | Resolve IP addresses to hostnames |
| `--no-prompt` | Skip interactive prompts (for automation) |
| `--user-agent <string>` | Custom User-Agent string |
| `--proxy-ip <ip>` | Proxy IP address |
| `--proxy-port <port>` | Proxy port |
| `--proxy-type <type>` | Proxy type (http/socks5) |
| `--difference <n>` | Similarity threshold for grouping screenshots |
| `--jitter <n>` | Add random delay (%) between requests |
| `--show-selenium` | Show the Selenium browser window (non-headless debug mode) |
| `--resolve-ip` | Show resolved IPs in report |
| `--ocr` | Enable OCR text extraction on screenshots |
| `--only-good` | Only report on hosts that responded successfully |
| `--cycle <secs>` | Delay between screenshot cycles |
| `--results <n>` | Limit report to N results per page |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f urls.txt -d eyewitness-report --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
##############################################################################################
#                        EyeWitness                           #
##############################################################################################
Starting Web Requests (25 Hosts)
[*] Attempting to screenshot http://192.168.1.10
[*] Attempting to screenshot https://192.168.1.11
[*] Attempting to screenshot http://192.168.1.12:8080

Done! Report written to eyewitness-report/report.html
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Screenshot a single target**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web --single https://testphp.vulnweb.com -d single-target --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
[*] Attempting to screenshot https://testphp.vulnweb.com
[+] Screenshot saved: single-target/screens/testphp.vulnweb.com.png
Report written to single-target/report.html
```

**Example 2 — Bulk scan from a target list**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f subdomains.txt -d bulk-report --no-prompt --threads 10
```
Output:
```
Starting Web Requests (150 Hosts)
[*] 142/150 completed successfully
[!] 8 hosts timed out
Report written to bulk-report/report.html
```

**Example 3 — Import Nmap XML scan directly**

```bash
$ nmap -p 80,443,8080 -oX scan.xml 192.168.1.0/24
$ eyewitness -x scan.xml -d nmap-eyewitness --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
[*] Parsing Nmap XML file: scan.xml
[*] Found 34 web services
Starting Web Requests (34 Hosts)
Report written to nmap-eyewitness/report.html
```

**Example 4 — Import Nessus scan results**

```bash
$ eyewitness --nessus scan.nessus -d nessus-report --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
[*] Parsing Nessus file: scan.nessus
[*] Found 58 HTTP/HTTPS services
Report written to nessus-report/report.html
```

**Example 5 — Scan through a SOCKS proxy**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f targets.txt --proxy-ip 127.0.0.1 --proxy-port 9050 --proxy-type socks5 -d proxied-scan --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
[*] Using SOCKS5 proxy 127.0.0.1:9050
Starting Web Requests (10 Hosts)
Report written to proxied-scan/report.html
```

**Example 6 — Increase timeout for slow internal hosts**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f internal-hosts.txt --timeout 15 -d internal-report --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
Starting Web Requests (40 Hosts)
[*] Timeout set to 15 seconds
[+] 38/40 completed successfully
```

**Example 7 — Custom User-Agent for evasion**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f targets.txt --user-agent "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64)" -d ua-scan --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
Starting Web Requests (20 Hosts)
Report written to ua-scan/report.html
```

**Example 8 — Only report successful responses**

```bash
$ eyewitness --web -f targets.txt --only-good -d filtered-report --no-prompt
```
Output:
```
Starting Web Requests (100 Hosts)
[*] Filtering: only successful responses included
[+] 67 hosts included in final report
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Rapid visual triage of internal network web services during an internal penetration test.
- Reviewing large external attack-surface screenshots for default credential pages and admin panels.
- Combining directly with Nmap/Nessus scan output to skip manual URL list creation.
- Identifying default installation pages (Tomcat manager, phpMyAdmin, Jenkins) across many hosts.
- Producing client-ready screenshot evidence in penetration test reports.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# eyewitness-from-nmap.sh — scan a subnet, extract web ports, and screenshot everything

SUBNET="$1"
OUTDIR="eyewitness-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Running Nmap scan on $SUBNET..."
nmap -p 80,443,8000,8080,8443 -oX "$OUTDIR/scan.xml" "$SUBNET"

echo "[*] Running EyeWitness against discovered web services..."
eyewitness -x "$OUTDIR/scan.xml" -d "$OUTDIR/report" --no-prompt --threads 10

echo "[+] Report ready: $OUTDIR/report/report.html"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always use `--no-prompt` in automated/scripted runs — the interactive prompt otherwise blocks execution.
- Feed EyeWitness Nmap XML directly (`-x`) rather than manually building target lists when Nmap data already exists.
- Use `--jitter` on external assessments to add randomized timing and reduce detection footprint.
- Increase `--timeout` for slow internal legacy systems to avoid false negatives.
- Review the report's "Category" grouping first — it surfaces default credential pages automatically.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `geckodriver`/`chromedriver` not found | Selenium driver missing | Install via `sudo apt install firefox-esr` and re-run setup.sh |
| Many hosts show blank screenshots | Timeout too short | Increase `--timeout` value |
| Report generation fails | Output directory permission issue | Use a writable `-d` path |
| Interactive prompt hangs in CI/script | Missing `--no-prompt` flag | Always add `--no-prompt` for automation |
| Proxy connections fail | Wrong `--proxy-type` | Match proxy type exactly (http vs socks5) |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/FortyNorthSecurity/EyeWitness
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/eyewitness/
- FortyNorth Security blog: https://www.fortynorthsecurity.com/

### 5.11 hakrawler

#### 1. Tool Introduction

hakrawler is a fast, lightweight web crawler written in Go by **Luke Stephens (hakluke)**, designed for quickly discovering endpoints, URLs, JavaScript files, forms, and subdomains from a target website. It is built for speed and simplicity — it is meant to be piped together with other recon tools in a Unix-style pipeline rather than used as a full standalone crawler like Burp or ZAP's spider. hakrawler can crawl both statically-linked HTML content and, with additional flags, discover URLs referenced inside JavaScript files, making it a staple in bug bounty and fast recon workflows.

#### 2. How to Install

Not pre-installed on Kali by default; install via Go:

```bash
# Requires Go installed
sudo apt update
sudo apt install golang-go -y

go install github.com/hakluke/hakrawler@latest

# Ensure GOPATH/bin is in your PATH
export PATH=$PATH:$(go env GOPATH)/bin
hakrawler -h
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
echo <url> | hakrawler [options]
cat urls.txt | hakrawler [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-d, -depth <n>` | Maximum crawl depth (default 2) |
| `-h, -headers` | Include custom headers (read from stdin, format `Header: Value`) |
| `-i, -insecure` | Disable TLS certificate verification |
| `-json` | Output results as JSON |
| `-plain` | Output plain URLs only (no source labels) |
| `-proxy <url>` | Route requests through a proxy |
| `-s, -subs` | Include subdomains found during crawl |
| `-t, -threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default 8) |
| `-timeout <secs>` | Request timeout (default 3s) |
| `-u, -unique` | Output only unique URLs |
| `-w, -wayback` | Include URLs from the Wayback Machine |
| `-usewayback` | Alias for wayback inclusion |
| `-scope <toplevel\|subs>` | Restrict crawl scope |
| `-outdir <dir>` | Output directory for results |
| `-forms` | Include HTML form details in output |
| `-size <bytes>` | Max response size to process |
| `-auth <token>` | Add an Authorization header value |
| `-sitemap` | Parse `sitemap.xml` for additional URLs |
| `-robots` | Parse `robots.txt` for additional URLs |
| `-linkfinder` | Extract endpoints from JavaScript files |

> Note: hakrawler's flags have shifted slightly across versions; run `hakrawler -h` to confirm the exact flag set for your installed build.

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler
```
Output:
```
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/index.php
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/categories.php
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
[script] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/js/jquery.min.js
[form] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Crawl with increased depth**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -depth 3
```
Output:
```
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/index.php
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=2
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=1
```

**Example 2 — Include subdomains in scope**

```bash
$ echo "https://example.com" | hakrawler -subs
```
Output:
```
[href] https://example.com/about
[href] https://blog.example.com/post-1
[href] https://api.example.com/v1/users
```

**Example 3 — Output unique URLs only, sorted**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -unique | sort
```
Output:
```
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/categories.php
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/index.php
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
```

**Example 4 — Crawl multiple targets from a file**

```bash
$ cat targets.txt | hakrawler -t 20 -depth 2
```
Output:
```
[href] https://target1.com/page1
[href] https://target2.com/dashboard
[href] https://target3.com/api/v1/status
```

**Example 5 — JSON output for automation**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -json | jq -r '.URL'
```
Output:
```
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php
```

**Example 6 — Crawl through Burp proxy**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
```
Output:
```
[+] Routing through proxy 127.0.0.1:8080
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/index.php
```

**Example 7 — Include Wayback Machine historical URLs**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -wayback
```
Output:
```
[href] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/index.php
[wayback] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/old-login.php
[wayback] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/backup.sql
```

**Example 8 — Extract forms for injection testing**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -forms
```
Output:
```
[form] action=/search.php method=POST params=[searchFor,goButton]
[form] action=/login.php method=POST params=[uname,pass]
```

**Example 9 — Chain with httpx and Nuclei**

```bash
$ echo "https://testphp.vulnweb.com" | hakrawler -depth 2 -unique | httpx -silent | nuclei -silent
```
Output:
```
[http-missing-security-headers] [http] [info] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Fast endpoint discovery as a first pass before running deeper crawlers like katana.
- Building URL lists to feed directly into Nuclei, httpx, or manual Burp analysis.
- Extracting form parameters as candidates for injection-based testing.
- Discovering JavaScript-embedded endpoints for API reconnaissance.
- Quick single-command crawl during time-constrained bug bounty recon.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# hakrawler-recon.sh — crawl a domain and prepare a clean, deduplicated URL list

DOMAIN="$1"
OUTFILE="hakrawler-urls-$(echo $DOMAIN | tr '.' '_').txt"

echo "https://$DOMAIN" | hakrawler -subs -depth 3 -unique -forms > "$OUTFILE"

echo "[*] Deduplicating and sorting..."
sort -u "$OUTFILE" -o "$OUTFILE"

echo "[+] $(wc -l < "$OUTFILE") unique URLs saved to $OUTFILE"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `-depth` conservatively on large sites — depth 3+ can generate an enormous number of requests quickly.
- Combine `-subs` with scope restriction carefully to avoid crawling unrelated third-party domains linked from the page.
- Pipe hakrawler directly into `httpx` and `nuclei` for a compact one-liner recon-to-scan chain.
- Use `-forms` output specifically when preparing an SQLi/XSS injection test plan.
- hakrawler is intentionally minimal — for JavaScript-heavy SPAs, prefer katana with headless rendering for better coverage.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No output produced | Target blocks default crawler User-Agent | Add custom headers via `-h` or `-headers` |
| Command not found | Go bin path not exported | Add `$(go env GOPATH)/bin` to `PATH` |
| Very few URLs discovered on SPA | JavaScript not executed during crawl | Use katana with `-headless` instead |
| TLS handshake errors | Self-signed/internal cert | Add `-insecure` flag |
| Crawl takes too long | Deep recursion on large site | Lower `-depth` or add `-timeout` |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/hakluke/hakrawler
- Author's blog/tools: https://hakluke.com/
- Kali/Go install guide: https://github.com/hakluke/hakrawler#installation

### 5.12 katana

#### 1. Tool Introduction

katana is a next-generation crawling and spidering framework developed by **ProjectDiscovery**, written in Go, designed to handle both traditional server-rendered HTML sites and modern JavaScript-heavy Single Page Applications (SPAs). Unlike simpler crawlers, katana supports a **headless browsing mode** (via chromedp) that fully renders JavaScript before extracting links, form actions, and API calls — making it far more effective against React/Angular/Vue applications. It also supports scope control, output filtering, passive-source integration, and direct piping into other ProjectDiscovery tools like httpx and Nuclei.

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux
sudo apt update
sudo apt install katana -y
katana -version

# Or via Go
go install github.com/projectdiscovery/katana/cmd/katana@latest
```

Headless mode requires Chromium:

```bash
sudo apt install chromium -y
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
katana -u <target> [options]
katana -list <targets_file> [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options (Key Flags)

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, -list <url/file>` | Target URL or file with list of URLs |
| `-d, -depth <n>` | Maximum crawl depth (default 3) |
| `-jc, -js-crawl` | Enable crawling/parsing of JavaScript files |
| `-hl, -headless` | Enable headless browser-based crawling |
| `-sc, -system-chrome` | Use local installed Chrome instead of downloading one |
| `-xhr, -xhr-extraction` | Extract XHR/API requests during headless crawl |
| `-jsluice` | Use jsluice for deeper JS endpoint extraction |
| `-c, -concurrency <n>` | Concurrent workers (default 10) |
| `-p, -parallelism <n>` | Concurrent parallel crawls |
| `-rd, -rate-limit-domain <n>` | Rate limit per domain |
| `-rl, -rate-limit <n>` | Global rate limit (requests/sec) |
| `-kf, -known-files <type>` | Fetch known files (`robotstxt`, `sitemapxml`, `all`) |
| `-fs, -field-scope <scope>` | Scope: `rdn` (root domain), `fqdn`, `dn` |
| `-cs, -crawl-scope <regex>` | Regex-based crawl scope |
| `-cos, -crawl-out-scope <regex>` | Exclude regex from crawl scope |
| `-mdc, -match-condition <cond>` | Match based on custom DSL condition |
| `-f, -field <field>` | Extract specific field: url, qurl, path, fqdn, rdn, rurl |
| `-em, -extension-match <ext>` | Match specific extensions |
| `-ef, -extension-filter <ext>` | Filter out specific extensions |
| `-o, -output <file>` | Write output to file |
| `-j, -jsonl` | Output as JSON Lines |
| `-silent` | Suppress banner, show only results |
| `-nc, -no-color` | Disable colored output |
| `-timeout <secs>` | Request timeout |
| `-retry <n>` | Retry count on failure |
| `-proxy <url>` | Route requests through proxy |
| `-H, -headers <header>` | Custom HTTP headers |
| `-form-extraction` | Extract HTML form details |
| `-tls-impersonate` | TLS fingerprint randomization/impersonation |
| `-aff, -automatic-form-fill` | Automatically fill and submit discovered forms |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ katana -u https://testphp.vulnweb.com
```
Output:
```
   __        __                 
  / /_____ _/ /____ ____  ___ _
 /  '_/ _  / __/ _  / _ \/ _  /
/_/\_\\_,_/\__/\_,_/_//_/\_,_/  v1.1.2

		projectdiscovery.io

[INF] Current katana version: v1.1.2
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/categories.php
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Headless crawl of a JavaScript SPA**

```bash
$ katana -u https://example-spa.com -headless -jc
```
Output:
```
[INF] Starting headless crawl...
https://example-spa.com/
https://example-spa.com/api/v1/products
https://example-spa.com/api/v1/user/profile
https://example-spa.com/static/app.bundle.js
```

**Example 2 — Extract XHR/API requests specifically**

```bash
$ katana -u https://example-spa.com -headless -xhr -jsonl | jq '.request.endpoint'
```
Output:
```json
"https://example-spa.com/api/v1/login"
"https://example-spa.com/api/v1/cart"
```

**Example 3 — Crawl with scope restricted to root domain**

```bash
$ katana -u https://example.com -fs rdn -d 4
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/
https://example.com/blog/post-1
https://sub.example.com/dashboard
```

**Example 4 — Extract only unique query-string URLs**

```bash
$ katana -u https://testphp.vulnweb.com -f qurl
```
Output:
```
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/listproducts.php?cat=1
```

**Example 5 — Filter by file extension (only JS files)**

```bash
$ katana -u https://example.com -em js
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/static/main.js
https://example.com/static/vendor.js
```

**Example 6 — Crawl a list of targets and save JSONL**

```bash
$ katana -list targets.txt -jsonl -o katana-results.jsonl
$ jq -r '.request.endpoint' katana-results.jsonl | head -3
```
Output:
```
https://target1.com/
https://target1.com/about
https://target2.com/login
```

**Example 7 — Known-files discovery (robots.txt, sitemap.xml)**

```bash
$ katana -u https://example.com -kf all
```
Output:
```
[INF] Found robots.txt with 12 disallowed paths
[INF] Found sitemap.xml with 84 URLs
https://example.com/admin (from robots.txt)
https://example.com/archive/2024 (from sitemap.xml)
```

**Example 8 — Route through Burp for combined manual/automated recon**

```bash
$ katana -u https://testphp.vulnweb.com -proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
```
Output:
```
[INF] Proxy configured: http://127.0.0.1:8080
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
```

**Example 9 — Chain with httpx and Nuclei**

```bash
$ katana -u https://testphp.vulnweb.com -silent | httpx -silent | nuclei -silent -tags xss,sqli
```
Output:
```
[sqli-error-based] [http] [high] https://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
```

**Example 10 — Automatic form filling and submission**

```bash
$ katana -u https://testphp.vulnweb.com -aff -form-extraction
```
Output:
```
[INF] Discovered form: /search.php (method=GET, field=searchFor)
[INF] Auto-filled and submitted form
https://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?searchFor=test&goButton=go
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Deep crawling of modern JavaScript SPAs where traditional crawlers fail to find links.
- API endpoint discovery via XHR/fetch interception during headless rendering.
- Building comprehensive, scope-restricted URL inventories for large bug bounty targets.
- Extracting and testing HTML forms automatically for injection points.
- Feeding structured JSONL crawl data into custom analysis or reporting pipelines.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# katana-full-crawl.sh — headless crawl + API extraction + Nuclei scan pipeline

TARGET="$1"
OUTDIR="katana-scan-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Running headless crawl with XHR extraction..."
katana -u "$TARGET" -headless -jc -xhr -jsonl -o "$OUTDIR/crawl.jsonl" -silent

echo "[*] Extracting unique endpoints..."
jq -r '.request.endpoint' "$OUTDIR/crawl.jsonl" | sort -u > "$OUTDIR/endpoints.txt"

echo "[*] Probing endpoints with httpx..."
cat "$OUTDIR/endpoints.txt" | httpx -silent -o "$OUTDIR/live-endpoints.txt"

echo "[*] Running Nuclei against live endpoints..."
nuclei -l "$OUTDIR/live-endpoints.txt" -silent -o "$OUTDIR/nuclei-findings.txt"

echo "[+] Pipeline complete. Findings: $OUTDIR/nuclei-findings.txt"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `-headless` and `-jc` together for full JavaScript-rendered coverage of modern web apps.
- Restrict scope with `-fs rdn` or `-cs`/`-cos` to prevent crawling unrelated third-party domains.
- Use `-kf all` early in a crawl to quickly pick up robots.txt/sitemap.xml disclosed paths.
- Combine `-xhr` with headless mode specifically when targeting API-driven SPA back ends.
- Pipe results directly into httpx and Nuclei for a compact, efficient end-to-end pipeline.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Headless mode fails to launch | Missing Chromium | `sudo apt install chromium` and retry, or use `-sc` for system Chrome |
| Crawl misses SPA routes | JS not executed | Ensure `-headless -jc` are both enabled |
| Too many out-of-scope results | No scope restriction set | Use `-fs rdn` or `-cs` with a regex |
| Extremely slow crawl | High depth + headless overhead | Reduce `-d` depth or increase `-c` concurrency carefully |
| Rate-limited/blocked by target | No rate limiting configured | Set `-rl` and `-rd` to reasonable values |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/projectdiscovery/katana
- Documentation: https://docs.projectdiscovery.io/tools/katana/overview
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/katana/

### 5.13 gau

#### 1. Tool Introduction

**gau** (Get All URLs) is a Go-based tool created by **lc (lc/0)** that fetches known URLs for a given domain from multiple passive sources: the **Wayback Machine**, **Common Crawl**, **OTX (AlienVault)**, and **URLScan.io**. Rather than actively crawling a site (which generates traffic against the live target), gau queries these third-party historical/passive archives to surface URLs that were ever indexed for the domain — including old parameters, deprecated endpoints, backup files, and forgotten API routes that may still be live and vulnerable, even if no longer linked from the current site.

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux
sudo apt update
sudo apt install gau -y
gau --version

# Or via Go
go install github.com/lc/gau/v2/cmd/gau@latest
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
echo <domain> | gau [options]
gau [options] <domain>
cat domains.txt | gau [options]
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--blacklist <ext>` | Exclude specific file extensions (e.g., `png,jpg,gif`) |
| `--fc, --filter-code <codes>` | Filter results by status code (requires `--providers-timeout`/verification) |
| `--from <date>` | Fetch URLs from a specific date (format: YYYYMM) |
| `--to <date>` | Fetch URLs up to a specific date |
| `--json` | Output results as JSON |
| `--mc, --match-code <codes>` | Match only specific status codes |
| `--mt, --match-type <types>` | Match specific content types |
| `--o, --output <file>` | Write output to a file |
| `--providers <list>` | Specify providers to query: wayback, commoncrawl, otx, urlscan |
| `--proxy <url>` | Route requests through a proxy |
| `--retries <n>` | Number of retries per request |
| `--subs` | Include subdomains in the query |
| `--threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads |
| `--timeout <secs>` | Timeout per request |
| `--verbose` | Verbose logging |
| `--config <file>` | Path to a custom config file |
| `--ip-blacklist <cidr>` | Exclude IP ranges from output |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | gau
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/Mod_Rewrite_Shop/
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=1
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Include subdomains in the search**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | gau --subs
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/
https://blog.example.com/2023/post-title
https://api.example.com/v1/legacy-endpoint
```

**Example 2 — Filter out image/static file noise**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | gau --blacklist png,jpg,gif,css,svg,woff
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/index.html
https://example.com/api/v1/users?id=5
https://example.com/download.php?file=report.pdf
```

**Example 3 — Query only Wayback Machine and Common Crawl**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | gau --providers wayback,commoncrawl
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/archive/2019/promo
https://example.com/old-api/v0/status
```

**Example 4 — Date-restricted historical search**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | gau --from 202001 --to 202112
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/covid-updates
https://example.com/legacy-portal/login
```

**Example 5 — Output as JSON with metadata**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | gau --json | jq '.url' | head -3
```
Output:
```
"http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php"
"http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query"
"http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1"
```

**Example 6 — Bulk scan a list of domains**

```bash
$ cat domains.txt | gau --threads 5 -o all-urls.txt
```
Output:
```
[+] Fetching URLs for 20 domains...
[+] 4,382 URLs written to all-urls.txt
```

**Example 7 — Match only specific content types (JS files)**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | gau --mt application/javascript
```
Output:
```
https://example.com/static/main.js
https://example.com/static/legacy-app.js
```

**Example 8 — Chain with httpx to filter for currently-live URLs**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | gau --blacklist png,jpg,gif | httpx -silent -mc 200
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
```

**Example 9 — Extract URLs with parameters for injection testing**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | gau | grep '?' | sort -u
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/listproducts.php?cat=2
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Discovering forgotten/deprecated endpoints and parameters not linked from the current live site.
- Finding historically indexed backup files, config files, or debug endpoints (e.g., `.bak`, `.sql`, `.env`).
- Building large parameter lists for automated injection testing (SQLi, XSS, SSRF).
- Passive reconnaissance that avoids generating direct traffic against the live target.
- Complementing active crawlers (katana/hakrawler) with historical data they cannot see.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# gau-recon.sh — passive URL discovery, cleanup, and live-host filtering

DOMAIN="$1"
OUTDIR="gau-recon-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Gathering historical URLs for $DOMAIN..."
echo "$DOMAIN" | gau --subs --blacklist png,jpg,jpeg,gif,css,svg,woff,ttf -o "$OUTDIR/raw-urls.txt"

echo "[*] Deduplicating..."
sort -u "$OUTDIR/raw-urls.txt" -o "$OUTDIR/urls-dedup.txt"

echo "[*] Filtering for live URLs..."
cat "$OUTDIR/urls-dedup.txt" | httpx -silent -mc 200 -o "$OUTDIR/live-urls.txt"

echo "[*] Extracting parameterized URLs for testing..."
grep '?' "$OUTDIR/live-urls.txt" > "$OUTDIR/param-urls.txt"

echo "[+] Done. $(wc -l < "$OUTDIR/param-urls.txt") parameterized live URLs saved."
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always pair gau with a live-host filter (httpx) since many archived URLs will be dead or redirected.
- Use `--blacklist` aggressively to strip static asset noise (images, fonts, CSS) that clutters results.
- Combine gau with waybackurls for slightly different data source coverage — results are not always identical.
- Use `--subs` on large organizations to surface forgotten subdomains along with their historical URLs.
- Extract parameterized URLs (`grep '?'`) as a fast-start seed list for automated fuzzing tools like ffuf or sqlmap.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Very few/no results | Domain has little historical index presence | Try `--providers` individually to isolate which source responds |
| Rate-limited by a provider | Too many requests too fast | Reduce `--threads`, add `--retries` |
| Output dominated by static files | No blacklist applied | Add `--blacklist png,jpg,gif,css,svg,woff` |
| Command not found | Go bin path not in PATH | Add `$(go env GOPATH)/bin` to `PATH` |
| Stale/duplicate entries | Multiple providers overlap | Pipe through `sort -u` after collection |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/lc/gau
- Wayback Machine API: https://archive.org/help/wayback_api.php
- Common Crawl: https://commoncrawl.org/
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/gau/

### 5.14 waybackurls

#### 1. Tool Introduction

waybackurls is a small, focused Go utility created by **Tom Hudson (tomnomnom)** that fetches all known URLs for a given domain (including subdomains) directly from the **Internet Archive's Wayback Machine CDX API**. It is one of the earliest and most widely used tools in the modern bug bounty recon toolkit, valued for its simplicity — it does exactly one thing well: pull historical URL records for a domain from web.archive.org. It is frequently chained with other tools (gau, httpx, gf, qsreplace) to build parameter-testing and endpoint-discovery pipelines.

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux
sudo apt update
sudo apt install waybackurls -y
waybackurls -h

# Or via Go
go install github.com/tomnomnom/waybackurls@latest
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
echo <domain> | waybackurls [options]
cat domains.txt | waybackurls [options]
waybackurls <domain>
```

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-dates` | Include the timestamp/date each URL was archived |
| `-no-subs` | Exclude subdomains; restrict to the exact domain given |
| `-get-versions` | Fetch all archived versions/snapshots of a specific URL (used with a single URL input) |

> waybackurls is intentionally minimalist. Unlike gau, it has very few flags — its power comes from Unix-pipeline composition with tools like `sort`, `uniq`, `grep`, `httpx`, and `gf`.

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/Mod_Rewrite_Shop/BuyProduct-1/
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=2
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Include archive timestamps**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls -dates
```
Output:
```
2019-03-14T02:11:07 http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
2020-07-22T09:45:12 http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
```

**Example 2 — Restrict to exact domain, excluding subdomains**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | waybackurls -no-subs
```
Output:
```
http://example.com/
http://example.com/about
http://example.com/contact
```

**Example 3 — Bulk domain list processing**

```bash
$ cat domains.txt | waybackurls > all-wayback-urls.txt
$ wc -l all-wayback-urls.txt
```
Output:
```
8452 all-wayback-urls.txt
```

**Example 4 — Deduplicate and sort results**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls | sort -u
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
```

**Example 5 — Extract only parameterized URLs**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls | grep '=' | sort -u
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=1
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/listproducts.php?cat=2
```

**Example 6 — Filter for specific file extensions (potential sensitive files)**

```bash
$ echo "example.com" | waybackurls | grep -E '\.(bak|sql|env|log|config)$'
```
Output:
```
http://example.com/backup/db_backup.sql
http://example.com/.env
http://example.com/debug.log
```

**Example 7 — Chain with httpx to find live URLs**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls | httpx -silent -mc 200
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/login.php
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
```

**Example 8 — Combine with gf patterns for XSS-prone URLs**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls | gf xss
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/search.php?test=query
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/guestbook.php?name=test
```

**Example 9 — Combine with qsreplace for automated payload injection**

```bash
$ echo "testphp.vulnweb.com" | waybackurls | grep '=' | qsreplace "<script>alert(1)</script>"
```
Output:
```
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=<script>alert(1)</script>
http://testphp.vulnweb.com/comment.php?aid=<script>alert(1)</script>
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Passive historical URL discovery without generating direct traffic to the live target.
- Surfacing old, forgotten, or deprecated endpoints and parameters for testing.
- Building large seed lists for XSS/SQLi/SSRF fuzzing pipelines via `gf` and `qsreplace`.
- Identifying accidentally archived sensitive files (backups, config files, logs).
- Complementing gau, katana, and hakrawler for maximum URL coverage in recon.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# waybackurls-recon.sh — passive recon + sensitive file discovery pipeline

DOMAIN="$1"
OUTDIR="wayback-recon-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Fetching Wayback URLs for $DOMAIN..."
echo "$DOMAIN" | waybackurls | sort -u > "$OUTDIR/all-urls.txt"

echo "[*] Searching for potentially sensitive archived files..."
grep -E '\.(bak|sql|env|log|config|zip|tar\.gz)$' "$OUTDIR/all-urls.txt" > "$OUTDIR/sensitive-files.txt"

echo "[*] Extracting parameterized URLs for fuzzing..."
grep '=' "$OUTDIR/all-urls.txt" > "$OUTDIR/param-urls.txt"

echo "[*] Checking which are still live..."
cat "$OUTDIR/all-urls.txt" | httpx -silent -mc 200 -o "$OUTDIR/live-urls.txt"

echo "[+] Recon complete."
echo "    Total URLs: $(wc -l < "$OUTDIR/all-urls.txt")"
echo "    Sensitive candidates: $(wc -l < "$OUTDIR/sensitive-files.txt")"
echo "    Live URLs: $(wc -l < "$OUTDIR/live-urls.txt")"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always deduplicate (`sort -u`) — the Wayback CDX API frequently returns the same URL from multiple snapshot timestamps.
- Grep for sensitive extensions (`.bak`, `.sql`, `.env`, `.log`) early — these are common high-value findings.
- Combine with `gau` for broader source coverage (Common Crawl, OTX, URLScan) since waybackurls only queries the Wayback Machine.
- Use `-no-subs` when you specifically want to scope results to a single host rather than the whole organization.
- Chain into `httpx` immediately to discard dead archived URLs and focus only on what's currently reachable.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No results returned | Domain never archived by Wayback Machine | Try `gau` instead for broader source coverage |
| Command not found | Go bin path not in PATH | Add `$(go env GOPATH)/bin` to `PATH` |
| Extremely large output on big domains | No filtering applied | Pipe through `grep`/`sort -u` to narrow results |
| API rate limiting / slow responses | Wayback CDX API throttling on large queries | Add delays between bulk domain requests |
| Duplicate near-identical URLs | Multiple archive snapshots of same page | Deduplicate with `sort -u` or `anew` |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/tomnomnom/waybackurls
- Wayback Machine CDX API docs: https://archive.org/help/wayback_api.php
- Kali tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/waybackurls/
- tomnomnom's tool suite: https://github.com/tomnomnom

## Chapter 6 – Directory & Content Discovery


Directory and content discovery is one of the foundational activities in web application reconnaissance and penetration testing. Web servers frequently host files, directories, backup copies, configuration files, administrative panels, and API endpoints that are not linked anywhere in the visible site navigation. These "hidden" resources are often forgotten by developers, left over from testing, or intentionally excluded from public menus — yet they remain accessible to anyone who requests the correct URL. Content discovery tools automate the process of guessing these paths by sending a very large number of HTTP requests, each built by combining a base URL with entries from a wordlist, and then analyzing the server's responses (status codes, response length, response time, and redirects) to determine which guesses correspond to real resources.

This chapter covers five of the most widely used content discovery utilities available in Kali Linux and other penetration testing distributions: **Gobuster**, **FFUF**, **Feroxbuster**, **Dirsearch**, and **WFuzz**. While all five tools share the same underlying goal — brute-forcing URLs against a wordlist — they differ in implementation language, performance characteristics, output formats, recursion behavior, and the breadth of protocols or discovery modes they support (directories, DNS subdomains, virtual hosts, S3 buckets, GET/POST parameters, and more). Understanding the strengths, syntax, and quirks of each tool allows a security professional to choose the right instrument for a given engagement, combine tools effectively, and interpret results with confidence.

The tools discussed in this chapter are intended strictly for authorized security testing, bug bounty programs, and educational lab environments (such as HackTheBox, TryHackMe, or your own local test servers). Running these tools against systems you do not own or do not have explicit written permission to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.

### 6.1 Gobuster

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Gobuster is a directory, file, DNS subdomain, virtual host, S3 bucket, and TFTP brute-forcing tool written in Go by OJ Reeves. Because it is written in Go, Gobuster compiles to a single static binary and takes advantage of Go's lightweight goroutine concurrency model, which makes it extremely fast compared to interpreted-language tools when sending large numbers of HTTP or DNS requests. Gobuster does not spider a site or parse HTML for links — it purely performs wordlist-based brute forcing, sending one request per candidate word (or per DNS query) and reporting back which candidates produced a "hit" based on status code or DNS resolution.

Gobuster is organized around **modes**, each invoked as a subcommand:

- `dir` – classic directory and file brute-forcing over HTTP/HTTPS.
- `dns` – subdomain brute-forcing via DNS resolution.
- `fuzz` – generic fuzzing using a `FUZZ` keyword, similar in spirit to FFUF.
- `vhost` – virtual host brute-forcing via the `Host` header.
- `s3` – Amazon S3 bucket enumeration.
- `gcs` – Google Cloud Storage bucket enumeration.
- `tftp` – TFTP file enumeration.

This modular design means Gobuster is really several specialized brute-forcing tools bundled behind one binary, which keeps the option set for each mode focused and easy to remember.

#### 2. How to Install

Gobuster ships as a package in Kali Linux's default repositories, and is also distributable as a single Go binary or via `go install`.

**Kali Linux (APT — recommended):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install gobuster -y
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
gobuster version
```

**Using Go (to get the latest version directly from source):**
```bash
go install github.com/OJ/gobuster/v3@latest
```
This places the compiled binary at `$HOME/go/bin/gobuster`; ensure that directory is in your `$PATH`.

**From source (GitHub):**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/OJ/gobuster.git
cd gobuster
go build
sudo mv gobuster /usr/local/bin/
```

#### 3. Syntax

General syntax pattern:
```bash
gobuster <mode> [options]
```

Examples of the pattern in practice:
```bash
gobuster dir -u <URL> -w <WORDLIST> [flags]
gobuster dns -d <DOMAIN> -w <WORDLIST> [flags]
gobuster vhost -u <URL> -w <WORDLIST> [flags]
gobuster fuzz -u <URL_WITH_FUZZ_KEYWORD> -w <WORDLIST> [flags]
gobuster s3 -w <WORDLIST> [flags]
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Global flags (available across all modes):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-h, --help` | Display help information |
| `-z, --no-progress` | Do not display progress updates |
| `-o, --output string` | Path to output file |
| `-q, --quiet` | Suppress the Gobuster banner |
| `-t, --threads int` | Number of concurrent threads (default 10) |
| `-v, --verbose` | Enable verbose output |
| `--delay duration` | Delay between requests (e.g., `100ms`) |
| `--no-color` | Disable colorized output |
| `--no-error` | Do not display errors |
| `-p, --proxy string` | Proxy URL, e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:8080` |
| `-r, --wordlist-offset int` | Resume wordlist from a given line number |
| `-w, --wordlist string` | Path to wordlist (or `-` for stdin) |
| `--random-agent` | Use a random User-Agent per request |
| `--timeout duration` | HTTP request timeout (default 10s) |

**`dir` mode specific flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url string` | Target URL |
| `-c, --cookies string` | Cookies to include in requests |
| `-x, --extensions string` | File extensions to search for, comma separated (e.g. `php,html,txt`) |
| `--extensions-file string` | File containing extensions, one per line |
| `-e, --expanded` | Expand results to show full URLs |
| `-f, --add-slash` | Append `/` to each request |
| `-H, --headers stringArray` | Custom headers, e.g. `-H "Auth: Bearer xyz"` |
| `-k, --no-tls-validation` | Skip SSL certificate verification |
| `-l, --include-length` | Include response length in output |
| `-m, --method string` | HTTP method to use (default `GET`) |
| `-n, --no-status` | Do not print status codes |
| `-P, --password string` | Password for Basic Auth |
| `-U, --username string` | Username for Basic Auth |
| `-s, --status-codes string` | Positive status codes (default is a built-in list) |
| `-b, --status-codes-blacklist string` | Negative status codes (overrides `-s`) |
| `-d, --discover-backup` | Discover backup files (`~`, `.bak`, `.old` etc. appended) |
| `--exclude-length string` | Exclude responses with given content-length(s) |
| `-r, --follow-redirect` | Follow redirects |
| `--retry` | Retry a request on timeout |
| `--retry-attempts int` | Number of retry attempts |
| `-a, --useragent string` | Set custom User-Agent |
| `--tls-cert string` / `--tls-key string` | Client certificate for TLS auth |

**`dns` mode specific flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-d, --domain string` | Target domain |
| `-r, --resolver string` | Custom DNS resolver (`server:port`) |
| `-c, --show-cname` | Show CNAME records (cannot be used with `-i`) |
| `-i, --show-ips` | Show IP addresses for resolved subdomains |
| `--wildcard` | Force continued operation when wildcard DNS is detected |

**`vhost` mode specific flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url string` | Target base URL |
| `--append-domain` | Append the base domain to each wordlist entry automatically |
| `-c, --cookies` | Cookies to include |
| `-H, --headers` | Custom headers |
| `-k, --no-tls-validation` | Skip TLS validation |
| `--exclude-length` | Exclude by content length to filter false positives |
| `-m, --method` | HTTP method |
| `-a, --useragent` | Custom User-Agent |

**`fuzz` mode specific flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url string` | URL containing the literal string `FUZZ` |
| `-b, --exclude-length` | Exclude responses by content length |
| `-s, --status-codes` | Positive status codes |
| `-m, --method` | HTTP method |
| `-H, --headers` | Custom headers (can also contain `FUZZ`) |

**`s3` / `gcs` mode specific flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-w, --wordlist` | Wordlist of bucket name candidates |
| `--maxfiles int` | Max number of files to list per open bucket |

#### 5. Basic Usage

Basic directory brute-force against a target:
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
```

Expected terminal output:

```
===============================================================
Gobuster v3.6
by OJ Reeves (@TheColonial) & Christian Mehlmauer (@_FireFart_)
===============================================================
[+] Url:                     http://target.local
[+] Method:                  GET
[+] Threads:                 10
[+] Wordlist:                /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
[+] Negative Status codes:   404
[+] User Agent:              gobuster/3.6
[+] Timeout:                 10s
===============================================================
Starting gobuster in directory enumeration mode
===============================================================
/about                (Status: 200) [Size: 1256]
/admin                (Status: 301) [Size: 313] [--> http://target.local/admin/]
/index.html           (Status: 200) [Size: 4521]
/login                (Status: 200) [Size: 2044]
/robots.txt           (Status: 200) [Size: 45]
===============================================================
Finished
===============================================================
```

#### 6. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Directory brute-force with common extensions:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -x php,html,txt
```
```
/index.php            (Status: 200) [Size: 4521]
/config.php           (Status: 403) [Size: 278]
/notes.txt            (Status: 200) [Size: 128]
```

**Example 2 — Increase threads for speed:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/big.txt -t 50
```
```
/uploads              (Status: 301) [Size: 315] [--> http://target.local/uploads/]
/backup               (Status: 200) [Size: 0]
```

**Example 3 — Save output to a file:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt -o gobuster_results.txt
```
```
[+] Output written to gobuster_results.txt
```

**Example 4 — DNS subdomain enumeration:**
```bash
gobuster dns -d target.local -w /usr/share/wordlists/SecLists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -i
```
```
Found: www.target.local [162.241.10.22]
Found: mail.target.local [162.241.10.55]
Found: dev.target.local [162.241.10.90]
```

**Example 5 — Virtual host discovery:**
```bash
gobuster vhost -u http://target.local -w vhosts.txt --append-domain
```
```
Found: intranet.target.local (Status: 200) [Size: 3120]
Found: staging.target.local (Status: 302) [Size: 0]
```

**Example 6 — Excluding false-positive response lengths:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt --exclude-length 4521
```
```
/admin                (Status: 301) [Size: 313] [--> http://target.local/admin/]
```

**Example 7 — Authenticated scan with Basic Auth:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt -U admin -P Passw0rd!
```
```
/dashboard             (Status: 200) [Size: 5210]
```

**Example 8 — Custom headers for a JWT-protected app:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOi..."
```
```
/api/users             (Status: 200) [Size: 892]
```

**Example 9 — Discovering backup files:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt -d
```
```
/config.php.bak        (Status: 200) [Size: 512]
/index.html~           (Status: 200) [Size: 4521]
```

**Example 10 — Through a Burp Suite proxy for manual review:**
```bash
gobuster dir -u http://target.local -w common.txt -p http://127.0.0.1:8080 -k
```
```
[proxying all requests through 127.0.0.1:8080]
```

**Example 11 — Fuzz mode against a query parameter:**
```bash
gobuster fuzz -u "http://target.local/search?q=FUZZ" -w params.txt -s 200
```
```
FUZZ=admin             (Status: 200) [Size: 100]
```

**Example 12 — S3 bucket enumeration:**
```bash
gobuster s3 -w bucket-names.txt
```
```
Bucket: target-backups            [BucketExists: true] [Region: us-east-1]
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Initial reconnaissance during a web application penetration test to enumerate hidden admin panels, backup files, and configuration files.
- Subdomain enumeration during the reconnaissance phase of an external network assessment.
- Virtual host discovery on servers hosting multiple sites behind a single IP address.
- Validating that sensitive files (`.git`, `.env`, `.bak`) are not exposed on production servers.
- Cloud storage bucket discovery during OSINT/attack-surface mapping engagements.
- Fuzzing API endpoints or parameter names when documentation is unavailable.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

A simple wrapper script that scans a list of targets sequentially and stores results per host:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# gobuster_batch.sh - run gobuster dir scans against a list of hosts

WORDLIST="/usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt"
EXTENSIONS="php,html,txt,bak"
OUTDIR="./gobuster_results"
THREADS=40

mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r target; do
    [ -z "$target" ] && continue
    safe_name=$(echo "$target" | sed 's/[^a-zA-Z0-9]/_/g')
    echo "[*] Scanning $target"
    gobuster dir -u "$target" -w "$WORDLIST" -x "$EXTENSIONS" \
        -t "$THREADS" -q -o "$OUTDIR/${safe_name}.txt"
done < targets.txt

echo "[*] All scans complete. Results in $OUTDIR"
```

Usage: populate `targets.txt` with one URL per line, then run `bash gobuster_batch.sh`.

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always start with a smaller wordlist (e.g., `common.txt`) before escalating to larger lists like `raft-large-words.txt` to save time.
- Use `-t` thoughtfully; too many threads can crash a fragile target or trigger WAF/IDS rate-limiting.
- Use `--exclude-length` or `-b` to filter out repetitive "soft 404" responses that return HTTP 200 for nonexistent paths.
- Combine `-x` with relevant extensions based on the technology stack identified (e.g., `aspx` for IIS/.NET, `jsp` for Java).
- Use `-o` to always keep a persistent log of results for later comparison and reporting.
- Respect scope and rate limits; add `--delay` when testing against production or rate-limited systems.
- Use `-k` only for lab/test environments with self-signed certificates, not against systems where certificate validation matters.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---|---|
| All requests return the same status/size ("wildcard" response) | Target uses a catch-all page for unknown paths; use `--exclude-length` or `-b` to filter it out |
| `dns` mode reports everything as found | Wildcard DNS is enabled on the domain; use `--wildcard` to acknowledge and let Gobuster attempt detection |
| Connection refused / timeouts | Check target is reachable, correct protocol (http vs https), and firewall/WAF isn't blocking your IP |
| TLS certificate errors | Add `-k` to skip validation for self-signed certs in lab environments |
| Extremely slow scan | Increase `-t` threads, reduce wordlist size, or check network latency |
| No results at all | Verify wordlist path is correct and not empty; test URL manually with `curl` first |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/OJ/gobuster
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/gobuster/
- SecLists wordlist collection: https://github.com/danielmiessler/SecLists

### 6.2 FFUF

#### 1. Tool Introduction

FFUF, short for **"Fuzz Faster U Fool,"** is an open-source web fuzzer written in Go by Joona Hoikkala (joohoi). Unlike Gobuster, which separates functionality into fixed modes, FFUF is built around a single generic concept: the user places the literal keyword `FUZZ` anywhere in the URL, headers, POST body, or even the Host header, and FFUF substitutes wordlist entries into that position on every request. This design makes FFUF exceptionally flexible — the same binary can perform directory brute-forcing, virtual host discovery, parameter fuzzing, value fuzzing, and even multi-position fuzzing (fuzzing two variables simultaneously with two separate wordlists).

FFUF is prized in the penetration testing and bug bounty community for its speed (thanks to Go's concurrency model), its powerful response filtering/matching engine (by status code, size, word count, line count, or regex), and its ability to output results in several structured formats (JSON, CSV, HTML, Markdown) suitable for reporting or chaining into other tools.

#### 2. How to Install

**Kali Linux (APT — recommended, pre-installed on many Kali images):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ffuf -y
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
ffuf -V
```

**Using Go:**
```bash
go install github.com/ffuf/ffuf/v2@latest
```

**From source:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/ffuf/ffuf.git
cd ffuf
go build
sudo mv ffuf /usr/local/bin/
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
ffuf -w <WORDLIST> -u <URL_CONTAINING_FUZZ_KEYWORD> [options]
```

Multiple wordlists (for multi-position fuzzing) use named keywords:
```bash
ffuf -w wordlist1.txt:W1 -w wordlist2.txt:W2 -u http://target.local/W1/W2
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Input options:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-w string` | Wordlist path, optionally with a keyword `-w list.txt:KEYWORD` |
| `-u string` | Target URL, must contain the fuzz keyword |
| `-X string` | HTTP method (default `GET`) |
| `-d string` | POST data body |
| `-H string` | Header, can be repeated; can contain `FUZZ` |
| `-b string` | Cookie data, e.g. `-b "session=FUZZ"` |
| `-r` | Follow redirects |
| `-recursion` | Enable recursive fuzzing into discovered directories |
| `-recursion-depth int` | Maximum recursion depth (0 = unlimited) |
| `-ignore-body` | Do not fetch response content (headers only, faster) |
| `-request string` | Load raw HTTP request from a file (Burp-style) |
| `-request-proto string` | Protocol to use with `-request` (default `https`) |

**Matcher options (what counts as a "hit"):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-mc string` | Match HTTP status codes (default `200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405`) |
| `-ml int` | Match number of lines in response |
| `-mr string` | Match a regular expression in response |
| `-ms int` | Match response size |
| `-mw int` | Match word count in response |
| `-mt duration` | Match response time |

**Filter options (exclude noise / soft-404s):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-fc string` | Filter by HTTP status code |
| `-fl int` | Filter by response line count |
| `-fr string` | Filter by regex |
| `-fs int` | Filter by response size |
| `-fw int` | Filter by word count |
| `-ft duration` | Filter by response time |

**General options:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-t int` | Number of concurrent threads (default 40) |
| `-p string` | Delay/rate limiter between requests, e.g. `0.1-0.5` (seconds) |
| `-rate int` | Requests per second limit |
| `-timeout int` | HTTP request timeout in seconds (default 10) |
| `-maxtime int` | Max total execution time in seconds |
| `-maxtime-job int` | Max time per fuzzing job (used with recursion) |
| `-o string` | Output file |
| `-of string` | Output format: `json, ejson, html, md, csv, ecsv` |
| `-v` | Verbose output |
| `-s` | Silent mode (only show results) |
| `-c` | Colorize output |
| `-se` | Auto-calibrate filters based on stopwords (helps detect soft-404s) |
| `-ac` | Automatically calibrate filtering options |
| `-x string` | Proxy URL |
| `-k` | Skip TLS certificate verification |
| `-replay-proxy string` | Replay matched requests through a proxy (e.g., Burp) |
| `-config string` | Load configuration from a file |

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
ffuf -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ
```

Expected terminal output:
```

        /'___\  /'___\           /'___\       
       /\ \__/ /\ \__/  __  __  /\ \__/        
       \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\        
        \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/        
         \ \_\   \ \_\  \ \____/  \ \_\         
          \/_/    \/_/   \/___/    \/_/         

       v2.1.0-dev
________________________________________________

 :: Method           : GET
 :: URL              : http://target.local/FUZZ
 :: Wordlist         : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
 :: Follow redirects : false
 :: Calibration      : false
 :: Timeout          : 10
 :: Threads          : 40
 :: Matcher          : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405
________________________________________________

admin                   [Status: 301, Size: 313, Words: 15, Lines: 10, Duration: 42ms]
index.html              [Status: 200, Size: 4521, Words: 812, Lines: 120, Duration: 39ms]
robots.txt              [Status: 200, Size: 45, Words: 4, Lines: 3, Duration: 40ms]
:: Progress: [4614/4614] :: Job [1/1] :: 320 req/sec :: Duration: [0:00:14] :: Errors: 0 ::
```

#### 6. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Basic directory fuzzing:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
uploads    [Status: 301, Size: 315, Words: 15, Lines: 10, Duration: 44ms]
```

**Example 2 — Filtering out soft-404 pages by size:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ -fs 4521
```
```
admin      [Status: 301, Size: 313, Words: 15, Lines: 10, Duration: 41ms]
```

**Example 3 — Extension fuzzing:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ.php -mc 200,403
```
```
config     [Status: 403, Size: 278, Words: 20, Lines: 8, Duration: 38ms]
```

**Example 4 — Virtual host discovery via Host header:**
```bash
ffuf -w vhosts.txt -u http://target.local/ -H "Host: FUZZ.target.local" -fs 0
```
```
staging    [Status: 200, Size: 3120, Words: 400, Lines: 90, Duration: 45ms]
```

**Example 5 — POST parameter value fuzzing (login brute-force safe example):**
```bash
ffuf -w users.txt -X POST -d "username=FUZZ&password=test" \
     -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
     -u http://target.local/login -mc 200 -fc 401
```
```
admin      [Status: 200, Size: 1902, Words: 250, Lines: 60, Duration: 55ms]
```

**Example 6 — Recursive directory fuzzing:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ -recursion -recursion-depth 2
```
```
[INFO] Adding a new job to the queue: http://target.local/uploads/FUZZ
images     [Status: 200, Size: 512, Words: 20, Lines: 5, Duration: 30ms]
```

**Example 7 — Multi-wordlist (clustered) fuzzing across two positions:**
```bash
ffuf -w users.txt:USER -w passwords.txt:PASS \
     -X POST -d "user=USER&pass=PASS" \
     -u http://target.local/login -mc 200
```
```
[Status: 200, Size: 1902] :: USER: admin :: PASS: Summer2024!
```

**Example 8 — Rate-limited scan against a sensitive production target:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ -rate 10
```
```
:: Progress: [4614/4614] :: Job [1/1] :: 10 req/sec :: Duration: [0:07:41] :: Errors: 0 ::
```

**Example 9 — Auto-calibration to skip false positives automatically:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ -ac
```
```
[INFO] Auto-calibrating for response size, word count...
admin      [Status: 301, Size: 313, Words: 15, Lines: 10, Duration: 41ms]
```

**Example 10 — Output results as JSON for downstream tooling:**
```bash
ffuf -w common.txt -u http://target.local/FUZZ -o results.json -of json
```
```
[INFO] Writing output to "results.json"
```

**Example 11 — Fuzzing an HTTP request loaded from Burp Suite:**
```bash
ffuf -request burp_request.txt -request-proto https -w common.txt
```
```
admin      [Status: 302, Size: 0, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 60ms]
```

**Example 12 — Fuzzing an API path with JSON body:**
```bash
ffuf -w endpoints.txt -u http://target.local/api/FUZZ \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" -mc 200,401
```
```
users      [Status: 401, Size: 89, Words: 10, Lines: 3, Duration: 41ms]
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- General-purpose directory and file discovery, often preferred over Gobuster for its filtering flexibility.
- Virtual host / subdomain discovery via `Host` header fuzzing.
- Parameter discovery — finding hidden GET/POST parameters accepted by an application.
- Credential and login form fuzzing in authorized password-policy testing.
- API endpoint enumeration for REST/GraphQL applications.
- Replaying a captured Burp Suite request with a wordlist substituted into any position (headers, cookies, body).

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ffuf_recon.sh - directory + vhost recon using ffuf

TARGET="$1"          # e.g. http://target.local
WORDLIST="/usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt"
VHOSTLIST="vhosts.txt"
OUT="ffuf_out_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)"

mkdir -p "$OUT"

echo "[*] Directory fuzzing $TARGET"
ffuf -w "$WORDLIST" -u "${TARGET}/FUZZ" -mc 200,301,302,403 \
     -o "$OUT/dirs.json" -of json -s

echo "[*] Vhost fuzzing on $TARGET"
ffuf -w "$VHOSTLIST" -u "$TARGET" -H "Host: FUZZ.$(echo $TARGET | sed 's~http://~~;s~https://~~')" \
     -fs 0 -o "$OUT/vhosts.json" -of json -s

echo "[*] Done. Results saved in $OUT/"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always run a baseline request first (`curl -I <url>/doesnotexist123`) to identify soft-404 response characteristics before scanning, then pre-set `-fs`/`-fw`/`-fl` accordingly.
- Prefer `-ac` (auto-calibration) on unfamiliar targets to reduce false positives automatically.
- Use `-recursion` carefully — it can massively multiply request volume; pair it with `-recursion-depth` to bound scope.
- Use `-of json` or `-of csv` when you plan to feed results into another script or reporting pipeline.
- Use `-p` or `-rate` to throttle requests against production systems or when a WAF is present.
- Named keyword wordlists (`-w list.txt:KEYWORD`) make multi-position fuzzing commands far more readable than relying on positional `FUZZ`, `FUZZ2`, etc.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---|---|
| Every word "matches" | Soft-404 page returning HTTP 200; add `-fs`/`-fw` filters or use `-ac` |
| `FUZZ keyword not found` error | Ensure the literal string `FUZZ` appears in `-u`, `-d`, `-H`, or `-b` |
| Too slow | Increase `-t` threads (default 40 is often conservative); check network latency |
| Getting blocked / banned mid-scan | Lower `-rate`, add `-p` delay, rotate `-x` proxy, or add realistic headers |
| TLS handshake errors | Add `-k` to skip certificate verification for lab targets |
| Recursion produces excessive noise | Lower `-recursion-depth`, add stricter matchers, or disable recursion and drill down manually |

#### 11. References
