WPScan is a free (for non-commercial use), black-box WordPress security scanner written in Ruby. It is developed and maintained by the WPScan team (now part of Automattic) and comes pre-installed on Kali Linux. WPScan is purpose-built to enumerate and audit WordPress installations, and it is considered the de-facto standard tool for WordPress penetration testing.

WPScan works by sending a series of HTTP requests to a target WordPress site and analyzing the responses (HTML source, HTTP headers, readme files, changelogs, JSON REST API endpoints, and more) to fingerprint:

- The WordPress core version
- Installed themes and their versions
- Installed plugins and their versions
- Registered usernames
- Configuration weaknesses (e.g., exposed `wp-config.php` backups, directory listing, debug logs)
- Weak/default credentials via password brute-forcing

Every detected component (core, theme, plugin) is automatically checked against the **WPScan Vulnerability Database**, a continuously updated repository of thousands of WordPress-related CVEs, giving testers immediate insight into exploitable weaknesses.

##### 4.1.1 Key Features
- Non-intrusive and aggressive enumeration modes
- Vulnerability data pulled live from the WPScan API (requires a free or paid API token)
- User enumeration via multiple techniques (author sitemap, REST API, login error messages)
- Password and username brute-forcing modules
- Plugin/theme vulnerability detection, including nulled/backdoored plugin detection
- Output in CLI-readable text, JSON, or CLI-No-Color formats for automation

---

#### 2. Installation

WPScan comes pre-installed on Kali Linux by default. Verify the installation first:

```bash
wpscan --version
```

If it is not installed, or you want to update to the latest release, use the following methods.

##### Method 1 – APT (Kali Linux default repository)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install wpscan -y
```

##### Method 2 – RubyGems (latest version, cross-distro)
```bash
sudo apt install ruby ruby-dev build-essential libcurl4-openssl-dev libxml2-dev libxslt1-dev zlib1g-dev -y
sudo gem install wpscan
```

##### Method 3 – From Source (GitHub)
```bash
git clone https://github.com/wpscanteam/wpscan.git
cd wpscan
sudo gem install bundler
bundle install --without test
```

##### Post-Installation: Updating the Vulnerability Database
WPScan requires a free API token from **https://wpscan.com/api** to pull live vulnerability data (up to 25 requests/day on the free tier). Once you have a token:

```bash
wpscan --api-token YOUR_API_TOKEN_HERE --update
```

You can also save the token permanently so you don't need to pass it every time:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.wpscan
echo "YOUR_API_TOKEN_HERE" > ~/.wpscan/scan.log
```
(Alternatively, export it as an environment variable: `export WPSCAN_API_TOKEN=YOUR_API_TOKEN_HERE`)

To manually refresh the local vulnerability database cache:
```bash
wpscan --update
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

The general syntax of WPScan is:

```bash
wpscan --url <target-url> [options]
```

Basic example:
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com
```

---

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

Below is the complete reference of WPScan CLI options as available in Kali Linux.

##### 4.1 Target Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--url \| -u <target url>` | The URL of the target WordPress site |
| `--force \| -f` | Do not check if the target is running WordPress before scanning |
| `--user-agent \| --ua <value>` | Set a custom User-Agent string |
| `--random-user-agent \| --rua` | Use a random User-Agent for every request |
| `--http-auth <login:password>` | HTTP Basic Authentication credentials |
| `--cookie-string <string>` | Set a custom cookie string |
| `--proxy <protocol://IP:port>` | Use an HTTP(S) proxy |
| `--proxy-auth <login:password>` | Proxy authentication credentials |
| `--disable-tls-checks` | Disable TLS/SSL certificate verification |

##### 4.2 Enumeration Options (`-e / --enumerate`)
| Value | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `vp` | Vulnerable plugins only |
| `ap` | All plugins |
| `p` | Popular plugins |
| `vt` | Vulnerable themes only |
| `at` | All themes |
| `t` | Popular themes |
| `tt` | Timthumbs |
| `cb` | Config backups |
| `dbe` | Db exports |
| `u` | User IDs range (e.g. `u1-10`) |
| `m` | Media IDs range (uploaded files) |
| `ap,at,cb,dbe,u` | Combine multiple modules (comma-separated, no spaces) |

Example:
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com -e vp,vt,u
```

##### 4.3 Password Attack Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--passwords \| -P <file>` | Wordlist file path for password brute-forcing |
| `--usernames \| -U <file/string>` | Username(s) or file of usernames |
| `--multicall-max-passwords <n>` | Max passwords per XML-RPC multicall request |
| `--login-uri <uri>` | Custom login page URI (e.g. for renamed wp-login.php) |
| `--password-attack <mode>` | Attack mode: `xmlrpc`, `wp-login`, `xmlrpc-multicall` |

##### 4.4 Detection & Scan Behavior
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--detection-mode <mode>` | `mixed` (default), `passive`, or `aggressive` |
| `--plugins-detection <mode>` | `mixed`, `passive`, `aggressive` |
| `--plugins-version-detection <mode>` | `mixed`, `passive`, `aggressive` |
| `--exclude-content-based <regex/string>` | Exclude results matching a pattern |
| `--stealthy` | Alias that forces passive detection, no aggressive checks |

##### 4.5 Throttling & Connection
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--max-threads \| -t <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default: 5) |
| `--throttle <milliseconds>` | Delay between requests |
| `--request-timeout <seconds>` | HTTP request timeout (default: 60) |
| `--connect-timeout <seconds>` | Connection timeout |
| `--max-scan-duration <seconds>` | Stop the scan after N seconds |

##### 4.6 Output Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--output \| -o <file>` | Save output to a file |
| `--format \| -f <format>` | `cli`, `cli-no-colour`, `cli-no-color`, `json` |
| `--verbose \| -v` | Verbose output |
| `--no-banner` | Suppress the ASCII banner |
| `--log <file>` | Log HTTP requests/responses |

##### 4.7 API / Database
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--api-token <token>` | WPScan.com API token |
| `--update` | Update the vulnerability database |
| `--no-update` | Skip the auto-update check for this run |

##### 4.8 Misc
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--help \| -h` | Show help menu |
| `--version` | Show installed version |
| `--clear-cache` | Clear the local scan cache |
| `--cache-ttl <seconds>` | Cache time-to-live |

---

#### 5. Basic Usage

The simplest possible scan:

```bash
wpscan --url http://testphp-wordpress.local
```

Expected output (truncated for readability):

```
_______________________________________________________________
         __          _______   _____
         \ \        / /  __ \ / ____|
          \ \  /\  / /| |__) | (___   ___  __ _ _ __ ®
           \ \/  \/ / |  ___/ \___ \ / __|/ _` | '_ \
            \  /\  /  | |     ____) | (__| (_| | | | |
             \/  \/   |_|    |_____/ \___|\__,_|_| |_|

         WordPress Security Scanner by the WPScan Team
                         Version 3.8.25
       Sponsored by Automattic - https://automattic.com/
       @_WPScan_, @ethicalhack3r, @erwan_lr, @firefart
_______________________________________________________________

[+] URL: http://testphp-wordpress.local/ [192.168.56.101]
[+] Started: Sun Jul 19 10:12:03 2026

Interesting Finding(s):

[+] Headers
 | Interesting Entry: Server: Apache/2.4.52 (Ubuntu)
 | Found By: Headers (Passive Detection)
 | Confidence: 100%

[+] XML-RPC seems to be enabled: http://testphp-wordpress.local/xmlrpc.php
 | Found By: Direct Access (Aggressive Detection)
 | Confidence: 100%

[+] WordPress readme found: http://testphp-wordpress.local/readme.html
 | Found By: Direct Access (Aggressive Detection)
 | Confidence: 100%

[+] WordPress version 6.5.3 identified (Insecure, released on 2024-04-09)
 | Found By: Rss Generator (Passive Detection)

[+] WordPress theme in use: twentytwentyfour
 | Location: http://testphp-wordpress.local/wp-content/themes/twentytwentyfour/
 | Latest Version: 1.1
 | Last Updated: 2024-04-02T00:00:00.000Z

[+] Enumerating All Plugins (via Passive and Aggressive Methods)
[+] Checking Plugin Versions (via Passive and Aggressive Methods)

[i] Plugin(s) Identified:

[+] contact-form-7
 | Location: http://testphp-wordpress.local/wp-content/plugins/contact-form-7/
 | Latest Version: 5.9.3
 | Last Updated: 2024-04-10T13:00:00.000Z
 | Found By: Urls In Homepage (Passive Detection)

[+] WPScan DB API OK
 | Plan: free
 | Requests Done (this run): 6
 | Requests Remaining: 19

[+] Finished: Sun Jul 19 10:13:41 2026
[+] Requests Done: 47
[+] Cached Requests: 5
[+] Data Sent: 12.611 KB
[+] Data Received: 391.223 KB
[+] Memory used: 214.652 MB
[+] Elapsed time: 00:01:38
```

---

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

##### Example 1 – Full vulnerability-focused scan with an API token
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --api-token abc123XYZ456 -e vp,vt
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] contact-form-7
 | [!] Title: Contact Form 7 < 5.8.4 - Unrestricted File Upload
 | Fixed in: 5.8.4
 | References:
 |  - https://wpscan.com/vulnerability/abcd1234
 |  - CVE-2024-XXXXX
```

##### Example 2 – Enumerate users only
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com -e u1-20
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[i] User(s) Identified:

[+] admin
 | Found By: Author Id Brute Forcing - Author Pattern (Aggressive Detection)
 | Confirmed By: Login Error Messages (Aggressive Detection)

[+] j.smith
 | Found By: Rss Generator (Passive Detection)
```

##### Example 3 – Brute-force login using a known username list and rockyou.txt
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com \
  --usernames admin,j.smith \
  --passwords /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \
  --password-attack wp-login
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[i] Valid Combinations Found:
 | Username: admin, Password: Summer2024!
```

##### Example 4 – Scan behind HTTP Basic Auth
```bash
wpscan --url http://staging.target-site.com --http-auth admin:staging123
```

##### Example 5 – Full plugin & theme enumeration with JSON output for automation
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com -e ap,at,cb,dbe --format json --output scan_results.json
```
```bash
cat scan_results.json | jq '.plugins | keys'
```
Output:
```
[
  "akismet",
  "contact-form-7",
  "elementor",
  "woocommerce"
]
```

##### Example 6 – Using a random User-Agent and proxy through Burp Suite
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --random-user-agent --proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
```

##### Example 7 – Aggressive detection mode for maximum coverage
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --detection-mode aggressive -e vp,vt,tt,cb,dbe,u
```

##### Example 8 – Stealthy/passive scan to avoid triggering WAF/IDS
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --stealthy
```

##### Example 9 – Scanning a target with a custom (renamed) login page
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --login-uri /secure-admin-login \
  -U admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
```

##### Example 10 – Limiting scan duration in a time-boxed engagement
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com --max-scan-duration 300 -e vp,vt,u
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[!] Max scan duration reached, exiting.
[+] Elapsed time: 00:05:00
```

##### Example 11 – Scan multiple targets from a list via bash loop
```bash
for site in $(cat targets.txt); do
  wpscan --url "$site" -e vp,vt --output "${site//[:\/]/_}.txt"
done
```

##### Example 12 – Detecting config backups and database exports (common misconfig)
```bash
wpscan --url http://target-site.com -e cb,dbe
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Config Backup(s) Found:
[+] wp-config.php.bak
 | http://target-site.com/wp-config.php.bak
 | Found By: Direct Access (Aggressive Detection)
```

---

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- **Pre-engagement recon:** Quickly identify WordPress version, theme, and plugin footprint before deeper testing.
- **Vulnerability validation:** Cross-check plugin/theme versions against known CVEs during a formal penetration test.
- **User enumeration for password attacks:** Build a target username list to feed into credential-stuffing or brute-force campaigns.
- **Configuration audit:** Detect exposed backup files, debug logs, or directory listing misconfigurations.
- **Bug bounty reconnaissance:** Fast triage of WordPress targets in scope to identify low-hanging fruit (outdated plugins).
- **Continuous security monitoring:** Scheduled scans (via cron) of an organization's own WordPress sites to catch newly disclosed vulnerabilities in installed plugins.

---

#### 8. Automation with Bash

##### 8.1 Automated Multi-Target Scan with Report Aggregation
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# wpscan_batch.sh - scan a list of WordPress sites and aggregate vulnerable findings

TARGETS="targets.txt"
API_TOKEN="YOUR_API_TOKEN_HERE"
OUTDIR="wpscan_reports_$(date +%F)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r url; do
    name=$(echo "$url" | sed -E 's#https?://##; s#[/:]#_#g')
    echo "[*] Scanning $url ..."
    wpscan --url "$url" \
        --api-token "$API_TOKEN" \
        -e vp,vt,u \
        --format json \
        --output "$OUTDIR/${name}.json" \
        --random-user-agent \
        --request-timeout 30
    echo "[+] Done: $OUTDIR/${name}.json"
done < "$TARGETS"

echo "[*] Extracting vulnerable plugins across all targets..."
for f in "$OUTDIR"/*.json; do
    echo "== $f =="
    jq -r '.plugins | to_entries[] | select(.value.vulnerabilities | length > 0) | .key' "$f"
done
```

##### 8.2 Cron Job for Weekly Compliance Scanning
```bash
# Add to crontab -e
0 3 * * 1 /usr/local/bin/wpscan_batch.sh >> /var/log/wpscan_weekly.log 2>&1
```

##### 8.3 Slack/Webhook Alert on New Vulnerability Found
```bash
#!/bin/bash
WEBHOOK="https://hooks.slack.com/services/XXX/YYY/ZZZ"
RESULT=$(wpscan --url http://target-site.com --api-token "$API_TOKEN" -e vp,vt --format json)
COUNT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq '[.plugins[].vulnerabilities[]?] | length')

if [ "$COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
    curl -X POST -H 'Content-type: application/json' \
    --data "{\"text\":\"⚠️ WPScan found $COUNT vulnerabilities on target-site.com\"}" \
    "$WEBHOOK"
fi
```

---

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always obtain and use a free WPScan API token — passive scanning without it will still fingerprint components but will not return live CVE matches.
- Start with `--stealthy` or `--detection-mode passive` when testing production systems to avoid unnecessary load or WAF triggers.
- Use `-e vp,vt` (vulnerable plugins/themes only) instead of `ap,at` (all plugins/themes) for faster scans when you only care about known vulnerabilities.
- Combine WPScan results with manual verification — automated tools can produce false positives, especially around version detection via passive methods.
- Throttle requests (`--throttle`) on shared hosting targets to avoid rate-limiting or accidental denial-of-service.
- Rotate or randomize the User-Agent (`--random-user-agent`) when the target has bot-detection systems in place.
- Never brute-force login credentials without explicit written authorization — this is often outside the scope of many penetration test agreements.
- Keep the local vulnerability database updated (`wpscan --update`) before every engagement.
- Save all scans in JSON format for later parsing, diffing between engagements, and integration into reporting pipelines.

---

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Solution |
|-------|-------|----------|
| `Error: 403 Forbidden` on all requests | WAF/CDN (e.g., Cloudflare) blocking scanner | Use `--random-user-agent`, add delays with `--throttle`, or route through a residential proxy (only if authorized) |
| `The remote website is up, but does not seem to be running WordPress` | Detection heuristics failed (custom setup) | Force the scan with `--force` |
| `No WPScan API Token given` | Missing/expired API token | Register at wpscan.com/api and pass `--api-token` |
| SSL certificate errors | Self-signed cert on staging/test environments | Add `--disable-tls-checks` |
| Extremely slow scans | Too many threads on a slow target or rate-limited connection | Lower `--max-threads`, increase `--request-timeout` |
| `Connection refused` | Target down, firewall blocking, or wrong port/protocol | Verify with `curl -I <url>` first |
| Ruby gem dependency errors during manual install | Missing system libraries | Install `libcurl4-openssl-dev`, `libxml2-dev`, `libxslt1-dev`, `zlib1g-dev` before `gem install wpscan` |
| API requests exhausted (`Requests Remaining: 0`) | Free tier daily limit (25/day) reached | Wait for daily reset or upgrade to a paid WPScan.com plan |

---

#### 11. References

- Official WPScan Documentation: https://wpscan.com/docs/
- WPScan GitHub Repository: https://github.com/wpscanteam/wpscan
- WPScan Vulnerability Database: https://wpscan.com/wordpress-plugins
- WPScan API Registration: https://wpscan.com/api
- Kali Linux Tools Listing – WPScan: https://www.kali.org/tools/wpscan/
- OWASP Testing Guide – WordPress Section: https://owasp.org/www-project-web-security-testing-guide/

### 9.2 JoomScan

#### 1. Tool Introduction

JoomScan (Joomla Vulnerability Scanner) is an open-source OWASP project written in Perl, purpose-built to detect, fingerprint, and audit Joomla-based websites. It was one of the earliest dedicated CMS scanners and remains one of the most reliable tools for Joomla-specific reconnaissance on Kali Linux.

JoomScan works by crawling a target site, parsing manifest/version files (such as `language/en-GB/en-GB.xml` or `includes/framework.php`), inspecting HTTP response headers, and comparing discovered components against a vulnerability signature database maintained by the OWASP JoomScan project. It can identify:

- The Joomla core version (via multiple detection techniques for reliability)
- Firewall / Web Application Firewall (WAF) presence in front of the target
- Installed components, modules, and templates
- Publicly known vulnerabilities tied to the detected Joomla version
- Common sensitive files and directory listing issues
- Robots.txt disclosure of admin/backup paths

##### 4.1.1 Key Features
- Version detection using multiple fallback methods for higher accuracy
- Firewall/WAF detection before scanning begins
- Vulnerability reporting tied to specific Joomla core versions
- Directory listing checks
- Session-based scanning with random User-Agent support
- Report generation in HTML format for client-ready deliverables
- Update mechanism to keep the vulnerability database current

---

#### 2. Installation

JoomScan is pre-installed on Kali Linux. Verify with:

```bash
joomscan --version
```

If missing, install using one of the following methods.

##### Method 1 – APT (Kali default repository)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install joomscan -y
```

##### Method 2 – From Source (GitHub, latest version)
```bash
sudo apt install perl liblwp-useragent-determined-perl git -y
git clone https://github.com/OWASP/joomscan.git
cd joomscan
perl joomscan.pl --version
```

##### Post-Installation: Updating the Vulnerability Database
```bash
joomscan --update
```
Expected output:
```
[+] Updating database ...
[+] Database is updated to the latest version.
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

General syntax:
```bash
joomscan -u <target-url> [options]
```
or, if running from source:
```bash
perl joomscan.pl -u <target-url> [options]
```

Basic example:
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com
```

---

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

##### 4.1 Target & Scan Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-u, --url <url>` | Target URL to scan |
| `-x, --proxy <proxy>` | Use an HTTP proxy for the scan (format: `IP:PORT`) |
| `--user-agent <string>` | Set a custom User-Agent string |
| `--random-agent` | Use a randomly selected User-Agent for each request |
| `--cookie <cookie>` | Pass a custom cookie header |
| `--timeout <seconds>` | Set the connection timeout |

##### 4.2 Enumeration & Detection
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-ec, --enumerate-components` | Enumerate installed Joomla components |
| `--novuln` | Skip the vulnerability check, only enumerate |
| `-f, --forcessl` | Force scanning over HTTPS |

##### 4.3 File & List Input
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-l, --list <file>` | Scan a list of URLs from a file |

##### 4.4 Update & Reporting
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--update` | Update the JoomScan vulnerability database |
| `-ir, --include-report <name>` | Save results to an HTML report with the given name |

##### 4.5 Misc
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-h, --help` | Show help menu |
| `-v, --version` | Show installed version |

> **Note:** JoomScan's flag set is intentionally minimal compared to WPScan — its design philosophy favors a fast, opinionated default scan (core version detection, firewall detection, and vulnerability matching) that runs automatically without needing many toggles.

---

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
     ____                     ____
    |  _ \                   / ___|  ___ __ _ _ __
    | | | |___  ___  _ __ | (___ / __/ _` | '_ \
    | |_| / _ \/ _ \| '_ \ \___ \ (_| (_| | | | |
    |____/\___/\___/|_| |_|____/\___\__,_|_| |_|

     OWASP Joomla Vulnerability Scanner (JoomScan)
     Version 0.0.7                       Update: 2026-06-01
     ------------------------------------------------------

[+] Firewall Detector
[++] Firewall not detected

[+] Detecting Joomla Version
[++] Joomla 4.4.2 is detected

[+] Core Joomla Vulnerability
[++] Total 3 vulnerabilities found for Joomla core

[++] Title    : Joomla! 4.2.8 - Improper Access Check
[++] Reference: CVE-2023-23752

[+] Checking Directory Listing

[+] Checking Admin Login Page

[+] Checking Sensitive Config Files

[+] Checking robots.txt
[++] robots.txt is found: http://target-joomla-site.com/robots.txt
[++] Interesting entries found in robots.txt:
      Disallow: /administrator/
      Disallow: /bin/
      Disallow: /cache/

[+] Scan completed in 42 seconds
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

##### Example 1 – Basic scan against a target
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[++] Joomla 4.4.2 is detected
[++] Total 3 vulnerabilities found for Joomla core
```

```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com -ec
```

```
[+] Enumerating Components
[++] com_contact  - http://target-joomla-site.com/index.php?option=com_contact
[++] com_content  - http://target-joomla-site.com/index.php?option=com_content
[++] com_users    - http://target-joomla-site.com/index.php?option=com_users
```

```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com -x 127.0.0.1:8080
```

```bash
joomscan -u target-joomla-site.com -f
```


```
[++] Report generated successfully.
```

```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com --random-agent
```

##### Example 7 – Batch-scan a list of Joomla targets
```bash
cat > joomla_targets.txt << 'EOF'
http://site1.example.com
http://site2.example.com
http://site3.example.com
EOF

joomscan -l joomla_targets.txt
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[*] Scanning http://site1.example.com
[++] Joomla 3.10.11 is detected
[*] Scanning http://site2.example.com
[++] Joomla 4.4.0 is detected
```

##### Example 8 – Enumeration only, skip vulnerability lookups (faster recon)
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com -ec --novuln
```

##### Example 9 – Update the vulnerability database before scanning
```bash
joomscan --update && joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com
```

##### Example 10 – Scan with a custom cookie (authenticated session context)
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com --cookie "joomla_user_state=logged_in; session=abc123"
```

##### Example 11 – Increase timeout for a slow / rate-limited target
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com --timeout 60
```

##### Example 12 – Full combined engagement scan
```bash
joomscan -u http://target-joomla-site.com -ec -f --random-agent -ir full_joomla_audit
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[++] Firewall not detected
[++] Joomla 4.4.2 is detected
[++] Total 3 vulnerabilities found for Joomla core
[+] Enumerating Components ... (12 components found)
[+] Saving report to: full_joomla_audit.html
```

---

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- **Rapid Joomla fingerprinting:** Confirm CMS platform and exact version during initial recon of a target IP range.
- **CVE mapping:** Immediately identify whether the detected Joomla core version is affected by publicly known vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2023-23752).
- **Component enumeration:** Map the attack surface by listing all `com_*` components in use, which is useful for locating third-party component vulnerabilities not covered by the core scan.
- **WAF detection:** Determine if a firewall is present before attempting more aggressive testing techniques.
- **Client reporting:** Generate HTML reports directly usable in a penetration test deliverable.
- **Bulk auditing:** Scan an organization's entire portfolio of Joomla sites in one pass using the `-l` list option.

---

#### 8. Automation with Bash

##### 8.1 Batch Scan with Per-Target HTML Reports
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# joomscan_batch.sh - scan multiple Joomla targets and save individual reports

TARGETS="joomla_targets.txt"
OUTDIR="joomscan_reports_$(date +%F)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r url; do
    name=$(echo "$url" | sed -E 's#https?://##; s#[/:]#_#g')
    echo "[*] Scanning $url ..."
    joomscan -u "$url" -ec --random-agent -ir "$OUTDIR/${name}_report"
done < "$TARGETS"

echo "[*] All scans complete. Reports saved in $OUTDIR/"
```

##### 8.2 Cron Job for Monthly Joomla Compliance Audit
```bash
# crontab -e
0 2 1 * * /usr/local/bin/joomscan_batch.sh >> /var/log/joomscan_monthly.log 2>&1
```

##### 8.3 Grep for Critical CVEs Across All Reports
```bash
#!/bin/bash
grep -riE "CVE-[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{4,7}" joomscan_reports_*/*.html | sort -u
```

---

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always run `joomscan --update` before a new engagement to ensure the vulnerability signature database is current.
- Use `--random-agent` on targets with basic bot-blocking to reduce the chance of being fingerprinted as an automated scanner.
- Pair JoomScan's core-version detection with manual verification of third-party components, since JoomScan's vulnerability database is strongest for Joomla core, not every extension.
- Use the `-ir` report flag for every engagement — having a timestamped HTML report is invaluable for client deliverables and later diffing.
- Run component enumeration (`-ec`) even when the core Joomla version appears secure, since many real-world Joomla compromises originate from vulnerable third-party components, not the core.
- Combine JoomScan output with CMSeeK or manual OSINT for a second, independent confirmation of the Joomla version, since HTTP header spoofing or version hiding plugins can occasionally mislead single-tool scans.

---

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Solution |
|-------|-------|----------|
| `Target is not running Joomla` | Detection heuristics failed on a heavily customized install | Manually verify via `curl -s http://target/administrator/manifests/files/joomla.xml` |
| Scan hangs indefinitely | Target is slow or rate-limiting the scanner | Increase `--timeout`, or scan during off-peak hours |
| `Firewall detected` blocking accurate scans | WAF is intercepting/altering scanner requests | Note the WAF in your report; consider `--random-agent` and slower request pacing (manual delay via wrapper script) |
| Report file not generating | Write-permission issue in the current directory | Run from a writable directory or specify a full path with `-ir /home/user/reports/name` |
| `--update` fails with connection error | No internet access or blocked GitHub access | Verify connectivity: `curl -I https://github.com`; check proxy settings |
| Outdated vulnerability results | Local database not updated recently | Run `joomscan --update` |
| Perl module errors when running from source | Missing dependencies | `sudo apt install liblwp-useragent-determined-perl libwww-perl` |

---

#### 11. References

- OWASP JoomScan GitHub Repository: https://github.com/OWASP/joomscan
- OWASP JoomScan Project Page: https://owasp.org/www-project-joomla-vulnerability-scanner/
- Kali Linux Tools Listing – JoomScan: https://www.kali.org/tools/joomscan/
- Joomla Official Security Announcements: https://developer.joomla.org/security-centre.html
- Joomla CVE Database (NVD search): https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/search/results?query=joomla

### 9.3 Droopescan

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Droopescan is a Python-based, plugin-oriented CMS security scanner. It was originally created to scan Drupal websites (hence the name), but its architecture is built around independent "scanner plugins," which means it can also scan other CMS platforms including WordPress, Joomla, SilverStripe, and Moodle using the same core engine.

Droopescan's main value in a CMS Security workflow is as a **cross-verification tool**: because it uses an entirely independent detection engine and signature set compared to WPScan or JoomScan, running it alongside the CMS-specific tools helps confirm findings and can occasionally catch things the primary scanner misses, particularly on Drupal, which lacks a dedicated tool as mature as WPScan.

It identifies:

- CMS platform and version (Drupal, WordPress, Joomla, SilverStripe, Moodle)
- Installed themes and modules/plugins
- Interesting URLs (admin panels, changelog files, install files)
- Version fingerprinting via static file hashes (MD5-based signature matching)

##### 4.1.1 Key Features
- Plugin-based architecture — new CMS scanners can be added without modifying the core
- Multi-threaded scanning for speed
- Hash-based version fingerprinting (compares static file hashes against a known signature database)
- Simple, scriptable output suitable for automation
- Host list scanning for bulk assessments

---

#### 2. Installation

Droopescan is available in the Kali Linux repositories. Verify with:

```bash
droopescan --version
```

If it's not installed, use one of the following methods.

##### Method 1 – APT (Kali default repository)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install droopescan -y
```

##### Method 2 – pip (Python package manager)
```bash
sudo apt install python3-pip -y
sudo pip3 install droopescan --break-system-packages
```

##### Method 3 – From Source (GitHub)
```bash
git clone https://github.com/SamJoan/droopescan.git
cd droopescan
sudo pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
python3 droopescan.py --help
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

General syntax:
```bash
droopescan scan <cms-plugin-name> -u <target-url> [options]
```

Or use the generic auto-detect scanner:
```bash
droopescan scan -u <target-url>
```

Basic example targeting Drupal specifically:
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com
```

---

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

##### 4.1 Scan Sub-Commands (CMS Plugins)
| Sub-command | Description |
|-------------|-------------|
| `scan drupal` | Scan a Drupal installation |
| `scan wordpress` | Scan a WordPress installation |
| `scan joomla` | Scan a Joomla installation |
| `scan silverstripe` | Scan a SilverStripe installation |
| `scan moodle` | Scan a Moodle installation |
| `scan` (no CMS specified) | Auto-detect the CMS type, then scan |

##### 4.2 Target Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-u, --url <url>` | Target URL |
| `-U, --url-file <file>` | File containing a list of target URLs (bulk scan) |
| `--host-header <header>` | Set a custom Host header (for vhost-based targeting) |

##### 4.3 Scan Behavior
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-t, --threads <n>` | Number of concurrent threads (default: 4) |
| `-e, --enumerate <types>` | Types to enumerate: `p` (plugins), `t` (themes), `v` (version), `i` (interesting urls), `a` (all) |
| `--timeout <seconds>` | HTTP request timeout |
| `--User-Agent <string>` / `--ua <string>` | Custom User-Agent header |
| `--headers <headers>` | Custom HTTP headers |
| `--follow-redirects` | Follow HTTP redirects |
| `--verify-ssl` | Verify SSL certificate validity (disabled by default) |
| `--proxy <url>` | Route requests through an HTTP proxy |
| `--cookie <cookie>` | Custom cookie value |

##### 4.4 Output Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-o, --output <format>` | Output format: `standard`, `json`, `csv` |
| `--quiet` | Suppress banner and non-essential output |

##### 4.5 Misc
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-h, --help` | Show help menu |
| `--version` | Show installed version |
| `--list` | List all available scan plugins (supported CMS types) |

---

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
[+] Starting scan against http://target-drupal-site.com

[+] Plugins found:
    None found.

[+] No themes found.

[+] Possible version(s):
    9.5.10
    9.5.11

[+] Possible interesting urls found:
    Default admin - http://target-drupal-site.com/user/login
    Changelog file - http://target-drupal-site.com/CHANGELOG.txt
    Installation file still exists - http://target-drupal-site.com/core/install.php

[+] Scan finished (0:00:14.271543 elapsed)
```

---

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

##### Example 1 – Basic Drupal scan
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Possible version(s):
    9.5.10
    9.5.11
```

##### Example 2 – Full enumeration (plugins, themes, version, interesting URLs)
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com -e a
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Plugins found:
    ctools    http://target-drupal-site.com/modules/ctools/
    views     http://target-drupal-site.com/modules/views/

[+] Themes found:
    olivero   http://target-drupal-site.com/themes/olivero/
```

##### Example 3 – Auto-detect CMS type before scanning
```bash
droopescan scan -u http://unknown-cms-target.com
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Detected CMS: wordpress
[+] Redirecting scan to 'wordpress' plugin...
[+] Possible version(s):
    6.4.3
```

##### Example 4 – Scan a WordPress site with Droopescan (cross-verification against WPScan)
```bash
droopescan scan wordpress -u http://target-wp-site.com -e vp,t
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Plugins found:
    akismet      http://target-wp-site.com/wp-content/plugins/akismet/
```

##### Example 5 – Bulk scan multiple hosts from a file
```bash
cat > drupal_targets.txt << 'EOF'
http://drupalsite1.example.com
http://drupalsite2.example.com
EOF

droopescan scan drupal -U drupal_targets.txt -e a
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[*] Scanning http://drupalsite1.example.com
[+] Possible version(s): 9.4.8
[*] Scanning http://drupalsite2.example.com
[+] Possible version(s): 10.1.2
```

##### Example 6 – JSON output for automated pipelines
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com -e a -o json > drupal_scan.json
```
```bash
cat drupal_scan.json | jq '.version'
```
Output:
```
["9.5.10", "9.5.11"]
```

##### Example 7 – Increase threads for a faster scan
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com -t 20
```

##### Example 8 – Scan through a Burp Suite proxy
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com --proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080
```

##### Example 9 – Set a custom User-Agent to avoid signature-based blocking
```bash
droopescan scan drupal -u http://target-drupal-site.com --ua "Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; SecurityAudit/1.0)"
```

##### Example 10 – Moodle scan (LMS-based CMS commonly found in education sector engagements)
```bash
droopescan scan moodle -u http://target-lms.example.edu -e a
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Possible version(s):
    4.3.1

[+] Possible interesting urls found:
    Admin login - http://target-lms.example.edu/login/index.php
```

##### Example 11 – SilverStripe scan
```bash
droopescan scan silverstripe -u http://target-silverstripe-site.com -e a
```

##### Example 12 – List all supported CMS scan plugins available in the current installation
```bash
droopescan scan --list
```
Output:
```
Available scanners:
    drupal
    joomla
    moodle
    silverstripe
    wordpress
```

---

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- **Drupal-specific security testing:** Since no tool matches WPScan's maturity for Drupal, Droopescan is the primary go-to scanner for Drupal engagements.
- **Cross-verification:** Run against a WordPress or Joomla target alongside WPScan/JoomScan to confirm version and plugin findings using an independent detection engine.
- **Education sector engagements:** Moodle support makes it valuable for testing Learning Management System (LMS) deployments common in universities and schools.
- **Multi-CMS environments:** In large organizations running a mix of CMS platforms, Droopescan's auto-detect mode (`scan` with no CMS argument) provides a quick unified entry point.
- **Bulk portfolio audits:** The `-U` bulk URL file option makes it efficient for auditing many hosts in one run.

---

#### 8. Automation with Bash

##### 8.1 Auto-Detect and Route to the Correct CMS Scanner
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# droopescan_auto.sh - detect CMS and run a full enumeration automatically

TARGET="$1"
if [ -z "$TARGET" ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <target-url>"
    exit 1
fi

echo "[*] Auto-detecting CMS for $TARGET"
droopescan scan -u "$TARGET" -e a -o json > "detect_result.json"

CMS=$(jq -r '.cms' detect_result.json 2>/dev/null)
echo "[+] Detected CMS: $CMS"

echo "[*] Running full targeted scan..."
droopescan scan "$CMS" -u "$TARGET" -e a -o json > "${CMS}_full_scan.json"
echo "[+] Saved results to ${CMS}_full_scan.json"
```

##### 8.2 Bulk Portfolio Scan with Summary Report
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# droopescan_portfolio.sh

TARGETS="drupal_targets.txt"
OUTDIR="droopescan_results_$(date +%F)"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r url; do
    name=$(echo "$url" | sed -E 's#https?://##; s#[/:]#_#g')
    droopescan scan drupal -u "$url" -e a -o json > "$OUTDIR/${name}.json"
done < "$TARGETS"

echo "== Summary of Detected Versions =="
for f in "$OUTDIR"/*.json; do
    echo "$f: $(jq -r '.version[]?' "$f" | paste -sd, -)"
done
```

##### 8.3 Cron Job for Weekly Drupal/Moodle Compliance Scans
```bash
# crontab -e
0 4 * * 3 /usr/local/bin/droopescan_portfolio.sh >> /var/log/droopescan_weekly.log 2>&1
```

---

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use Droopescan as a **secondary/verification scanner** for WordPress and Joomla, not a primary replacement for WPScan/JoomScan, since its plugin/theme signature database for those platforms is less comprehensive.
- For Drupal targets, always run with `-e a` (all enumeration types) to get the fullest picture, since individual flags can miss important interesting-URL findings.
- Increase `-t` (threads) cautiously — too high a thread count on a small server can cause noticeable load or trigger rate-limiting/WAF blocks.
- Combine Droopescan's `--proxy` option with Burp Suite to manually inspect and replay interesting requests found during a scan.
- Use `-o json` output for every scan so results integrate cleanly into automated reporting or SIEM ingestion pipelines.
- Periodically run `droopescan scan --list` after updates to confirm which CMS plugins are available in your installed version.

---

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Solution |
|-------|-------|----------|
| `No plugins/themes found` on a site clearly using known plugins | Signature database out of date for newer plugin releases | Update droopescan via `pip3 install --upgrade droopescan --break-system-packages` or pull latest from GitHub |
| SSL errors on HTTPS targets | Self-signed or invalid certificate | Ensure `--verify-ssl` is NOT set (verification is off by default) or investigate the cert manually with `openssl s_client` |
| Scan returns wrong/ambiguous version list | Hash-based fingerprinting matched multiple close releases | Cross-check manually via `CHANGELOG.txt` or `/core/install.php` banner |
| `Connection timed out` | Target slow, firewalled, or blocking scanner traffic | Increase `--timeout`, reduce `-t` threads, verify connectivity with `curl` |
| Auto-detect scan misidentifies CMS | Custom/heavily modified CMS installation | Manually specify the CMS scanner instead of using the generic `scan` auto-detect |
| `pip3 install` fails with "externally-managed-environment" | Newer Debian/Kali Python policy | Add `--break-system-packages` flag or use a virtual environment |
| Bulk scan (`-U`) skips some hosts | Malformed URLs in the input file (missing scheme) | Ensure every line starts with `http://` or `https://` |

---

#### 11. References

- Droopescan GitHub Repository: https://github.com/SamJoan/droopescan
- Kali Linux Tools Listing – Droopescan: https://www.kali.org/tools/droopescan/
- Drupal Security Advisories: https://www.drupal.org/security
- Moodle Security Announcements: https://moodle.org/security/
- PyPI Package Page: https://pypi.org/project/droopescan/

### 9.4 CMSeeK

#### 1. Tool Introduction

CMSeeK is a modern, Python 3-based CMS detection and exploitation framework. Unlike WPScan or JoomScan, which are dedicated to a single platform, CMSeeK is designed as a **universal CMS fingerprinting tool**, capable of identifying more than 180 different Content Management Systems using a signature-detection engine (built on top of the CMSeeK detection database, which is compatible with the widely-used `whatcms`/`cms-detector` style signature format).

For the most common and high-value CMS platforms — WordPress, Joomla, Drupal, Magento, PrestaShop, OpenCart, and others — CMSeeK goes a step further and triggers dedicated **deep-scan modules** that extract:

- CMS name, version, and detection confidence
- Users/authors (where applicable)
- Installed plugins/themes/extensions and their versions
- Known vulnerabilities associated with the detected CMS/version
- Server technology stack (PHP version, web server, etc.)
- robots.txt and admin panel discovery
- Whether the target is running behind a CDN/WAF

CMSeeK is typically the **first tool run** in a CMS security workflow because of its speed and broad platform coverage — its output determines which CMS-specific deep-scan tool (WPScan, JoomScan, Droopescan) should be used next.

##### 4.1.1 Key Features
- Detects 180+ CMS platforms via signature matching
- Deep-scan modules for major platforms (WordPress, Joomla, Drupal, Magento, etc.)
- Result caching in structured JSON per target for easy automation/integration
- Bulk scanning support via list input
- Update mechanism to refresh CMS detection signatures
- Lightweight and fast compared to full CMS-specific scanners — ideal for the initial triage phase

---

#### 2. Installation

CMSeeK is not always pre-installed by default on every Kali image, so it is commonly installed manually. Verify first:

```bash
cmseek --version
```

If missing, install from the official GitHub repository (the standard installation method for this tool):

##### Method 1 – Git Clone (Official Method)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install git python3 python3-pip -y
git clone https://github.com/Tuhinshubhra/CMSeeK.git
cd CMSeeK
sudo pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
```

Run it from within the cloned directory:
```bash
python3 cmseek.py --version
```

##### Method 2 – Kali Package (if available in your repository release)
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install cmseek -y
```

##### Method 3 – Create a Global Symlink for Convenience
```bash
cd CMSeeK
chmod +x cmseek.py
sudo ln -s "$(pwd)/cmseek.py" /usr/local/bin/cmseek
cmseek --version
```

##### Post-Installation: Updating Signatures
```bash
python3 cmseek.py --update
```
Expected output:
```
[+] Checking for updates...
[+] CMSeeK is up to date! (v1.1.3)
```

---

#### 3. Syntax

General syntax:
```bash
python3 cmseek.py -u <target-url> [options]
```
(or `cmseek -u <target-url> [options]` if symlinked globally)

Basic example:
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-site.com
```

---

#### 4. All Command-Line Options

##### 4.1 Target Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-u, --url <url>` | Target URL to scan |
| `-l, --list <file>` | File containing a list of target URLs for bulk scanning |
| `--random-agent` | Use a random User-Agent header |
| `--user-agent <string>` | Set a specific custom User-Agent |
| `--follow-redirects` | Follow HTTP redirects to the final URL |
| `--no-check-certificate` | Disable SSL certificate verification |
| `--timeout <seconds>` | Set the HTTP request timeout |

##### 4.2 Scan Behavior
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-b, --batch` | Batch mode — non-interactive scanning (needed for scripting/automation) |
| `-v, --verbose` | Verbose output, shows every request/detection attempt |
| `--is-wp` | Force WordPress deep-scan module regardless of detection |
| `--is-joomla` | Force Joomla deep-scan module |
| `--is-drupal` | Force Drupal deep-scan module |
| `--wp-enumerate <types>` | WordPress-specific enumeration: `u` (users), `p` (plugins), `vp` (vulnerable plugins), `vt` (vulnerable themes) |
| `--wp-en-uc` | Enumerate WordPress config file backups |

##### 4.3 Update & Maintenance
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `--update` | Update CMSeeK's CMS detection signature database |
| `--clear-cache` / `--rm` | Clear cached scan results |

##### 4.4 Output Options
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-r, --result <path>` | View/output the cached JSON result for a previously scanned target |
| `--no-color` | Disable colorized terminal output |

##### 4.5 Misc
| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-h, --help` | Show help menu |
| `--version` | Show installed version |

---

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b
```

Expected output (truncated):

```
                         ▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄
                        ▐░░░░░░░░░░░▌
                         ▀▀▀▀█░█▀▀▀▀
                             ▐░▌
                             ▐░▌
                       CMSeeK v1.1.3
              CMS Detection and Exploitation Suite
                  Twitter: @Tuhinshubhra
_______________________________________________________________

[+] Target: http://target-site.com
[+] Checking Connection... OK
[+] Target Server: nginx/1.24.0
[+] Checking Whether Target is Blocking us...NO
[+] Checking For Firewall...NOT DETECTED
[+] Detecting CMS

[+] Target CMS: WordPress
[+] Detected Confidence: 100%
[+] Detected Version: 6.5.3
[+] CMS URL: https://wordpress.org

[+] Handing off to WordPress Deep-Scan Module...

[+] Extracted Meta Generator Tag: WordPress 6.5.3

[+] Results saved at: /home/user/CMSeeK/Result/target-site_com/cms.json
```

---

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

##### Example 1 – Basic auto-detect scan in batch (non-interactive) mode
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Target CMS: WordPress
[+] Detected Version: 6.5.3
```

##### Example 2 – WordPress deep-scan with plugin/user enumeration
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-wp-site.com -b --wp-enumerate up
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Enumerating WordPress Users
[+] Found User: admin
[+] Found User: editor01

[+] Enumerating WordPress Plugins
[+] Plugin Found: woocommerce (v8.9.1)
[+] Plugin Found: yoast-seo (v22.5)
```

##### Example 3 – Force a specific CMS deep-scan module (useful when auto-detect is inconclusive)
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b --is-joomla
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Forcing Joomla Deep-Scan Module...
[+] Joomla Version Detected: 4.4.2
```

```bash
cat > mixed_targets.txt << 'EOF'
http://site1.example.com
http://site2.example.com
http://site3.example.com
EOF

cmseek -l mixed_targets.txt -b
```

```
[*] Scanning http://site1.example.com

[*] Scanning http://site2.example.com

[*] Scanning http://site3.example.com
[+] Target CMS: Joomla (v3.10.11)
```

```bash
cmseek -r target-site_com
```
Output (excerpt):

```json
{
  "cms_id": "wordpress",
  "cms_name": "WordPress",
  "cms_url": "https://wordpress.org",
  "detection_param": "meta_generator",
  "confidence": "100",
  "version": "6.5.3",
  "server": "nginx/1.24.0"
}
```

```bash

```

```bash
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b -v
```
Output (excerpt):

```
[verbose] Trying signature: wordpress -> /wp-login.php -> 200
[verbose] Match found: wordpress
```

##### Example 8 – Enumerate WordPress config backups specifically
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-wp-site.com -b --wp-en-uc
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Checking for Config Backups...
[+] Found: wp-config.php.bak (HTTP 200)
```

##### Example 9 – Update signature database before running a fresh engagement
```bash
cmseek --update
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b
```

##### Example 10 – Clear cached results before re-scanning a target that has since been patched
```bash
cmseek --clear-cache
cmseek -u http://target-site.com -b
```

##### Example 11 – Combine with jq to extract only the CMS name and version for a report
```bash
cmseek -r target-site_com | jq '{cms: .cms_name, version: .version}'
```
Output:
```json
{
  "cms": "WordPress",
  "version": "6.5.3"
}
```

##### Example 12 – Full engagement scan: detection + deep-scan + all enumeration flags
```bash
cmseek -u http://target-wp-site.com -b --wp-enumerate vp,vt,u --random-agent --timeout 30
```
Output (excerpt):
```
[+] Target CMS: WordPress (v6.5.3)
[+] Enumerating Vulnerable Plugins... 2 found
[+] Enumerating Vulnerable Themes... 0 found
[+] Enumerating Users... 3 found
```

---

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- **First-pass CMS triage:** Quickly determine which CMS platform (out of 180+ possibilities) a target is running before deciding which specialized tool to run next.
- **Unknown-CMS environments:** In large external attack-surface assessments where the CMS platform per host is unknown, CMSeeK's bulk-scan mode rapidly categorizes hundreds of hosts.
- **Lightweight WordPress/Joomla/Drupal auditing:** When a full WPScan/JoomScan run isn't necessary, CMSeeK's built-in deep-scan modules provide a fast summary of version, users, and plugins.
- **Asset inventory building:** Security teams use CMSeeK output (cached as structured JSON) to build and maintain an inventory of CMS platforms and versions across an organization's web properties.
- **Shadow IT discovery:** Identifying forgotten or unmanaged CMS installations (old Joomla/Drupal sites) during external reconnaissance.

---

#### 8. Automation with Bash

##### 8.1 Bulk Triage and Auto-Routing to Specialized Scanners
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# cmseek_triage.sh - detect CMS for each target, then route to the right specialized tool

TARGETS="mixed_targets.txt"
mkdir -p triage_results

while IFS= read -r url; do
    echo "[*] Detecting CMS for $url"
    cmseek -u "$url" -b > /dev/null 2>&1

    name=$(echo "$url" | sed -E 's#https?://##; s#[/:]#_#g')
    result="$HOME/CMSeeK/Result/${name}/cms.json"

    if [ -f "$result" ]; then
        cms=$(jq -r '.cms_id' "$result")
        echo "[+] $url -> $cms"

        case "$cms" in
            wordpress)
                wpscan --url "$url" -e vp,vt --format json --output "triage_results/${name}_wpscan.json" ;;
            joomla)
                joomscan -u "$url" -ec -ir "triage_results/${name}_joomscan" ;;
            drupal)
                droopescan scan drupal -u "$url" -e a -o json > "triage_results/${name}_droopescan.json" ;;
            *)
                echo "[i] No specialized scanner routed for CMS: $cms" ;;
        esac
    fi
done < "$TARGETS"
```

##### 8.2 Nightly CMS Inventory Snapshot
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# nightly_cms_inventory.sh
cmseek --update
cmseek -l company_hosts.txt -b

echo "== CMS Inventory Report $(date) ==" > inventory_report.txt
for dir in "$HOME"/CMSeeK/Result/*/; do
    if [ -f "${dir}cms.json" ]; then
        jq -r '"\(.cms_name) \(.version // "unknown")"' "${dir}cms.json" >> inventory_report.txt
    fi
done
sort inventory_report.txt | uniq -c | sort -rn
```

##### 8.3 Cron Job for Weekly Asset Inventory
```bash
# crontab -e
0 1 * * 0 /usr/local/bin/nightly_cms_inventory.sh >> /var/log/cmseek_weekly.log 2>&1
```

---

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always run CMSeeK first in a CMS security assessment — its speed and broad coverage make it ideal for triage before committing time to a heavier, CMS-specific scan.
- Use `-b` (batch mode) for any scripted or automated workflow; without it, CMSeeK may prompt interactively and break automation pipelines.
- Periodically run `cmseek --update` to keep the 180+ platform signature database current, since new CMS platforms and detection signatures are added regularly.
- Use `--clear-cache` before re-scanning a target that you know has changed (e.g., after a patch or migration), since CMSeeK caches results per-target by default.
- Combine CMSeeK's JSON output with `jq` for easy integration into asset inventory databases or SIEM pipelines.
- When CMSeeK's auto-detection is inconclusive or ambiguous (common on heavily customized sites), use the `--is-wp`, `--is-joomla`, or `--is-drupal` flags to force the relevant deep-scan module and get a second opinion.
- For any positively identified WordPress, Joomla, or Drupal target, follow up CMSeeK's initial pass with the dedicated deep-scan tool (WPScan, JoomScan, or Droopescan respectively) for the most authoritative, up-to-date vulnerability data.

---

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Solution |
|-------|-------|----------|
| `CMS Not Detected` on a known CMS site | Custom install hides generator tags/signatures | Try `-v` verbose mode to see which signatures were tested; force the module with `--is-wp` / `--is-joomla` / `--is-drupal` |
| Script hangs waiting for input | Ran without `-b` (batch mode) in an automated pipeline | Always include `-b` for non-interactive scripted scans |
| `ModuleNotFoundError` on startup | Missing Python dependencies | Re-run `pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages` inside the CMSeeK directory |
| Cached results are stale after target was patched | CMSeeK reuses cached JSON by target name | Run `cmseek --clear-cache` before re-scanning |
| SSL handshake failures | Self-signed certificate on staging environment | Add `--no-check-certificate` |
| `--update` fails | No internet access or GitHub connectivity blocked | Verify with `curl -I https://github.com`; check firewall/proxy rules |
| Deep-scan module doesn't trigger even though CMS is correctly detected | Confidence threshold not met, or module intentionally skipped for low-confidence matches | Force the module manually with the relevant `--is-*` flag |
| Global `cmseek` command not found | Tool installed via git clone without symlink setup | Either run `python3 cmseek.py` from within the cloned directory, or create the symlink as shown in the Installation section |

---

#### 11. References

- CMSeeK Official GitHub Repository: https://github.com/Tuhinshubhra/CMSeeK
- CMSeeK Wiki / Usage Documentation: https://github.com/Tuhinshubhra/CMSeeK/wiki
- Kali Linux Tools Listing – CMSeeK: https://www.kali.org/tools/cmseek/
- CMS Detection Signature Format Reference (whatcms-style): https://github.com/Tuhinshubhra/CMSeeK#cms-detection

## Chapter 10 – Password Attacks

Password attacks remain one of the most common and effective ways to gain unauthorized (in the context of authorized penetration testing) access to systems, applications, and services. Despite advances in authentication technology such as multi-factor authentication (MFA), biometrics, and passwordless login, password-based authentication is still the dominant mechanism protecting operating systems, web applications, network services, databases, wireless networks, and countless other systems worldwide.

This chapter covers the primary categories of password attacks that a security professional will encounter and need to master:

- **Online password attacks** – attacks performed directly against a live service (SSH, FTP, RDP, HTTP forms, SMB, etc.) by repeatedly submitting login attempts. Tools: Hydra, Medusa, Ncrack, Patator.
- **Wordlist generation and customization** – building targeted, intelligent wordlists rather than relying on generic lists. Tools: CeWL, Crunch, CUPP.
- **Offline password attacks** – cracking password hashes that have already been captured or dumped, performed without touching the live target. Tools: John the Ripper, Hashcat.
- **Legacy Windows password recovery** – recovering LM/NTLM hashes using rainbow tables. Tool: Ophcrack.

Kali Linux ships with all of these tools pre-installed, and each has its own strengths depending on the protocol, hash type, and constraints of the target environment (network speed, account lockout policies, hash algorithm, available compute/GPU resources, etc.).

> **Legal and ethical notice:** Every tool and technique in this chapter must only be used against systems you own or have explicit, documented authorization to test. Unauthorized password attacks are illegal in most jurisdictions and constitute a serious violation of computer misuse laws. All examples in this chapter assume a lab environment or an engagement with a signed rules-of-engagement (RoE) document.

### 10.1 Hydra

#### 1. Introduction

THC-Hydra (commonly just "Hydra") is one of the oldest and most widely used **parallelized network login cracker**. It was originally released by van Hauser of The Hacker's Choice (THC) and is designed to perform rapid, multi-threaded brute-force and dictionary attacks against a huge range of network protocols and services.

Hydra's core strength is protocol coverage — it ships with modules for more than 50 protocols, including SSH, FTP, Telnet, HTTP/HTTPS (GET, POST, and form-based auth), SMB, RDP, VNC, MySQL, MSSQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SNMP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, LDAP, Cisco, Teamspeak, XMPP, and many more. It is pre-installed on Kali Linux and is typically the first tool a penetration tester reaches for when they need to test credentials against a running service.

Hydra supports single-username, username-list, single-password, and password-list attacks, as well as combined "login:password" pair lists, making it flexible enough for brute-force, dictionary, and credential-stuffing/spraying attacks alike.

##### 2. Key Features
- Over 50 supported protocol modules
- Multi-threaded (parallel) connections for speed
- Supports both CLI and a GTK graphical front-end (`hydra-gtk` / `xhydra`)
- Resume support for interrupted scans (`-R`)
- Proxy support (HTTP/SOCKS) for anonymization or pivoting
- Colorized output (`-C`/verbose modes) and progress reporting

#### 3. Installation

Hydra is included by default on Kali Linux. If it is missing or you want to update it:

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install hydra hydra-gtk -y
```

Verify the installation:

```bash
hydra -h
```

To build the latest version from source (useful for the newest protocol modules):

```bash
git clone https://github.com/vanhauser-thc/thc-hydra.git
cd thc-hydra
sudo apt install libssl-dev libssh-dev libidn11-dev libpcre3-dev \
  libgtk2.0-dev libmariadb-dev-compat firebird-dev libmemcached-dev \
  libgpg-error-dev libgcrypt20-dev libgcrypt20-dev -y
./configure
make
sudo make install
```

#### 4. Syntax

General command form:

```bash
hydra [options] [-l LOGIN|-L LOGINFILE] [-p PASS|-P PASSFILE] target service [service-options]
```

Or using a URL-style target specification:

```bash
hydra [options] service://target[:port][/optional-parameters]
```

#### 5. Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-l LOGIN` | Single username to try |
| `-L FILE` | File containing a list of usernames |
| `-p PASS` | Single password to try |
| `-P FILE` | File containing a list of passwords |
| `-C FILE` | Colon-separated `login:password` combo file (skips `-l`/`-L`/`-p`/`-P`) |
| `-M FILE` | List of target hosts/IPs to attack, one per line |
| `-o FILE` | Write successful found login/password pairs to FILE |
| `-b FORMAT` | Output format for `-o`: text, json, jsonv1 |
| `-f` | Exit after the first valid login/password pair is found (single target) |
| `-F` | Exit after the first valid login/password pair is found (across all targets in `-M`) |
| `-t TASKS` | Number of parallel connections/tasks per target (default 16) |
| `-T TASKS` | Number of parallel connections across all targets in `-M` mode |
| `-s PORT` | Non-default port for the service |
| `-S` | Use SSL/TLS for the connection |
| `-e nsr` | Additional checks: `n`=try null password, `s`=try login as password, `r`=try reversed login as password |
| `-x MIN:MAX:CHARSET` | Generate passwords instead of using a file (brute-force mode) |
| `-y` | Disable use of `:` as delimiter in `-x`/rules |
| `-u` | Loop users instead of passwords (try each user against all passwords before moving to next user is default; `-u` flips the loop order) |
| `-v` / `-V` | Verbose mode; show login+pass combination for each attempt |
| `-d` | Debug mode |
| `-R` | Restore/resume a previous aborted/crashed session |
| `-I` | Ignore an existing restore file (start fresh, do not prompt) |
| `-q` | Do not print connection error/warning messages |
| `-w TIME` | Wait time (seconds) between connection attempts per thread (default 32) |
| `-W TIME` | Wait time for responses |
| `-c TIME` | Wait time between login attempts across all threads (for very sensitive/lockout-prone targets) |
| `-4` / `-6` | Force IPv4 or IPv6 |
| `-4` / `-6` | Use IPv4/IPv6 addressing |
| `-m OPT` | Pass an optional module-specific string (e.g., HTTP form path) |
| `-U` | Print module-specific usage/help for the given service |
| `-h` | Print Hydra help |
| `server` | Target IP address or hostname |
| `service` | Protocol module to use, e.g. ssh, ftp, http-get, http-post-form, rdp, smb, mysql |

#### 6. Basic Usage

Simplest possible SSH brute-force attempt against a single target with one username and a password list:

```bash
hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://192.168.1.10
```

Expected output:

```
Hydra v9.5 (c) 2023 by van Hauser/THC & David Maciejak - for legal purposes only

Hydra (https://github.com/vanhauser-thc/thc-hydra) starting at 2026-07-19 10:02:11
[DATA] max 16 tasks per 1 server, overall 16 tasks, 14344399 login tries (l:1/p:14344399), ~896525 tries per task
[DATA] attacking ssh://192.168.1.10:22/
[STATUS] 743.00 tries/min, 743 tries in 00:01h, 14343656 to do in 321:36h, 16 active
[22][ssh] host: 192.168.1.10   login: admin   password: summer2023
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
Hydra (https://github.com/vanhauser-thc/thc-hydra) finished at 2026-07-19 10:03:47
```

#### 7. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — SSH brute-force with a username list and password list**

```bash
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -t 4 ssh://10.10.10.5
```
```
[DATA] max 4 tasks per 1 server, overall 4 tasks, 42992 login tries
[22][ssh] host: 10.10.10.5   login: sysadmin   password: letmein123
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
```

**Example 2 — FTP with single login and stop on first success**

```bash
hydra -l ftpuser -P passwords.txt -f ftp://10.10.10.20
```
```
[21][ftp] host: 10.10.10.20   login: ftpuser   password: welcome1
1 of 1 target successfully completed, 1 valid password found
```

**Example 3 — HTTP POST form login brute-force**

```bash
hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt 10.10.10.30 http-post-form \
  "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:Invalid credentials"
```
```
[DATA] attacking http-post-form://10.10.10.30:80/login.php
[80][http-post-form] host: 10.10.10.30   login: admin   password: P@ssw0rd!
```

**Example 4 — RDP password spraying with a combo file**

```bash
hydra -C rdp_combos.txt rdp://10.10.10.40
```
```
[3389][rdp] host: 10.10.10.40   login: helpdesk   password: Welcome2024
```

**Example 5 — SMB credential check across a subnet using -M**

```bash
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt -M targets.txt -o smb_results.txt smb
```
```
[445][smb] host: 10.10.10.51   login: backupsvc   password: Backup#1
[445][smb] host: 10.10.10.62   login: printsvc    password: Printer2020
2 targets successfully completed, 2 valid passwords found
```

**Example 6 — MySQL with generated brute-force passwords (`-x`)**

```bash
hydra -l root -x 4:6:a1 mysql://10.10.10.70
```
```
[3306][mysql] host: 10.10.10.70   login: root   password: a1b2c
```

**Example 7 — Verbose mode to watch every attempt live**

```bash
hydra -l admin -P small_list.txt -V ssh://10.10.10.5
```
```
[ATTEMPT] target 10.10.10.5 - login "admin" - pass "123456" - 1 of 12
[ATTEMPT] target 10.10.10.5 - login "admin" - pass "password" - 2 of 12
[ATTEMPT] target 10.10.10.5 - login "admin" - pass "admin123" - 3 of 12
[22][ssh] host: 10.10.10.5   login: admin   password: admin123
```

**Example 8 — Resuming a crashed/interrupted session**

```bash
hydra -R
```
```
[INFO] Restoring old session from ./hydra.restore
[DATA] resuming previous run at attempt 118432 of 14344399
```

#### 8. Common Use Cases

- Testing SSH/RDP/FTP servers for default or weak credentials during an internal or external pentest
- Validating password policy enforcement (does the target lock out after N failed attempts?)
- Attacking self-hosted web application login forms (`http-post-form` / `http-get-form`)
- Credential stuffing with breached `login:pass` combo lists against exposed services
- Verifying that a discovered password is reused across multiple services (SSH, FTP, SMB, etc.)

#### 9. Automation with Bash

Loop Hydra over multiple services discovered by an Nmap scan:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# hydra-multiservice.sh - brute-force every open service found by nmap
TARGET="10.10.10.5"
USERLIST="users.txt"
PASSLIST="/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt"

for svc in ssh ftp telnet; do
  echo "[*] Attacking $svc on $TARGET"
  hydra -L "$USERLIST" -P "$PASSLIST" -t 4 -o "results_${svc}.txt" "$svc://$TARGET"
done

echo "[*] Done. Results saved as results_<service>.txt"
```

Automatically parse hosts from a `targets.txt` file and attack each with SSH:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
while read -r host; do
  hydra -l admin -P passwords.txt -f "ssh://$host" -o "found_${host}.txt"
done < targets.txt
```

#### 10. Tips and Best Practices

- Always start with a small, curated password list (top 100/500) before firing off `rockyou.txt` — most weak credentials are found in the first few thousand attempts.
- Reduce `-t` (threads) against sensitive or lockout-prone targets (e.g., Active Directory) to avoid triggering account lockout policies — 1–4 threads with `-c` delay is safer for AD.
- Use `-e nsr` to automatically test blank passwords, username-as-password, and reversed-username-as-password — these catch a surprising number of accounts.
- Save output with `-o` (and `-b json` for machine-parsing) so results integrate cleanly into reporting or other tools.
- Use `-M` with a target list plus `-F` to stop as soon as any target yields valid credentials, useful for large subnet sprays.
- For HTTP form attacks, always confirm the exact failure string (e.g., "Invalid credentials") by testing a wrong login manually first — an incorrect failure string will cause 100% false positives or false negatives.

#### 11. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---------|-------------|
| `[ERROR] could not connect to target` | Verify the service is reachable (`nc -zv host port`) and the port/`-s` value is correct |
| No results despite correct credentials being in the list | Check that the failure string in `http-post-form` is exact; test with `-V` to view raw attempts |
| Extremely slow scan | Increase `-t`, but watch for target-side lockout/rate limiting; some services intentionally throttle failed logins |
| `Too many redirects` on http-form modules | Add `:H=Cookie: value` or handle session cookies manually with `-m` module options |
| Account gets locked after a few attempts | Use `-t 1` and add `-c` delay, or switch to a slower/smarter tool like Patator with jitter |
| Hydra hangs indefinitely | Add `-w` and `-W` timeout values; some services silently drop connections instead of rejecting |

#### 12. References

- Official repository: https://github.com/vanhauser-thc/thc-hydra
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/hydra/
- Man page: `man hydra`
- THC (The Hacker's Choice): https://www.thc.org/

### 10.2 Medusa

#### 1. Introduction

Medusa is a speedy, massively parallel, modular login brute-forcer, created by the Foofus.net team as a direct alternative to Hydra. Like Hydra, it can attack many different network services, but it was designed from the ground up around a clean, thread-based, modular architecture (each protocol is a separate `.mod` shared library), which makes it easy to extend and, according to its authors, more stable under heavy parallel load.

Medusa supports thread-per-host parallelism, meaning it can brute-force multiple hosts and multiple usernames simultaneously, each in its own thread, rather than serializing across targets. It ships with modules for services including SSH, FTP, Telnet, HTTP, SMB, MS-SQL, MySQL, PostgreSQL, POP3, IMAP, VNC, PcAnywhere, Rlogin, SNMP, SVN, VMware Authentication Daemon, Web Form, and more.

##### 2.Key Features
- Thread-based parallel testing of multiple hosts, users, or passwords at once
- Modular design (`.mod` files) — new protocols can be added without recompiling the core
- Flexible input: single or list-based users/passwords, combined with wildcards
- Can resume/stop early once valid credentials are found
- Non-blocking, resumable network I/O for stability on large host lists

#### 3. Installation

Medusa is preinstalled on Kali Linux. To install/update manually:

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install medusa -y
```

Verify installation and list available modules:

```bash
medusa -d
```

Building from source:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/jmk-foofus/medusa.git
cd medusa
./configure
make
sudo make install
```

#### 4. Syntax

```bash
medusa -h HOST|-H FILE -u USER|-U FILE -p PASS|-P FILE -M MODULE [options]
```

#### 5. Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-h HOST` | Single target host |
| `-H FILE` | File containing list of target hosts |
| `-u USER` | Single username |
| `-U FILE` | File containing list of usernames |
| `-p PASS` | Single password |
| `-P FILE` | File containing list of passwords |
| `-C FILE` | Combo file of `host:user:password` (skips other targeting options) |
| `-O FILE` | Log successful attempts to FILE |
| `-e ns` | Additional password checks: `n`=null password, `s`=password same as username |
| `-M MODULE` | Module to use (e.g. ssh, ftp, http, smbnt, mysql, telnet, vnc) |
| `-m OPT` | Pass a parameter to the selected module (module-specific, e.g. `-m PORT:8080`) |
| `-n PORT` | Use a non-default port |
| `-s` | Enable SSL |
| `-g SEC` | Give up on a login after SEC seconds (timeout) |
| `-r SEC` | Sleep SEC seconds between retries |
| `-R RETRIES` | Number of retry attempts on connection failure |
| `-t TASKS` | Total number of parallel logins to perform (threads) |
| `-T TASKS` | Total number of parallel hosts to test |
| `-L` | Parallelize logins using one username per thread rather than one password per thread |
| `-f` | Stop scanning this host after first valid login/pass found |
| `-F` | Stop scanning entirely (all hosts) after first valid login/pass found |
| `-b` | Suppress the startup banner |
| `-q` | Display module's usage information |
| `-d` | Dump a list of all available modules |
| `-v LEVEL` | Verbose level (0–6) — controls how much output is shown |
| `-w LEVEL` | Error verbosity level (0–10) |
| `-V` | Display version |
| `-Z RESUMEFILE` | Resume a previous scan from a resume file |

#### 6. Basic Usage

```bash
medusa -h 192.168.1.10 -u admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -M ssh
```

Expected output:

```
Medusa v2.2 [http://www.foofus.net] (C) JoMo-Kun / Foofus Networks <jmk@foofus.net>

ACCOUNT CHECK: [ssh] Host: 192.168.1.10 (1 of 1, 0 complete) User: admin (1 of 1, 0 complete) Password: 123456 (1 of 14344399 complete)
ACCOUNT CHECK: [ssh] Host: 192.168.1.10 (1 of 1, 0 complete) User: admin (1 of 1, 0 complete) Password: password (2 of 14344399 complete)
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ssh] Host: 192.168.1.10 User: admin Password: summer2023 [SUCCESS]
```

#### 7. Practical Examples

**Example 1 — Basic SSH brute-force**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.5 -u sysadmin -P rockyou.txt -M ssh -t 4
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ssh] Host: 10.10.10.5 User: sysadmin Password: letmein123 [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 2 — Multiple hosts and users from files**

```bash
medusa -H hosts.txt -U users.txt -P passwords.txt -M ftp -t 8 -T 4
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ftp] Host: 10.10.10.20 User: ftpuser Password: welcome1 [SUCCESS]
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ftp] Host: 10.10.10.21 User: backup Password: Backup2020 [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 3 — RDP brute-force with connection timeout**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.40 -u helpdesk -P passwords.txt -M rdp -g 5 -r 3
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [rdp] Host: 10.10.10.40 User: helpdesk Password: Welcome2024 [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 4 — MySQL root password test with stop-on-success**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.70 -u root -P common-mysql-passwords.txt -M mysql -f
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [mysql] Host: 10.10.10.70 User: root Password: toor [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 5 — Web form brute-force**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.30 -u admin -P rockyou.txt -M http \
  -m FORM:"/login.php:username=&password=&Login=Login:F=Invalid credentials"
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [http] Host: 10.10.10.30 User: admin Password: P@ssw0rd! [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 6 — Combo file attack (host:user:pass)**

```bash
medusa -C combos.txt -M ssh
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ssh] Host: 10.10.10.5 User: root Password: toor123 [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 7 — Verbose output for debugging**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.5 -u admin -P small.txt -M ssh -v 5
```
```
[DEBUG] Establishing connection to 10.10.10.5:22
[DEBUG] Sending credentials admin:123456
[DEBUG] Server response: Authentication failed
[DEBUG] Sending credentials admin:admin123
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ssh] Host: 10.10.10.5 User: admin Password: admin123 [SUCCESS]
```

**Example 8 — Non-default SSH port**

```bash
medusa -h 10.10.10.5 -u admin -P passwords.txt -M ssh -n 2222
```
```
ACCOUNT FOUND: [ssh] Host: 10.10.10.5 User: admin Password: Sp1cyTaco! [SUCCESS]
```

#### 8. Common Use Cases

- Parallel credential testing across an entire subnet or host list (`-H` + `-T`)
- SSH/FTP/RDP weak-credential audits during internal penetration tests
- Testing web application login endpoints for weak/default passwords
- Verifying database service (MySQL/MSSQL/PostgreSQL) default credentials
- Second-opinion / cross-validation tool alongside Hydra to confirm findings

#### 9. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# medusa-sweep.sh — brute force SSH across a list of hosts, one result file per host
while read -r host; do
  echo "[*] Testing $host"
  medusa -h "$host" -U users.txt -P passwords.txt -M ssh -t 4 -O "medusa_${host}.log"
done < hosts.txt
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# medusa-multi-module.sh — test the same creds against several services
HOST="10.10.10.5"
for mod in ssh ftp telnet; do
  medusa -h "$HOST" -u admin -P passwords.txt -M "$mod" -f -O "medusa_${mod}.log"
done
```

#### 10. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `-T` to spread load across many hosts in parallel rather than only increasing `-t` (per-host threads) — this is much faster for subnet-wide sweeps.
- Set `-g` (timeout) and `-R` (retries) explicitly on unreliable networks/VPNs to avoid false negatives from dropped connections.
- Use `-f`/`-F` in large sweeps so Medusa doesn't waste time continuing to brute-force a host once valid creds are already found.
- Run `medusa -d` first to confirm exactly which modules and their options are available in your installed version — module option syntax (`-m`) varies by protocol.
- Combine `-e ns` with a short password list first — many internal services still use blank or username-as-password credentials.

#### 11. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause / Fix |
|---------|-------------|
| `[ERROR] Failed to open module` | Module name is wrong or module `.mod` file missing — run `medusa -d` to list valid module names |
| Scan seems to hang | Increase `-g` (timeout) or reduce `-t`; the target may be silently dropping excess connections |
| False "FOUND" results on web form module | The `F=` (failure string) match is too broad/wrong — verify exact text returned on a bad login manually |
| High CPU/network usage crashes the scanner | Reduce `-t` and `-T`; very high thread counts can exhaust local file descriptors |
| Permission denied errors on install | Use `sudo` for `apt install` or `make install` |

#### 12. References

- Official site: http://www.foofus.net/?page_id=51
- GitHub: https://github.com/jmk-foofus/medusa
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/medusa/
- Man page: `man medusa`

### 10.3 Ncrack

#### 1. Introduction

Ncrack is a high-speed network authentication cracking tool built by the Nmap project team, designed specifically to help companies audit their own network device and service credentials at scale. Unlike Hydra and Medusa, which grew organically to support many protocols, Ncrack was engineered with a strong focus on **reliability at scale and Nmap-style scripting syntax**, making it particularly good for large enterprise audits (e.g., testing thousands of RDP or SSH endpoints for weak/default passwords).

Ncrack uses an Nmap-like command-line syntax and timing templates (`-T0` through `-T5`), and it supports a "grammar" file format for defining custom authentication modules. It natively understands RDP, SSH, Telnet, HTTP(S), POP3(S), IMAP(S), SMB, VNC, FTP, and a few others, with RDP and SSH being particularly well optimized (RDP brute-forcing especially is one of Ncrack's standout strengths, since it correctly handles NLA in many configurations).

##### 2. Key Features
- Nmap-style CLI syntax and timing templates for fine control over aggressiveness
- Excellent RDP and SSH module reliability compared to older tools
- Can resume a scan using a saved session state
- Interactive runtime console (press keys during a scan to view stats or change timing)
- Outputs in normal, XML, and "greppable" formats — good for automation pipelines

#### 3. Installation

Ncrack is included on Kali Linux by default. To install/update:

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ncrack -y
```

Verify:

```bash
ncrack -V
```

Build from source (latest features):

```bash
git clone https://github.com/nmap/ncrack.git
cd ncrack
./configure
make
sudo make install
```

#### 4. Syntax

```bash
ncrack [options] {target specification}
```

Target specification generally looks like:

```bash
ncrack -p protocol://target[:port] [options]
```

or protocol options can be passed with `-p`:

```bash
ncrack -p PORT --user USER --pass PASS target
```

#### 5. Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-p PORT[,PORT2,...]` or `service` | Target port(s)/service(s), e.g. `-p ssh,rdp` or `-p 22,3389` |
| `-iL FILE` | Read target list from FILE |
| `-iX FILE` | Read targets from Nmap XML output |
| `-U FILE` | Username list file (global, applies to all targets unless overridden) |
| `-P FILE` | Password list file |
| `--user USER` | Single username |
| `--pass PASS` | Single password |
| `-u`, `-p` (module option) | Module-specific credentials, e.g. `ssh --user root --pass toor` |
| `-CL FILE` | Combined `user:pass` credentials list file |
| `-T0`–`-T5` | Timing template from Paranoid (0) to Insane (5) |
| `-d LEVEL` | Debugging level |
| `-v` | Increase verbosity (can be repeated, e.g. `-vv`) |
