```
Docker CIS 1.6.0 Compliance Report
====================================
┌────────┬───────────────────────────────┬────────┐
│  ID    │           Title                │ Status │
├────────┼───────────────────────────────┼────────┤
│ 5.6    │ Root user should not be used   │ FAIL   │
│ 5.10   │ Memory usage should be limited │ PASS   │
└────────┴───────────────────────────────┴────────┘
```

**Example 12 – Scan multiple images in a loop and export CSV summary:**
```bash
for img in nginx:1.25 redis:7.2 alpine:3.19; do
  count=$(trivy image --severity CRITICAL --format json "$img" | jq '[.Results[]?.Vulnerabilities[]?] | length')
  echo "$img,$count" >> summary.csv
done
cat summary.csv
```
```
nginx:1.25,4
redis:7.2,1
alpine:3.19,1
```

#### 7 Common Use Cases

- **CI/CD pipeline gating** – fail a build if CRITICAL vulnerabilities without a fix are found.
- **Registry scanning** – periodically scan every image in a private registry for drift.
- **Pre-deployment gate** – scan Kubernetes manifests/Helm charts with `trivy config` before `kubectl apply`.
- **Supply-chain auditing** – generate SBOMs for every release artifact and archive them for compliance.
- **Secret leak detection** – scan repositories and images before publishing to catch hard-coded credentials.
- **Compliance reporting** – run `--compliance docker-cis` or `k8s-cis` reports for auditors.

#### 8 Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# trivy-ci-gate.sh - Fail CI build on CRITICAL vulnerabilities with a fix available
set -euo pipefail

IMAGE="$1"
REPORT_DIR="./trivy-reports"
mkdir -p "$REPORT_DIR"

echo "[*] Updating Trivy DB..."
trivy image --download-db-only

echo "[*] Scanning $IMAGE ..."
trivy image \
  --severity CRITICAL,HIGH \
  --ignore-unfixed \
  --exit-code 1 \
  --format json \
  --output "$REPORT_DIR/$(echo "$IMAGE" | tr '/:' '_').json" \
  "$IMAGE"

STATUS=$?
if [ "$STATUS" -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "[!] Build blocked: CRITICAL/HIGH vulnerabilities found in $IMAGE"
  exit 1
fi
echo "[+] $IMAGE passed the security gate."
```

Run it:
```bash
chmod +x trivy-ci-gate.sh
./trivy-ci-gate.sh myapp:latest
```

#### 9 Tips and Best Practices

- Run `trivy image --download-db-only` on a schedule (cron) so scans stay fast and offline-capable.
- Always combine `--ignore-unfixed` with CI gating — blocking builds for vulnerabilities with no available fix creates noise without actionable remediation.
- Use `--scanners vuln,misconfig,secret` together on `fs`/`repository` targets for full-spectrum coverage in one pass.
- Store `.trivyignore` files in version control with comments explaining *why* each CVE is suppressed, and set expiry review dates.
- In air-gapped environments, mirror the vulnerability DB using `trivy --cache-dir` and `oras`/`skopeo` to move the OCI DB artifact.
- Prefer `--format json` piped to `jq` for machine-parseable pipeline decisions rather than parsing table output.
- Pin the Trivy version in CI to avoid unexpected new checks silently breaking existing pipelines.

#### 10 Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `unable to open GOB file` cache error | Corrupted local cache | Run `trivy clean --all` and rescan |
| Scan hangs on DB download | Firewall blocking GitHub Container Registry | Set `--skip-db-update` and pre-seed cache, or allow `ghcr.io` |
| `permission denied` scanning Docker socket | User not in `docker` group | `sudo usermod -aG docker $USER` and re-login |
| Rate limit errors pulling DB | GitHub API rate limiting | Set `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var to authenticate DB pulls |
| No vulnerabilities found on known-bad image | Wrong `--vuln-type` filter | Remove filter or set `--vuln-type os,library` |
| `context deadline exceeded` | Default 5m timeout too short for large images | Increase with `--timeout 15m` |

#### 11 References

- Official documentation: https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy/
- GitHub repository: https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy
- Vulnerability DB source: https://github.com/aquasecurity/trivy-db
- Aqua Security blog: https://www.aquasec.com/blog/


### 17.2 Docker Bench for Security

#### 1 Introduction

Docker Bench for Security is an open-source shell script published by Docker, Inc. that audits a running Docker host against dozens of automated checks based on the **CIS Docker Benchmark**, a set of security configuration best practices published by the Center for Internet Security. Unlike Trivy, which analyzes image contents for vulnerabilities, Docker Bench inspects the *host configuration, daemon configuration, and running container configuration* — things like whether the Docker daemon socket is exposed without TLS, whether containers run with unnecessary privileges, whether logging is configured, and whether the host kernel has appropriate hardening enabled.

It checks areas including:

- Host configuration (kernel, partitioning, auditd rules for Docker files)
- Docker daemon configuration (`/etc/docker/daemon.json`, socket permissions, ICC, live-restore)
- Docker daemon configuration files (permissions/ownership of Docker files and directories)
- Container images and build files (USER instruction, trusted base images, health checks)
- Container runtime (privileged mode, capabilities, `--net=host`, PID/IPC namespace sharing, resource limits)
- Docker security operations (image sprawl, container sprawl)
- Docker Swarm configuration (if applicable)

#### 2 Installation

**Method 1 – Run directly via Docker container (simplest, no install needed):**
```bash
docker run --rm --net host --pid host --userns host --cap-add audit_control \
  -e DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST=$DOCKER_CONTENT_TRUST \
  -v /etc:/etc:ro \
  -v /usr/bin/containerd:/usr/bin/containerd:ro \
  -v /usr/bin/runc:/usr/bin/runc:ro \
  -v /usr/lib/systemd:/usr/lib/systemd:ro \
  -v /var/lib:/var/lib:ro \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro \
  --label docker_bench_security \
  docker/docker-bench-security
```

**Method 2 – Clone and run natively (needed for host-level auditd checks):**
```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install git -y
git clone https://github.com/docker/docker-bench-security.git
cd docker-bench-security
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh
```

**Method 3 – Kali package (if available in repos):**
```bash
sudo apt-get install docker-bench-security -y
```

**Verify installation (git method):**
```bash
cd docker-bench-security
./docker-bench-security.sh -v
```
Expected output:
```
Docker Bench for Security v1.6.0
```

#### 3 Syntax

```
./docker-bench-security.sh [OPTIONS]
```

#### 4 Command-Line Options

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-b` | Do not print colors |
| `-h` | Print usage help |
| `-c CHECK` | Run only specific check(s), comma-separated (e.g. `-c container_images`) |
| `-e CHECK` | Exclude specific check(s) from the run |
| `-l FILE` | Log output to a specified file |
| `-i INCLUDE` | Include only specific check IDs (e.g. `-i 4.1,4.5`) |
| `-x EXCLUDE` | Exclude specific check IDs |
| `-n LIMIT` | Limit output to N lines per section |
| `-p PRINT` | Print the results in a given format |
| `-u USERS` | Specify list of sudo users to check |
| `-j` | Output results as JSON |
| `-v` | Print version and exit |

Common check group names usable with `-c`/`-e`:
```
check_host_configuration
check_docker_daemon_configuration
check_docker_daemon_configuration_files
check_container_images
check_container_runtime
check_docker_security_operations
check_docker_swarm_configuration
```

#### 5 Basic Usage

```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh
```

Expected output (truncated):
```
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Docker Bench for Security v1.6.0
#
# Docker, Inc. (c) 2025 -
#
# Checks for dozens of common best-practices around deploying Docker containers in production.
# Inspired by the CIS Docker Community Edition Benchmark v1.6.0.
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Initializing Sun Jul 19 10:31:02 +05 2026

[INFO] 1 - Host Configuration
[WARN] 1.1  - Ensure a separate partition for containers has been created
[PASS] 1.2  - Ensure only trusted users are allowed to control Docker daemon
[WARN] 1.3  - Ensure auditing is configured for the Docker daemon
[WARN] 1.4  - Ensure auditing is configured for Docker files and directories - /run/containerd

[INFO] 2 - Docker daemon configuration
[PASS] 2.1  - Ensure network traffic is restricted between containers on the default bridge
[WARN] 2.2  - Ensure the logging level is set to 'info'
[PASS] 2.5  - Ensure aufs storage driver is not used

[INFO] 4 - Container Images and Build File
[WARN] 4.1  - Ensure a user for the container has been created
[PASS] 4.5  - Ensure Content trust for Docker is Enabled

[INFO] 5 - Container Runtime
[WARN] 5.1  - Ensure AppArmor Profile is Enabled
[PASS] 5.2  - Ensure SELinux security options are set, if applicable
[WARN] 5.4  - Ensure privileged containers are not used
[WARN] 5.7  - Ensure privileged ports are not mapped within containers

[INFO] Checks: 115
[INFO] Score: 42
```

#### 6 Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 – Run only the container runtime checks:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -c check_container_runtime
```
```
[INFO] 5 - Container Runtime
[WARN] 5.1  - Ensure AppArmor Profile is Enabled
[WARN] 5.4  - Ensure privileged containers are not used
     * Container running in Privileged mode: web_debug
[PASS] 5.9  - Ensure the host's network namespace is not shared
[WARN] 5.25 - Ensure the container is restricted from acquiring additional privileges
```

**Example 2 – Exclude Swarm checks on a non-Swarm host:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -e check_docker_swarm_configuration
```
```
[INFO] 7 - Docker Swarm Configuration
[INFO]   * Skipped: Docker Swarm checks excluded
```

**Example 3 – Log full results to a file:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -l /var/log/docker-bench.log
cat /var/log/docker-bench.log | tail -5
```
```
[INFO] Checks: 115
[INFO] Score: 47
[INFO] Total Warnings: 34
[INFO] Total Passes: 55
[INFO] Total Info: 26
```

**Example 4 – Output as JSON for programmatic parsing:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -j
```
```json
{
  "start": "2026-07-19T10:41:07Z",
  "end": "2026-07-19T10:41:19Z",
  "checks": [
    {"id": "1.1", "desc": "Ensure a separate partition for containers has been created", "result": "WARN"},
    {"id": "4.1", "desc": "Ensure a user for the container has been created", "result": "WARN"}
  ],
  "score": 42
}
```

**Example 5 – Run only specific check IDs:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -i 5.4,5.7,5.9
```
```
[WARN] 5.4  - Ensure privileged containers are not used
[WARN] 5.7  - Ensure privileged ports are not mapped within containers
[PASS] 5.9  - Ensure the host's network namespace is not shared
```

**Example 6 – Run via Docker container against the host daemon:**
```bash
docker run --rm --net host --pid host --userns host --cap-add audit_control \
  -v /etc:/etc:ro -v /var/lib:/var/lib:ro -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro \
  --label docker_bench_security docker/docker-bench-security
```
```
[INFO] 3 - Docker daemon configuration files
[PASS] 3.1  - Ensure that the docker.service file ownership is set to root:root
[PASS] 3.2  - Ensure that docker.service file permissions are appropriately set
[WARN] 3.19 - Ensure that the Docker socket file ownership is set to root:docker
```

**Example 7 – Detect a container running as root:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -c check_container_images
```
```
[WARN] 4.1  - Ensure a user for the container has been created
     * Container running as root: nginx_prod (nginx:latest)
```

**Example 8 – Combine host and daemon checks only:**
```bash
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -c check_host_configuration,check_docker_daemon_configuration
```
```
[INFO] 1 - Host Configuration
[WARN] 1.1  - Ensure a separate partition for containers has been created
[INFO] 2 - Docker daemon configuration
[PASS] 2.6  - Ensure TLS authentication for Docker daemon is configured
```

#### 7 Common Use Cases

- Auditing a fresh Docker host before it goes into production.
- Periodic (weekly/monthly) compliance sweeps of production Docker hosts.
- Pre-flight validation on Kali or any pentest jump box being used as a Docker host to avoid self-inflicted misconfigurations.
- Feeding results into a SIEM or GRC tool via the `-j` JSON output for tracking compliance drift over time.
- Validating remediation after fixing a previously flagged item.

#### 8 Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# docker-bench-weekly.sh - Weekly Docker host audit with alerting
set -euo pipefail

REPO_DIR="/opt/docker-bench-security"
REPORT="/var/log/docker-bench-$(date +%F).log"
THRESHOLD=50   # minimum acceptable score

cd "$REPO_DIR"
sudo ./docker-bench-security.sh -l "$REPORT" -j > "${REPORT}.json"

SCORE=$(grep -oP '(?<=Score: )\d+' "$REPORT" | tail -1)

echo "[*] Docker Bench score: $SCORE"

if [ "$SCORE" -lt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
  echo "[!] Score below threshold ($THRESHOLD). Sending alert..."
  mail -s "Docker Bench Alert: score $SCORE" secteam@example.com < "$REPORT"
fi
```

Schedule with cron:
```bash
crontab -e
# Run every Monday at 06:00
0 6 * * 1 /opt/docker-bench-security/docker-bench-weekly.sh
```

#### 9 Tips and Best Practices

- Run Docker Bench natively with `sudo` (not only inside a container) whenever possible — host-level auditd checks require access to the real filesystem and cannot be fully evaluated from inside a container.
- Treat `WARN` as "review", not automatically "fix" — some warnings (e.g. Swarm checks on a non-Swarm single-host setup) are expected and can be excluded with `-e`.
- Re-run after every Docker daemon upgrade; `daemon.json` defaults sometimes change between versions.
- Combine with Trivy: Docker Bench secures the *host/daemon/runtime*, Trivy secures the *image contents* — neither replaces the other.
- Bake remediated `daemon.json` settings (e.g. `"icc": false`, `"live-restore": true`, `"userland-proxy": false`) into your host provisioning (Ansible/Terraform) so the audit passes by default on every new host.
- Avoid running unnecessary containers in `--privileged` mode; this is consistently one of the highest-impact findings.

#### 10 Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Script exits immediately with "This script must be run as root" | Not run with sudo | Prefix with `sudo` |
| Host configuration checks all show `INFO`/skip | Running inside a container without host mounts | Use the native git-clone method, not the container method, for host-level checks |
| `command not found: docker` | Docker not installed or not in PATH | Install Docker Engine first |
| Audit rules checks always WARN | `auditd` not installed/configured | `sudo apt install auditd` and add rules per CIS recommendations |
| JSON output missing fields | Older Docker Bench version | Update to latest via `git pull` in the cloned repo |

#### 11 References

- GitHub repository: https://github.com/docker/docker-bench-security
- CIS Docker Benchmark: https://www.cisecurity.org/benchmark/docker
- Docker security documentation: https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/

### 17.3 kube-hunter

#### 1 Introduction

kube-hunter, developed by Aqua Security, is an active penetration-testing tool that hunts for security weaknesses in Kubernetes clusters the way a real attacker would. Rather than statically checking configuration files (like kube-bench does), kube-hunter actively probes network services, APIs, and exposed ports to discover exploitable vulnerabilities — things like an anonymously accessible Kubelet API, an exposed etcd port without authentication, a Dashboard exposed without RBAC, or a container that can access the cloud metadata API to steal IAM credentials.

kube-hunter can run in three main modes:

- **Remote scanning** – target a cluster from outside, given one or more IPs/hostnames.
- **Internal/pod scanning** – deployed as a pod inside the cluster, it discovers other nodes and services from an insider's perspective.
- **Network scanning** – scans an entire CIDR range or subnet to discover Kubernetes clusters.

Findings are categorized by "hunter" modules (e.g. `Access Kubelet API`, `Etcd Remote Access`, `Dashboard Exposed`, `Cloud metadata API`) and severity, and each finding is mapped to a specific attack vector with a public vulnerability description (`khv` code) explaining the exploitation path.

#### 2 Installation

**Method 1 – pip (Python package, most common on Kali):**
```bash
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install python3-pip -y
pip3 install kube-hunter --break-system-packages
```

**Method 2 – Docker container:**
```bash
docker pull aquasec/kube-hunter
docker run --rm -it aquasec/kube-hunter --remote SOME_IP
```

**Method 3 – Clone from source:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/aquasecurity/kube-hunter.git
cd kube-hunter
pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages
python3 kube_hunter/__main__.py --help
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
kube-hunter --help
```
Expected output:
```
usage: kube-hunter [-h] [--list] [--interface] [--pod] [--quick]
                    [--cidr CIDR] [--remote REMOTE [REMOTE ...]] ...
Kube-Hunter: hunts for security weaknesses in Kubernetes clusters
```

#### 3 Syntax

```
kube-hunter [OPTIONS]
```

At least one scanning scope flag is required: `--remote`, `--cidr`, `--interface`, or `--pod` (internal/active scan).

#### 4 Command-Line Options

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `--remote REMOTE [REMOTE ...]` | Scan specific remote IP(s)/hostname(s) |
| `--cidr CIDR` | Scan a CIDR range (e.g. `10.0.0.0/24`) |
| `--interface` | Auto-scan all interfaces on the local machine |
| `--pod` | Run as if inside a pod, discovering cluster components from within |
| `--active` | Enable active hunters that attempt exploitation (not just discovery) |
| `--quick` | Scan only the main, most common ports, skipping less common ones |
| `--include-patched-versions` | Don't skip vulnerabilities on patched-version software |
| `--mapping` | Only map cluster nodes/services, no vulnerability hunting |
| `--report {plain,yaml,json}` | Set output report format |
| `--dispatch {stdout,http}` | Where to send the report |
| `--log LOG_LEVEL` | Logging level: `DEBUG`, `INFO`, `WARNING` |
| `--statistics` | Include hunter statistics in report |
| `--network-timeout` | Set network timeout for probes |
| `-h, --help` | Show help |
| `--list` | List all available hunter modules and exit |
| `-x, --exclude-namespaces` | Exclude given namespaces during pod-scope hunts |

#### 5 Basic Usage

```bash
kube-hunter --remote 192.168.1.50
```

Expected output:
```
~ Started
~ Discovering Open Kubernetes Services...
+-------------------+------------------+-------------+
| SERVICE           | LOCATION         | DESCRIPTION |
+-------------------+------------------+-------------+
| Kubelet API       | 192.168.1.50:10250 | The Kubelet is the main... |
+-------------------+------------------+-------------+

~ Hunting for vulnerabilities...
+----------------------+-------------------+----------+-------------------------------+
| ID                   | LOCATION          | CATEGORY | VULNERABILITY                |
+----------------------+-------------------+----------+-------------------------------+
| KHV005               | 192.168.1.50:10250| Access Risk | Exposed Kubelet API allows anonymous access |
+----------------------+-------------------+----------+-------------------------------+

Nodes
+----------------+----------------+
| TYPE           | LOCATION       |
+----------------+----------------+
| Node/Master    | 192.168.1.50   |
+----------------+----------------+
```

#### 6 Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 – Quick scan against a single node (common ports only):**
```bash
kube-hunter --remote 192.168.1.50 --quick
```
```
~ Started (quick scan mode - common ports only)
~ Discovering Open Kubernetes Services...
+------------+---------------------+
| SERVICE    | LOCATION            |
+------------+---------------------+
| API Server | 192.168.1.50:6443   |
+------------+---------------------+
```

**Example 2 – Scan an entire subnet for clusters:**
```bash
kube-hunter --cidr 192.168.1.0/24
```
```
~ Started
~ Scanning CIDR 192.168.1.0/24 ...
+-------------------+---------------------+
| SERVICE           | LOCATION            |
+-------------------+---------------------+
| Kubernetes API    | 192.168.1.50:6443   |
| Kubelet API       | 192.168.1.51:10250  |
+-------------------+---------------------+
```

**Example 3 – Internal pod-based scan with active exploitation attempts:**
```bash
kubectl run kube-hunter --rm -ti --image=aquasec/kube-hunter -- --pod --active
```
```
~ Started in pod mode, discovering cluster from within
~ Found open service: Kubelet API 10.0.1.5:10250
~ Attempting active hunter: Kubelet Command Execution
+--------+-------------------+----------+---------------------------------------+
| ID     | LOCATION          | CATEGORY | VULNERABILITY                          |
+--------+-------------------+----------+---------------------------------------+
| KHV002 | 10.0.1.5:10250    | Remote Code Execution | Kubelet exposes execution API |
+--------+-------------------+----------+---------------------------------------+
```

**Example 4 – Output findings as JSON:**
```bash
kube-hunter --remote 192.168.1.50 --report json --dispatch stdout > findings.json
cat findings.json | python3 -m json.tool | head -20
```
```json
{
    "nodes": [
        {"type": "Node/Master", "location": "192.168.1.50"}
    ],
    "services": [
        {"service": "Kubelet API", "location": "192.168.1.50:10250"}
    ],
    "vulnerabilities": [
        {
            "id": "KHV005",
            "location": "192.168.1.50:10250",
            "vid": "None",
            "category": "Access Risk",
            "severity": "medium",
            "vulnerability": "Anonymous authentication is enabled"
        }
    ]
}
```

**Example 5 – List all available hunter modules:**
```bash
kube-hunter --list
```
```
Discovery Hunters:
  - ApiServiceDiscovery
  - KubectlClientDiscovery
  - KubeletDiscovery
Hunting Hunters:
  - AccessApiServer
  - AccessKubeletApi
  - EtcdRemoteAccess
  - DashboardExposer
  - CloudMetadataApi
  - PrivilegeEscalation
```

**Example 6 – YAML report to a file:**
```bash
kube-hunter --remote 192.168.1.50 --report yaml --dispatch stdout > report.yaml
cat report.yaml
```
```yaml
nodes:
- type: Node/Master
  location: 192.168.1.50
services:
- service: Kubelet API
  location: 192.168.1.50:10250
vulnerabilities:
- id: KHV005
  location: 192.168.1.50:10250
  category: Access Risk
  severity: medium
  vulnerability: Anonymous authentication is enabled
```

**Example 7 – Scan the local network interface (attacker on the same LAN):**
```bash
sudo kube-hunter --interface
```
```
~ Started, scanning all local network interfaces
~ Discovered network 192.168.1.0/24 on eth0
+-------------------+---------------------+
| SERVICE           | LOCATION            |
+-------------------+---------------------+
| Kubernetes API    | 192.168.1.50:6443   |
+-------------------+---------------------+
```

**Example 8 – Enable debug logging for troubleshooting a scan:**
```bash
kube-hunter --remote 192.168.1.50 --log DEBUG
```
```
[DEBUG] Sending probe to 192.168.1.50:443
[DEBUG] Sending probe to 192.168.1.50:6443
[DEBUG] Response received: HTTP 401 Unauthorized
~ Discovering Open Kubernetes Services...
```

#### 7 Common Use Cases

- **Red team / penetration testing engagements** against Kubernetes clusters to identify real, exploitable exposure — not just configuration drift.
- **Post-deployment verification** that a newly built cluster is not accidentally exposing the Kubelet, etcd, or Dashboard to the internet.
- **Insider-threat simulation** by deploying kube-hunter as a pod to see what a compromised workload could discover and reach.
- **Subnet reconnaissance** during an internal network penetration test to discover the existence of undocumented "shadow" Kubernetes clusters.
- **Validating network policies** — confirming that NetworkPolicies actually block lateral movement to sensitive components like etcd.

#### 8 Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# kube-hunter-scan.sh - Automated remote scan with JSON archiving and alerting
set -euo pipefail

TARGET="$1"
OUTFILE="kube-hunter-$(date +%F-%H%M).json"

echo "[*] Running kube-hunter against $TARGET ..."
kube-hunter --remote "$TARGET" --report json --dispatch stdout --log WARNING > "$OUTFILE"

CRITICAL_COUNT=$(python3 -c "
import json
data = json.load(open('$OUTFILE'))
vulns = data.get('vulnerabilities', [])
print(sum(1 for v in vulns if v.get('severity') in ('high', 'critical')))
")

echo "[*] High/Critical findings: $CRITICAL_COUNT"

if [ "$CRITICAL_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "[!] Critical exposure detected! Review $OUTFILE immediately."
  exit 1
fi

echo "[+] No critical exposures found."
```

Run it:
```bash
chmod +x kube-hunter-scan.sh
./kube-hunter-scan.sh 192.168.1.50
```

#### 9 Tips and Best Practices

- Always get **written authorization** before running kube-hunter (especially `--active`) against any cluster you do not own — active hunters attempt real exploitation such as remote code execution via the Kubelet API.
- Start with discovery-only scans (no `--active`) to map exposure before attempting exploitation.
- Run in `--pod` mode periodically inside production clusters to simulate what a compromised workload could reach; combine with strict NetworkPolicies to shrink the blast radius.
- Use `--quick` for fast triage across many targets, then follow up with a full scan on interesting hosts.
- Cross-reference findings with kube-bench and kubescape results — kube-hunter shows *what's exploitable right now*, while the others show *what's misconfigured relative to a benchmark*.
- Findings mentioning the Cloud Metadata API (KHV) are especially high priority — they often lead directly to cloud account compromise via stolen IAM credentials.

#### 10 Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| No services discovered on a known cluster | Firewall/NetworkPolicy blocking probes | Confirm network path with `nc`/`nmap` first, or run in `--pod` mode from inside the cluster |
| `ModuleNotFoundError` on pip install | Missing dependency | Reinstall with `pip3 install -r requirements.txt --break-system-packages` |
| Scan is very slow | Full port scan on large CIDR | Use `--quick` or narrow the `--cidr` range |
| `--active` hunters don't run | Active hunters not enabled by default | Explicitly pass `--active` |
| Permission denied on `--interface` | Requires raw socket access | Run with `sudo` |

#### 11 References

- GitHub repository: https://github.com/aquasecurity/kube-hunter
- Vulnerability catalog (KHV codes): https://aquasecurity.github.io/kube-hunter/
- Aqua Security blog on Kubernetes attacks: https://www.aquasec.com/blog/

### 17.4 kube-bench

#### 1 Introduction

kube-bench, developed by Aqua Security, is an open-source Go application that checks whether a Kubernetes cluster (control-plane nodes, etcd nodes, and worker nodes) is deployed according to the **CIS Kubernetes Benchmark**. Where kube-hunter tests a cluster from an attacker's perspective by actively probing it, kube-bench tests it from a compliance perspective by inspecting configuration files, running processes, and file permissions on each node and comparing them against the benchmark's recommended settings.

kube-bench automatically detects the Kubernetes version and platform (self-managed, EKS, GKE, AKS, OpenShift, RKE, k3s, etc.) and selects the correct benchmark test set (`cis-1.23`, `cis-1.24`, `eks-1.2.0`, `gke-1.2.0`, etc.). Each check has a unique ID (e.g. `1.2.1`), a description, a remediation suggestion, and a result of `PASS`, `FAIL`, `WARN`, or `INFO`.

#### 2 Installation

**Method 1 – Binary release (Kali/Debian, most common):**
```bash
curl -L https://github.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench/releases/download/v0.9.3/kube-bench_0.9.3_linux_amd64.deb -o kube-bench.deb
sudo dpkg -i kube-bench.deb
```

**Method 2 – Install via Go:**
```bash
go install github.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench@latest
```

**Method 3 – Run as a Docker container against the host:**
```bash
docker run --pid=host -v /etc:/etc:ro -v /var:/var:ro -v /usr/bin/kubectl:/usr/bin/kubectl:ro \
  -v $(which kubelet):/usr/bin/kubelet:ro \
  aquasec/kube-bench:latest --version 1.24
```

**Method 4 – Run as a Kubernetes Job inside the cluster (recommended for control-plane checks):**
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench/main/job.yaml
kubectl logs -l app=kube-bench
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
kube-bench version
```
Expected output:
```
kube-bench version: 0.9.3
```

#### 3 Syntax

```
kube-bench [command] [flags]
```

Primary command is `run`, optionally scoped with `--targets`.

#### 4 Command-Line Options

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `run` | Run the benchmark checks (default command) |
| `--targets` | Node types to test: `master,node,etcd,policies,controlplane` |
| `--benchmark` | Force a specific benchmark version (e.g. `cis-1.24`, `eks-1.2.0`, `gke-1.2.0`) |
| `--version` | Force a specific Kubernetes version's test set |
| `-f, --check` | Run only specific check IDs, comma-separated (e.g. `1.1.1,1.1.2`) |
| `-s, --skip` | Skip specific check IDs |
| `--config-dir` | Path to the directory containing benchmark YAML definitions |
| `--config` | Path to `config.yaml` defining node type mappings |
| `-j, --json` | Output results as JSON |
| `--outputfile` | Write output to a specified file |
| `-D, --dashboard` | Output for the kube-bench dashboard format |
| `--include-test-output` | Include raw command output for each check in the report |
| `--noremediations` | Suppress remediation text in output |
| `--noresults` | Suppress the actual PASS/FAIL results (summary only) |
| `--nosummary` | Suppress the summary line |
| `-v, --v` | Log verbosity level |
| `-h, --help` | Show help |

#### 5 Basic Usage

```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets master,node
```

Expected output (truncated):
```
[INFO] 1 Control Plane Security Configuration
[INFO] 1.1 Control Plane Node Configuration Files
[PASS] 1.1.1 Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive (Automated)
[FAIL] 1.1.9 Ensure that the admission control plugin AlwaysPullImages is set (Automated)
[WARN] 1.1.20 Ensure that the Kubernetes PKI directory and file ownership is set to root:root (Manual)

== Summary master ==
45 checks PASS
12 checks FAIL
8 checks WARN
0 checks INFO

[INFO] 4 Worker Node Security Configuration
[PASS] 4.1.1 Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive (Automated)
[FAIL] 4.2.6 Ensure that the --protect-kernel-defaults argument is set to true (Automated)

== Summary node ==
20 checks PASS
5 checks FAIL
2 checks WARN

== Summary total ==
65 checks PASS
17 checks FAIL
10 checks WARN
0 checks INFO
```

#### 6 Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 – Run only the master/control-plane checks:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets master
```
```
[INFO] 1 Control Plane Security Configuration
[PASS] 1.2.1 Ensure that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false (Automated)
[FAIL] 1.2.6 Ensure that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is set as appropriate (Automated)

== Summary master ==
38 checks PASS
9 checks FAIL
6 checks WARN
```

**Example 2 – Force a specific benchmark for a managed EKS cluster:**
```bash
kube-bench run --benchmark eks-1.2.0
```
```
[INFO] 3 Worker Node Security Configuration
[PASS] 3.1.1 Ensure that the kubeconfig file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive (Automated)
[FAIL] 3.2.1 Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created (Automated)

== Summary total ==
24 checks PASS
6 checks FAIL
```

**Example 3 – Output results as JSON:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets node --json > node-results.json
cat node-results.json | python3 -m json.tool | head -25
```
```json
{
    "Controls": [
        {
            "id": "4",
            "text": "Worker Node Security Configuration",
            "tests": [
                {
                    "section": "4.1",
                    "results": [
                        {
                            "test_number": "4.1.1",
                            "test_desc": "Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive",
                            "status": "PASS"
                        }
                    ]
                }
            ]
        }
    ]
}
```

**Example 4 – Run a single check by ID:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --check 1.2.6
```
```
[FAIL] 1.2.6 Ensure that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is set as appropriate (Automated)

== Remediation ==
Follow the Kubernetes documentation and set the --kubelet-certificate-authority
parameter to the path to the cert file for the certificate authority.

== Summary ==
0 checks PASS
1 checks FAIL
0 checks WARN
```

**Example 5 – Skip known/accepted-risk checks:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets node --skip 4.2.6,4.2.10
```
```
== Summary node ==
23 checks PASS
3 checks FAIL
2 checks WARN
(2 checks skipped)
```

**Example 6 – Run inside the cluster as a Job and view logs:**
```bash
kubectl apply -f job.yaml
kubectl get pods -l app=kube-bench
kubectl logs kube-bench-4kjpl
```
```
NAME                READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
kube-bench-4kjpl    0/1     Completed   0          8s

[INFO] 4 Worker Node Security Configuration
[PASS] 4.1.1 Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
== Summary total ==
21 checks PASS
4 checks FAIL
```

**Example 7 – Suppress remediation text for a shorter report:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets master --noremediations
```
```
[FAIL] 1.1.9 Ensure that the admission control plugin AlwaysPullImages is set (Automated)
[FAIL] 1.2.6 Ensure that the --kubelet-certificate-authority argument is set as appropriate (Automated)

== Summary master ==
45 checks PASS
12 checks FAIL
```

**Example 8 – Write output to a file with test command output included:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --targets node --include-test-output --outputfile node-detailed.txt
cat node-detailed.txt | head -15
```
```
[PASS] 4.1.1 Ensure that the kubelet service file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
    Command: 'stat -c permissions=%a /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service.d/10-kubeadm.conf'
    Output: permissions=644
```

**Example 9 – Run using a custom config directory for a non-standard distro:**
```bash
sudo kube-bench run --config-dir /opt/kube-bench/cfg --benchmark rke-cis-1.6
```
```
[INFO] Using benchmark: RKE CIS 1.6
[PASS] 1.1.1 Ensure that the API server pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
== Summary total ==
30 checks PASS
5 checks FAIL
```

#### 7 Common Use Cases

- **Pre-production compliance gate** for newly provisioned Kubernetes clusters, self-managed or managed.
- **Continuous compliance monitoring** via a scheduled Kubernetes CronJob that runs kube-bench and ships results to a dashboard.
- **Audit evidence generation** for SOC2/ISO27001/PCI-DSS assessments that require CIS Benchmark alignment.
- **Post-upgrade validation** — re-running after a Kubernetes version upgrade to catch new/changed benchmark controls.
- **Managed cloud cluster hardening** — using platform-specific benchmarks (`eks-1.2.0`, `gke-1.2.0`, `aks-1.2.0`) to validate provider-recommended settings.

#### 8 Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# kube-bench-audit.sh - Run kube-bench cluster-wide and summarize failures
set -euo pipefail

OUTPUT="kube-bench-$(date +%F).json"

echo "[*] Running kube-bench across all targets..."
sudo kube-bench run --targets master,node,etcd,policies --json > "$OUTPUT"

FAIL_COUNT=$(python3 -c "
import json
data = json.load(open('$OUTPUT'))
count = 0
for control in data.get('Controls', []):
    for test in control.get('tests', []):
        for r in test.get('results', []):
            if r.get('status') == 'FAIL':
                count += 1
print(count)
")

echo "[*] Total FAIL checks: $FAIL_COUNT"

if [ "$FAIL_COUNT" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "[!] Cluster failed $FAIL_COUNT CIS benchmark checks. See $OUTPUT"
  exit 1
fi

echo "[+] Cluster passed all CIS Kubernetes Benchmark checks."
```

Schedule as a weekly CronJob inside the cluster, or via host cron:
```bash
crontab -e
0 3 * * 0 /opt/scripts/kube-bench-audit.sh >> /var/log/kube-bench-cron.log 2>&1
```

#### 9 Tips and Best Practices

- Always let kube-bench auto-detect the benchmark version first; only force `--benchmark` when auto-detection picks the wrong managed-platform variant.
- Run separately on each node type (`master`, `node`, `etcd`) rather than always running all targets — some checks are only meaningful on specific node roles.
- On managed clusters (EKS/GKE/AKS), expect many control-plane checks to show `INFO` since the cloud provider manages that layer — focus remediation effort on worker-node and policy checks you actually control.
- Track FAIL count over time as a trend metric, not just a pass/fail gate — CIS compliance is rarely 100% achievable immediately and should improve incrementally.
- Combine kube-bench (compliance posture) with kube-hunter (active exploitability) for a complete picture: a FAIL in kube-bench doesn't always mean it's currently exploitable, and a PASS doesn't guarantee no exploitable paths exist.
- Store historical JSON reports in version control or an artifact store to demonstrate compliance trends to auditors.

#### 10 Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `error: unable to detect the Kubernetes version` | Running outside cluster context without kubeconfig | Set `--version` manually or run as an in-cluster Job |
| All checks show `[INFO]` only | Wrong `--targets` for the node role | Confirm node role and set `--targets` accordingly |
| `permission denied` reading config files | Not run with sudo/root | Re-run with `sudo` |
| Wrong benchmark selected on managed cluster | Auto-detection heuristic mismatch | Explicitly pass `--benchmark eks-1.2.0` (or gke/aks equivalent) |
| Job pod stuck in `Pending` | Missing node selector/tolerations for control-plane nodes | Edit `job-master.yaml` tolerations to match your control-plane taints |

#### 11 References

- GitHub repository: https://github.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench
- CIS Kubernetes Benchmark: https://www.cisecurity.org/benchmark/kubernetes
- Aqua Security documentation: https://aquasecurity.github.io/kube-bench/

### 17.5 kubescape

#### 1 Introduction

Kubescape, originally created by ARMO and now a CNCF project, is a comprehensive open-source Kubernetes security platform that unifies several security functions into a single tool: compliance scanning against recognized frameworks (CIS Kubernetes Benchmark, NSA/CISA Kubernetes Hardening Guidance, MITRE ATT&CK for Containers, PCI-DSS, SOC2), image vulnerability scanning, Kubernetes manifest/Helm-chart misconfiguration scanning, RBAC visualization, and a continuous risk-scoring engine.

Where kube-bench focuses purely on the CIS benchmark and kube-hunter focuses purely on active exploitation, kubescape aims to be the "single pane of glass" — it can scan a cluster, a set of YAML files, or a Helm chart, and produces a **risk score** (0–100%) alongside detailed, framework-mapped findings with remediation guidance. It can also generate a network policy recommendation based on observed traffic, and export results to popular formats (JSON, JUnit, SARIF, PDF, HTML) for integration into CI/CD and ticketing systems.

Key capabilities:

- **Cluster scan** – connects via kubeconfig and evaluates live cluster resources.
- **Manifest/file scan** – scans local YAML/Helm files before deployment (shift-left).
- **Image scan** – integrated vulnerability scanning (similar to Trivy) for container images referenced in the cluster.
- **RBAC analysis** – visualizes over-privileged service accounts and roles.
- **Compliance frameworks** – `cis-v1.23-t1.0.1`, `nsa`, `mitre`, `soc2`, and custom frameworks.
- **Continuous monitoring** – runs as an in-cluster operator with a scheduled scan and dashboard.

#### 2 Installation

**Method 1 – Install script (recommended):**
```bash
curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubescape/kubescape/master/install.sh | /bin/bash
```

**Method 2 – Via Homebrew (if brew is set up on Kali):**
```bash
brew install kubescape
```

**Method 3 – Binary download:**
```bash
curl -LO https://github.com/kubescape/kubescape/releases/latest/download/kubescape-ubuntu-latest
chmod +x kubescape-ubuntu-latest
sudo mv kubescape-ubuntu-latest /usr/local/bin/kubescape
```

**Method 4 – Docker container:**
```bash
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd):/results" -v "$HOME/.kube/config:/root/.kube/config" \
  quay.io/kubescape/kubescape:latest scan --format json --output /results/results.json
```

**Verify installation:**
```bash
kubescape version
```
Expected output:
```
Your current version is: v3.0.15
```

#### 3 Syntax

```
kubescape <command> [flags]
```

Primary commands: `scan`, `list`, `download`, `fix`, `patch`, `update`.

Scan sub-forms:
```
kubescape scan
kubescape scan framework <framework-name>
kubescape scan control <control-name>
kubescape scan image <image-name>
kubescape scan manifest <path>
```

#### 4 Command-Line Options

| Flag | Description |
|---|---|
| `framework` | Scan against a named framework (e.g. `cis-v1.23-t1.0.1`, `nsa`, `mitre`) |
| `control` | Scan a single named/ID'd control |
| `image` | Scan a container image for vulnerabilities |
| `--format` | Output format: `pretty-printer`, `json`, `junit`, `sarif`, `pdf`, `html` |
| `--output, -o` | Write report to a file |
| `--severity-threshold` | Fail only above a given severity |
| `--compliance-threshold` | Minimum acceptable compliance score (percentage) |
| `--exceptions` | Path to an exceptions file to suppress known findings |
| `--include-namespaces` | Restrict scan to specific namespaces |
| `--exclude-namespaces` | Exclude specific namespaces |
| `--fail-threshold` | Exit non-zero if score below this percentage |
| `--enable-host-scan` | Enable host-level sensor scanning (DaemonSet) |
| `--submit` | Submit results to the ARMO/Kubescape SaaS dashboard |
| `--kubeconfig` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `--use-from` | Use cached rule/control definitions instead of downloading |
| `--verbose, -v` | Verbose output |
| `-h, --help` | Show help |

`kubescape list` options:
| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `kubescape list frameworks` | List available compliance frameworks |
| `kubescape list controls` | List all individual controls |
| `kubescape list exceptions` | List configured exceptions |

#### 5 Basic Usage

```bash
kubescape scan
```

Expected output (truncated):
```
[INFO] Downloading configuration...
[INFO] Scanning cluster: my-cluster
[INFO] Done scanning namespace: default
[INFO] Done scanning namespace: kube-system

┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬────────┬───────────┐
│                  CONTROL NAME               │ STATUS │ % COMPLIANCE │
├───────────────────────────────────────────┼────────┼───────────┤
│ Privileged container                        │ failed │ 40%       │
│ Resource limits                             │ failed │ 25%       │
│ Host PID/IPC privileges                     │ passed │ 100%      │
│ Allowed hostPath                            │ failed │ 60%       │
│ Automatic mapping of service account        │ failed │ 30%       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────┴────────┴───────────┘

Resources Summary
+-------------------+--------+--------+
| Severity          | Resources | %   |
+-------------------+--------+--------+
| Critical          | 4         | -   |
| High              | 12        | -   |
| Medium            | 25        | -   |

Overall risk-score (0-100) -> 68
```

#### 6 Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 – Scan the cluster against the NSA/CISA framework:**
```bash
kubescape scan framework nsa
```

```
[INFO] Scanning with framework: NSA-1.2
┌───────────────────────────────┬────────┬────────────┐

│ Immutable container filesystem │ failed │ 20%        │
```

```
Overall risk-score (0-100) -> 76
```

```bash
kubescape scan --enable-host-scan
```

**Example 11 – Scan the MITRE ATT&CK framework and view technique mapping:**
```bash

```

```
[INFO] Scanning with framework: MITRE ATT&CK for Containers
┌────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┬────────┐
│ TACTIC                  │ TECHNIQUE                  │ STATUS │
```

```bash

```
```
[INFO] Analyzing ./k8s/deployment.yaml
[INFO] Applying fix: add 'runAsNonRoot: true' to securityContext
[INFO] Applying fix: add resource limits (cpu/memory)
[INFO] Fixed file written to ./k8s/deployment.fixed.yaml
```

#### 7 Common Use Cases

- **Unified security posture dashboard** combining compliance, vulnerabilities, and RBAC risk in one score.
- **Shift-left scanning** of Helm charts and raw manifests in CI/CD before they ever reach a cluster.
- **Multi-framework compliance** for organizations that must simultaneously demonstrate CIS, NSA, and MITRE ATT&CK alignment.
- **RBAC least-privilege review** to find over-permissioned service accounts and cluster-role bindings.
- **Executive reporting** via HTML/PDF export for non-technical stakeholders and auditors.
- **Continuous in-cluster monitoring** using the Kubescape Operator for scheduled scans and drift alerts.

#### 8 Automation with Bash

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# kubescape-ci-gate.sh - Scan manifests in CI and block merge on low compliance
set -euo pipefail

MANIFEST_DIR="$1"
THRESHOLD=75
REPORT="kubescape-report-$(date +%F).json"

echo "[*] Scanning manifests in $MANIFEST_DIR ..."
kubescape scan manifest "$MANIFEST_DIR" \
  --format json \
  --output "$REPORT" \
  --compliance-threshold "$THRESHOLD"

STATUS=$?
SCORE=$(python3 -c "
import json
data = json.load(open('$REPORT'))
print(data.get('summaryDetails', {}).get('score', 'N/A'))
")

echo "[*] Compliance score: $SCORE%"

if [ "$STATUS" -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "[!] Manifests failed the compliance gate (threshold: ${THRESHOLD}%)."
  exit 1
fi

echo "[+] Manifests passed the compliance gate."
```

Run it:
```bash
chmod +x kubescape-ci-gate.sh
./kubescape-ci-gate.sh ./k8s/
```

#### 9 Tips and Best Practices

- Start with the `nsa` or `armobest` framework for a practical, actionable baseline before attempting full CIS compliance, which can be extensive.
- Use `kubescape scan manifest` in pull-request CI checks so misconfigurations are caught before merge, not after deployment.
- Maintain an `exceptions.json` file in version control (with ticket references) for accepted risks rather than silently ignoring failed controls.
- Combine the risk score trend over time (not a single snapshot) as your real KPI — a rising score indicates genuine security debt reduction.
- Use `kubescape scan image` as a complementary check alongside Trivy; results are usually consistent since both draw from similar vulnerability databases, but cross-verification catches DB staleness.
- Enable `--enable-host-scan` periodically (not on every CI run, since it deploys a DaemonSet) to capture node-level kernel/OS hardening gaps that manifest-only scans miss.
- Review RBAC findings carefully — overly broad `ClusterRoleBinding`s to `cluster-admin` are consistently among the highest-risk findings in real clusters.

#### 10 Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Error: unable to connect to cluster` | Invalid or missing kubeconfig context | Set `--kubeconfig` explicitly or run `kubectl config current-context` to verify |
| Scan takes very long on large clusters | Scanning all namespaces including system ones | Use `--include-namespaces` to scope the scan |
| Framework download fails (air-gapped env) | No internet access to fetch control definitions | Use `--use-from` with a pre-downloaded framework file via `kubescape download framework nsa --output nsa.json` |
| Host-scan DaemonSet stuck `Pending` | Insufficient node resources or taints | Adjust resource requests or add tolerations in the host-scanner spec |
| Exceptions not being applied | Malformed exceptions JSON schema | Validate against the schema shown in `kubescape list exceptions --help` |
| `fix` command doesn't fully resolve findings | Some controls require manual/context-aware decisions | Use `fix` output as a starting point, then manually review the diff |

#### 11 References

- Official documentation: https://kubescape.io/docs/
- GitHub repository: https://github.com/kubescape/kubescape
- CNCF project page: https://www.cncf.io/projects/kubescape/
- MITRE ATT&CK for Containers: https://attack.mitre.org/matrices/enterprise/containers/


## Chapter 18 – Reverse Engineering

Reverse engineering is the process of analyzing a compiled program, firmware image, or binary artifact to understand how it works without having access to its original source code. It sits at the core of malware analysis, vulnerability research, exploit development, CTF (Capture The Flag) challenges, software auditing, and digital forensics. Where a developer starts with source code and compiles it down into machine instructions, a reverse engineer works in the opposite direction — starting from raw bytes, disassembled instructions, or runtime behavior, and building a mental (and often documented) model of what the program does, how it does it, and why.

This chapter is dedicated to the tools that make this process possible on Kali Linux, one of the most widely used penetration testing and security research distributions. Kali ships with, or makes readily available, a mature ecosystem of reverse engineering utilities that range from full graphical disassemblers and decompilers to small, sharp command-line utilities that do one job extremely well.

Broadly, the reverse engineering workflow can be split into two complementary approaches:

- **Static analysis** — examining a binary without executing it. This includes disassembling machine code back into assembly language, decompiling assembly into pseudo-C, extracting embedded strings, inspecting file headers and sections, and mapping out function calls and control flow. Tools such as Ghidra, Radare2, Cutter, objdump, and strings fall primarily into this category.
- **Dynamic analysis** — examining a binary while it runs. This includes tracing system calls, tracing library calls, monitoring memory access, attaching debuggers, and observing runtime behavior such as network connections or file I/O. Tools such as ltrace and strace fall into this category, and are typically used alongside debuggers like GDB.

In practice, effective reverse engineering blends both approaches. A static pass with Ghidra or Radare2 helps build a structural understanding of a binary — its functions, imports, and control flow — while a dynamic pass with strace or ltrace confirms what the binary actually does when executed against real inputs. strings and objdump are often the fastest first steps, giving quick, low-effort insight before committing to a deeper static or dynamic analysis session.

**Legal and ethical note:** All examples in this chapter use small, deliberately compiled test programs or standard system binaries for demonstration purposes. Reverse engineering software you do not own or do not have explicit permission to analyze may violate software license agreements or local law. Always ensure you have proper authorization before analyzing third-party binaries, and use these techniques responsibly within legal and ethical boundaries such as authorized CTF competitions, personal projects, or explicitly licensed security research.

### 18.1 Ghidra

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Ghidra is a free and open-source software reverse engineering (SRE) framework developed by the National Security Agency (NSA)'s Research Directorate and released publicly in 2019. It is one of the most feature-complete reverse engineering platforms available at no cost, comparable in capability to commercial tools such as IDA Pro. Ghidra provides disassembly, a powerful native decompiler that converts machine code into readable pseudo-C, a graphical control-flow-graph viewer, a scripting engine (Java and Python via Jython/PyGhidra), a full plugin/extension architecture, project management for multi-binary analysis, version tracking, and built-in support for a very wide range of processor architectures (x86, x86_64, ARM, AArch64, MIPS, PowerPC, RISC-V, SPARC, and dozens more).

What sets Ghidra apart for most reverse engineers is its decompiler quality — rather than reading raw assembly instruction by instruction, analysts can read machine-generated C-like pseudocode with named variables, recovered types, and simplified control flow, dramatically accelerating the process of understanding what a function actually does. Ghidra also supports headless (non-GUI, scriptable) analysis, making it usable in automated pipelines and CI environments, not just interactive sessions.

#### 2. How to Install

Ghidra requires a Java Runtime Environment (JDK 17 or later for modern Ghidra releases) and is distributed as a platform-independent archive.

**Method 1 — Kali repository package (simplest):**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ghidra -y
```

**Method 2 — Manual install from official release (recommended for latest version):**

```bash
sudo apt install openjdk-21-jdk -y
wget https://github.com/NationalSecurityAgency/ghidra/releases/download/Ghidra_11.2_build/ghidra_11.2_PUBLIC_20240926.zip
unzip ghidra_11.2_PUBLIC_20240926.zip -d /opt/
sudo ln -s /opt/ghidra_11.2_PUBLIC/ghidraRun /usr/local/bin/ghidra
```

Verify installation:

```bash
$ ghidra --version
Ghidra 11.2 PUBLIC
```

Launch the GUI:

```bash
$ ghidraRun
```

#### 3. Syntax

**GUI launcher:**

```bash
ghidraRun
```

**Headless analyzer (for automation/scripting, located in the install directory's `support/` folder):**

```bash
analyzeHeadless <project_location> <project_name>[/<folder_path>] [options]
```

#### 4. Command-Line Options (Headless Analyzer, Kali Linux)

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-import <file/dir>` | Import a file or directory of files into the project |
| `-process [<name>]` | Process already-imported program(s) matching name (wildcards allowed) |
| `-postScript <script> [args]` | Run a post-analysis script after auto-analysis |
| `-preScript <script> [args]` | Run a script before auto-analysis |
| `-scriptPath <dir>` | Add a directory to the script search path |
| `-propertiesPath <dir>` | Additional directory for script properties |
| `-overwrite` | Overwrite existing program with same name on import |
| `-recursive` | Recursively import files from a directory |
| `-deleteProject` | Delete the project when the headless run finishes |
| `-noanalysis` | Skip automatic analysis |
| `-analysisTimeoutPerFile <secs>` | Timeout per file for auto-analysis |
| `-processor <languageID>` | Force a specific processor/language spec for raw binaries |
| `-cspec <compilerSpecID>` | Force a specific compiler spec |
| `-loader <loaderClass>` | Force a specific loader class |
| `-readOnly` | Do not save changes back to the project |
| `-max-cpu <num>` | Max number of CPU cores to use for analysis |
| `-log <path>` | Write log to specified file |
| `-scriptlog <path>` | Write script log to specified file |
| `-keystore <path>` | Keystore for shared/server project authentication |
| `-connect <user>` | Connect to a Ghidra Server as specified user |
| `-p` | Prompt for password when connecting to a server |
| `-commit ["<comment>"]` | Commit changes back to a shared/server repository |
| `-okToDelete` | Confirm deletion of pre-existing programs during `-process` |

**GUI keyboard/menu-based "options" (most-used):**

| Action | Location |
|---|---|
| Auto Analyze | `Analysis → Auto Analyze` |
| Decompile pane | `Window → Decompiler` (opens automatically alongside Listing) |
| Symbol Tree | `Window → Symbol Tree` |
| Function Graph | `Window → Function Graph` |
| Script Manager | `Window → Script Manager` |
| Defined Strings | `Window → Defined Strings` |
| Bookmarks | `Window → Bookmarks` |
| Patch Instruction | Right-click instruction → `Patch Instruction` |
| Rename variable | Right-click variable in decompiler → `Rename Variable` |
| Retype variable | Right-click variable → `Retype Variable` |
| Create function | Right-click address → `Create Function` |

#### 5. Basic Usage

**GUI workflow:**

```bash
$ ghidraRun
```

1. `File → New Project` → choose Non-Shared Project → name it and pick a directory.
2. `File → Import File` → select the binary (e.g., `./sample_binary`).
3. Double-click the imported file to open the CodeBrowser.
4. When prompted "Analyze?", click **Yes**, keep default analyzers, click **Analyze**.
5. Once complete, browse `Symbol Tree → Functions`, click `main`, and read the Decompile pane on the right.

**Headless CLI equivalent:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects MyProject -import ./sample_binary -postScript ExportDecompiled.java

INFO  ANALYZING all memory (HighFunctionDBUtil)
INFO  Analysis succeeded for file: /sample_binary
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Import and auto-analyze a binary headlessly:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects proj1 -import ./hello -overwrite

Ghidra Headless Analyzer 11.2
INFO  Opening non-shared project: /home/kali/ghidra_projects/proj1
INFO  IMPORTING: /home/kali/hello
INFO  Auto-analysis starting for: hello
INFO  Auto-analysis complete for: hello (5.2 secs)
```

**Example 2 — Batch-import an entire directory of samples recursively:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects malware_batch -import ./samples/ -recursive -overwrite

INFO  IMPORTING: /home/kali/samples/sample1.exe
INFO  IMPORTING: /home/kali/samples/sample2.exe
INFO  Auto-analysis complete for: sample1.exe (8.1 secs)
INFO  Auto-analysis complete for: sample2.exe (6.4 secs)
```

**Example 3 — Run a headless script to list all function names:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects proj1 -process hello -noanalysis -postScript ListFunctions.py

main
puts
__libc_start_main
_init
_fini
```

*(`ListFunctions.py`, saved to the Ghidra scripts folder, iterates `currentProgram.getFunctionManager().getFunctions(True)` and prints each name.)*

**Example 4 — Export decompiled C pseudocode of every function to a text file via headless script:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects proj1 -process hello -postScript ExportDecompiled.java /home/kali/output/hello_decomp.c

INFO  SCRIPT: ExportDecompiled.java
INFO  Wrote decompilation for 7 functions to /home/kali/output/hello_decomp.c
$ head -8 /home/kali/output/hello_decomp.c

undefined8 main(void)
{
  puts("Hello, RE!");
  return 0;
}
```

**Example 5 — GUI: Viewing decompiled output for `main` after auto-analysis:**

```
Decompile: main

undefined8 main(void)

{
  puts("Enter password: ");
  fgets(local_38,0x20,stdin);
  if (strcmp(local_38,"S3cr3tP@ss\n") == 0) {
    puts("Access Granted.");
  }
  else {
    puts("Access Denied.");
  }
  return 0;
}
```

**Example 6 — Rename a variable in the decompiler for clarity (GUI):**
Right-click `local_38` in the Decompile pane → `Rename Variable` → type `user_input` → Enter. The decompiler pane instantly updates every occurrence:

```
fgets(user_input,0x20,stdin);
if (strcmp(user_input,"S3cr3tP@ss\n") == 0) {
```

**Example 7 — Use the built-in Python script console to enumerate cross-references to a function:**

```python
# In Ghidra's Script Manager, create a new script "xrefs_to_strcmp.py"
from ghidra.program.model.symbol import RefType
fm = currentProgram.getFunctionManager()
func = getFunction("strcmp")
refs = getReferencesTo(func.getEntryPoint())
for r in refs:
    print("strcmp called from: " + str(r.getFromAddress()))
```

Output in the Console window:

```
strcmp called from: 00101185
strcmp called from: 001012f0
```

**Example 8 — Search for all defined strings containing "password":**
`Window → Defined Strings`, then filter box: `password`

```
Address     Value
00102008    "Enter password: "
00102030    "S3cr3tP@ss"
```

**Example 9 — Function Graph view for visualizing control flow of a function with branches:**
`Window → Function Graph` while `main` is selected shows colored basic blocks connected by green (true-branch) and red (false-branch) edges — useful for quickly spotting the "success" vs "failure" path in a crackme.

**Example 10 — Diff two versions of a binary using Version Tracking:**
`File → Configure → Version Tracking` (or the Version Tracking tool), select `hello_v1` as source and `hello_v2` as destination, run automatic correlators. Output panel lists matched/unmatched functions:

```
Function          Match %    Status
main              94%        Accepted (Exact Match)
check_license     61%        Accepted (Structural Match)
new_telemetry     --         Unmatched (added in v2)
```

**Example 11 — Extract all imported symbols headlessly for a threat-intel style report:**

```bash
$ analyzeHeadless /home/kali/ghidra_projects proj1 -process sample.exe -postScript ExportImports.py /home/kali/output/imports.txt
$ cat /home/kali/output/imports.txt

kernel32.dll!CreateFileA
kernel32.dll!WriteFile
ws2_32.dll!connect
advapi32.dll!RegSetValueExA
```

**Example 12 — Patch an instruction directly in the GUI (e.g., flip a conditional jump for testing):**
Right-click the `JNZ` instruction at address `00101198` in the Listing view → `Patch Instruction` → change to `JMP` → Ghidra updates both the Listing and the Decompile view to reflect the patched logic (note: this modifies Ghidra's analysis database only, not the original file on disk, unless separately exported).

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Deep static analysis of malware samples, including decompilation to quickly understand logic without manually reading raw assembly.
- Solving CTF reverse-engineering challenges (crackmes, obfuscated binaries) using the decompiler and cross-reference tools.
- Vulnerability research — locating unsafe function usage (`strcpy`, `sprintf`, `system`) and tracing data flow from user input to dangerous sinks.
- Firmware analysis for embedded/IoT devices using Ghidra's broad processor architecture support (MIPS, ARM, PowerPC, etc.).
- Building custom automated analysis pipelines using the headless analyzer plus Java/Python scripts for large-scale sample triage.
- Patch diffing between two versions of the same binary using Version Tracking to identify what a security patch changed.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

**Batch headless analysis and decompilation export for an entire malware corpus:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# ghidra_batch.sh
GHIDRA_HOME="/opt/ghidra_11.2_PUBLIC"
PROJECT_DIR="/home/kali/ghidra_projects"
SCRIPT_DIR="/home/kali/ghidra_scripts"
OUT_DIR="/home/kali/decomp_output"
mkdir -p "$OUT_DIR"

for f in ./samples/*; do
    name=$(basename "$f")
    "$GHIDRA_HOME/support/analyzeHeadless" "$PROJECT_DIR" "batch_${name}" \
        -import "$f" -overwrite \
        -scriptPath "$SCRIPT_DIR" \
        -postScript ExportDecompiled.java "$OUT_DIR/${name}.c" \
        -deleteProject
    echo "[+] Finished: $name -> $OUT_DIR/${name}.c"
done
```

**Nightly cron job for automated triage of newly dropped samples:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# nightly_ghidra_triage.sh — add to crontab: 0 2 * * * /home/kali/nightly_ghidra_triage.sh
NEW_SAMPLES=$(find /data/incoming -newer /tmp/last_run_marker -type f)
touch /tmp/last_run_marker

for f in $NEW_SAMPLES; do
    /opt/ghidra_11.2_PUBLIC/support/analyzeHeadless /data/ghidra_proj nightly \
        -import "$f" -postScript ExportImports.py "/data/reports/$(basename "$f").imports.txt" \
        -deleteProject
done
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Let auto-analysis fully complete before manually navigating — interrupting it can leave the symbol tree and decompiler output incomplete or inconsistent.
- Use the decompiler as a starting point, not absolute truth — always cross-check unusual or ambiguous decompiled logic against the raw disassembly listing, especially around optimized or hand-written assembly.
- Rename variables and functions as you understand them; Ghidra propagates these names throughout the decompiler view and cross-references, making subsequent analysis dramatically faster.
- Use `Window → Defined Strings` early — it's often faster to pivot from an interesting string straight to its cross-referencing function than to browse function-by-function.
- For headless batch processing, always pass `-deleteProject` or manage project storage carefully — Ghidra projects can consume significant disk space at scale.
- Save custom analysis scripts to a personal script directory and register it with `-scriptPath` so they're reusable across headless runs and GUI sessions alike.
- For firmware/raw binary analysis, manually specify `-processor` and base load address if Ghidra's auto-detection guesses incorrectly (common on non-ELF/PE raw dumps).

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Ghidra won't launch, no window appears | Missing or incompatible JDK | Install JDK 17+/21 (`sudo apt install openjdk-21-jdk`) and ensure it's on `PATH` |
| Analysis takes an extremely long time or hangs | Very large binary, or aggressive analyzers enabled (e.g., Decompiler Parameter ID) | Increase `-analysisTimeoutPerFile`, disable unnecessary analyzers before running |
| Decompiler shows `undefined4`/generic types everywhere | No debug info, complex/optimized code, or missing type info | Manually retype key variables/structures, or import a matching header/data type archive |
| Headless script fails with `ClassNotFoundException` | Script not in the configured script path | Add the directory with `-scriptPath`, verify the script class name matches the filename |
| Wrong architecture detected on raw firmware import | Ambiguous headerless binary | Manually specify `-processor` and base address during import |
| GUI is extremely slow / laggy | Insufficient RAM allocated to JVM | Edit `support/launch.properties` to raise `-Xmx` heap size |
| "Program was analyzed with a different version" warning on import | Sharing a Ghidra project across mismatched versions | Re-run analysis with the current version, or standardize team Ghidra versions |

#### 11. References

- Official Ghidra GitHub repository: https://github.com/NationalSecurityAgency/ghidra
- Official Ghidra documentation and installation guide (bundled `docs/` folder in every release)
- Ghidra Cheat Sheet (official): https://ghidra-sre.org/CheatSheet.html
- Kali Linux Tools listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/ghidra/
- NSA Ghidra release announcement and overview: https://www.nsa.gov/ghidra
- "The Ghidra Book: The Definitive Guide" by Chris Eagle and Kara Nance

### 18.2 Radare2

#### 1. Tool Introduction

Radare2 (commonly abbreviated `r2`) is a free and open-source framework for reverse engineering and binary analysis. Unlike GUI-first tools, Radare2 is built around a powerful, fully scriptable command-line core, structured as a suite of individual utilities (`r2`, `rabin2`, `radiff2`, `rasm2`, `rahash2`, `ragg2`, and more) that can be composed together or driven entirely through its own compact command language. Every action available interactively can also be scripted, making Radare2 exceptionally well-suited to automation, CI pipelines, and large-scale batch analysis.

Radare2 supports an enormous range of file formats and processor architectures, includes its own disassembler and (via the `r2dec` or `r2ghidra` plugins) decompilation support, a built-in hex editor, a debugger front-end (local `ptrace`-based debugging and remote `gdbserver`/`r2 -d` support), binary diffing (`radiff2`), ESIL (Evaluable Strings Intermediate Language) for emulating instruction semantics, and a visual-mode ASCII-art interface (`V`, `VV`) for graph-based navigation directly in the terminal — no GUI required. Its steep initial learning curve (driven by its terse, single-letter command grammar) is offset by unmatched flexibility and scriptability once mastered.

#### 2. How to Install

Radare2 is available in the Kali repositories, though many practitioners prefer the latest git version for newer analysis features.

**Method 1 — Kali repository package:**

```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install radare2 -y
```

**Method 2 — Latest from official installer script (recommended for latest features):**

```bash
git clone https://github.com/radareorg/radare2
cd radare2
sudo ./sys/install.sh
```

Verify installation:

```bash
$ r2 -v
radare2 5.9.8 0 @ linux-x86-64 git.5.9.8
commit: unknown build: 2024-11-02__17:22:19
```

Update an existing git-based install:

```bash
$ cd radare2 && git pull && sudo ./sys/install.sh
```

#### 3. Syntax

```bash
r2 [options] file
```

Once inside the interactive shell, commands follow Radare2's own compact grammar:

```
[command][subcommand] [arguments]
```

e.g., `pdf` = "print disassembly of function", `afl` = "analyze function list", `s main` = "seek to symbol main".

#### 4. Command-Line Options (`r2` binary itself, Kali Linux)

| Option | Description |
|---|---|
| `-a arch` | Set the target architecture (e.g., `-a arm`) |
| `-b bits` | Set bit width (16/32/64) |
| `-d` | Open the file in debug mode (run and attach a debugger) |
| `-e key=val` | Set an eval config variable at startup (e.g., `-e asm.bytes=false`) |
| `-i script` | Run an `.r2` script file after loading |
| `-c cmd` | Run a single command after loading and stay interactive |
| `-q` | Quit after running startup commands, don't enter interactive mode |
| `-qq` | Quit even faster, skip auto-analysis banner |
| `-A` | Run full analysis (`aaa`) automatically on load |
| `-AA` | Run even more aggressive analysis |
| `-n` | Do not load rabin2 info (skip binary format parsing, treat as raw) |
| `-w` | Open the file in write mode (allow patching) |
| `-p project` | Open/create a named project |
| `-P patchfile` | Apply an `.r2` patch/project file on load |
| `-v` | Show version information |
| `-L` | List all supported IO/bin/asm/anal plugins |
| `-h` | Show help/usage |
| `-s addr` | Seek to a specific address immediately on load |

**Most-used interactive core commands:**

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `aaa` | Analyze all (functions, xrefs, strings) |
| `afl` | List all analyzed functions |
| `pdf` | Print disassembly of current function |
| `pdg` | Print decompiled pseudocode (requires r2dec/r2ghidra plugin) |
| `s <addr\|symbol>` | Seek to an address or symbol |
| `s main` | Seek to the `main` function |
| `px` / `pxw` | Print hexdump / print hexdump as words |
| `iz` / `izz` | List strings in data sections / list all strings in the file |
| `ii` | List imports |
| `iE` | List exported symbols |
| `axt` | Find cross-references *to* the current address |
| `axf` | Find cross-references *from* the current address |
| `V` | Enter visual mode |
| `VV` | Enter visual graph mode (function call graph) |
| `db <addr>` | Set a breakpoint (debug mode) |
| `dc` | Continue execution (debug mode) |
| `dr` | Show registers (debug mode) |
| `wx <hex>` | Write raw hex bytes at current seek (requires `-w`) |
| `q` | Quit |

#### 5. Basic Usage

```bash
$ r2 -A ./hello

[0x00001040]> afl
0x00001139    1     23 main
0x00001030    1      6 sym.imp.puts
0x00001000    3     27 entry0

[0x00001040]> s main
[0x00001139]> pdf
┌ 23: int main(void);
│           0x00001139      f30f1efa       endbr64
│           0x0000113d      55             push rbp
│           0x0000113e      4889e5         mov rbp, rsp
│           0x00001141      488d05bc0e00.  lea rax, str.Hello__RE
│           0x00001148      4889c7         mov rdi, rax
│           0x0000114b      e8e0feffff     call sym.imp.puts
│           0x00001150      b800000000     mov eax, 0
│           0x00001155      5d             pop rbp
└           0x00001156      c3             ret
```

#### 6. Practical Examples with Output

**Example 1 — Full auto-analysis then list all functions:**

```bash
$ r2 -qc "aaa; afl" ./hello

0x00001030    1      6 sym.imp.puts
0x00001139    1     23 main
0x00001000    3     27 entry0
```

**Example 2 — Print decompiled pseudocode of `main` (requires `r2dec` or `r2ghidra` plugin installed via `r2pm`):**

```bash
$ r2pm -ci r2dec
$ r2 -qc "aaa; s main; pdd" ./hello

/* r2dec pseudo-C output for main */
int main() {
    puts("Hello, RE!");
    return 0;
}
```

**Example 3 — List all strings found in the binary:**

```bash
$ r2 -qc "izz" ./hello

vaddr=0x00002004 paddr=0x00002004 ordinal=001 sz=12 len=11 section=.rodata type=ascii string="Hello, RE!"
```

**Example 4 — List imported functions:**

```bash
$ r2 -qc "ii" ./hello

nth vaddr      bind   type   lib name
1   ---------- GLOBAL FUNC       puts
2   ---------- GLOBAL FUNC       __libc_start_main
```

**Example 5 — Use `rabin2` to quickly extract binary metadata without entering `r2`:**

```bash
$ rabin2 -I ./hello

arch     x86
bits     64
os       linux
class    ELF64
canary   true
nx       true
pic      true
stripped false
```

**Example 6 — Diff two binaries with `radiff2`:**

```bash
$ radiff2 -s ./app_v1 ./app_v2

similarity: 0.87
0x00001149 main               0x00001149 main
0x000011a2 check_license       0x000011a2 check_license
```

**Example 7 — Disassemble with `rasm2` standalone, without loading a full binary:**

```bash
$ rasm2 -d "554889e5"

push rbp
mov rbp, rsp
```

**Example 8 — Find cross-references to a suspicious function like `strcmp`:**

```bash
$ r2 -qc "aaa; s sym.imp.strcmp; axt" ./crackme

main 0x1178 [CALL] call sym.imp.strcmp
```

**Example 9 — Enter visual graph mode to inspect a function's control flow (interactive, ASCII rendering):**

```bash
$ r2 -c "aaa; s main; VV" ./crackme

  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │ 0x1149  cmp eax, 0    │
  │         je 0x1170     │
  └──────────┬────────────┘
        ┌────┴────┐
   [true]         [false]
  ┌─────▼─────┐  ┌────▼──────┐
  │ Access OK │  │ Access No │
  └───────────┘  └───────────┘
```
(Press `q` to exit visual mode.)

**Example 10 — Debug mode: run the binary under r2's debugger, set a breakpoint, and inspect registers:**

```bash
$ r2 -d ./crackme

[0x7f...]> db main
[0x7f...]> dc
hit breakpoint at: 0x561ba1149
[0x561ba1149]> dr rax
rax = 0x0000000000000000
```

**Example 11 — Hash the binary and individual sections using `rahash2`:**

```bash
$ rahash2 -a sha256 ./hello

hello: 0x00000000-0x00002050 sha256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b85
```

**Example 12 — Patch a byte in write mode and save the modified binary:**

```bash
$ r2 -w -qc "s 0x115e; wx 90 90" ./crackme
$ rabin2 -I ./crackme | grep stripped
stripped false
```
*(This example NOPs out a conditional jump at 0x115e by overwriting it with two `0x90` NOP bytes, a common technique to bypass a simple check for educational/CTF purposes on a binary you own.)*

#### 7. Common Use Cases

- Fast, entirely command-line-driven static analysis and disassembly of ELF, PE, Mach-O and other binary formats.
- Scripted, automated batch analysis of large sample sets using `r2 -qc` one-liners or `.r2` script files.
- CTF-style crackme solving: locating comparison logic, patching bytes to bypass checks, and searching for strings/gadgets.
- Binary diffing across firmware or application versions using `radiff2` to identify what changed between builds.
- Interactive debugging via r2's built-in debugger for dynamic analysis, breakpoints, and register/memory inspection.
- Exploit development support — finding ROP gadgets (with the `/R` search or the companion `rasm2`/`ragg2` tools), analyzing mitigations (canary, NX, PIE) via `rabin2 -I`.

#### 8. Automation with Bash

**Batch metadata + import extraction across a sample directory:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# r2_batch_info.sh
for f in ./samples/*; do
    echo "=== $f ==="
    rabin2 -I "$f" 2>/dev/null
    echo "--- imports ---"
    rabin2 -i "$f" 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $NF}'
    echo
done > r2_batch_report.txt
```

**Automated function-list + string extraction pipeline using r2 scripting:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# r2_auto_triage.sh
for f in ./samples/*; do
    name=$(basename "$f")
    r2 -qc "aaa; afl; izz" "$f" > "reports/${name}.r2report.txt" 2>/dev/null
    echo "[+] $name processed"
done
```

**Automated diffing of a firmware update against its previous version:**

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# firmware_diff.sh
radiff2 -s old_firmware.bin new_firmware.bin > diff_summary.txt
SIMILARITY=$(grep similarity diff_summary.txt | awk '{print $2}')
echo "Similarity score: $SIMILARITY"
if (( $(echo "$SIMILARITY < 0.90" | bc -l) )); then
    echo "[!] Significant changes detected — review diff_summary.txt"
fi
```

#### 9. Tips and Best Practices

- Always run `aaa` (or `aaaa` for the most thorough analysis on complex binaries) before browsing functions — Radare2 does not auto-analyze by default outside of `-A`.
- Use `r2 -qc "<commands>; quit"` one-liners for scripting instead of manually typing commands interactively; this is the foundation of nearly all Radare2 automation.
- Install `r2dec` or `r2ghidra` via `r2pm` early — raw disassembly-only analysis is much slower to read than decompiled pseudocode for anything beyond trivial functions.
- Learn the visual mode (`V`, then `p` to cycle print modes, `VV` for graph mode) — it is dramatically faster for navigating control flow than scrolling linear disassembly once you're comfortable with the keybindings.
- Use `rabin2 -I` early on any new sample to quickly check security mitigations (NX, PIE, canary, RELRO) before diving into deeper analysis — this instantly tells you what exploitation techniques are even feasible.
- Prefer `axt`/`axf` (cross-reference commands) over manually scrolling to trace how functions call each other; this scales far better on large binaries.
- Keep a personal library of `.r2` script files for common analysis patterns (import dumps, string extraction, function summaries) and reuse them with `-i script.r2`.

#### 10. Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| `afl` shows no/very few functions | Analysis wasn't run | Run `aaa` (or `-A` on load) before browsing functions |
| `pdd`/`pdg` command not found | Decompiler plugin not installed | Install with `r2pm -ci r2dec` or `r2pm -ci r2ghidra` |
| Visual mode graph looks broken/garbled | Terminal too small or unsupported terminal emulator | Resize terminal, or use a modern terminal (avoid basic `tty`/serial consoles) |
| `r2 -w` changes don't persist | Forgot `-w` flag, or file permissions | Always open with `-w` for patching; check file isn't read-only |
| Analysis extremely slow on large binary | Default `aaa` can be slow on huge binaries | Use lighter analysis (`aa` instead of `aaa`), or `-e anal.timeout=` |
| Debug mode (`-d`) fails to attach | Missing privileges or `ptrace` restrictions | Run with `sudo`, check Yama `ptrace_scope` setting |
| Wrong architecture/bits detected | Raw/headerless binary | Manually specify with `-a <arch> -b <bits>` |
| `r2pm` package install fails | r2pm database out of date | Run `r2pm update` before installing packages |

#### 11. References

- Official Radare2 GitHub repository: https://github.com/radareorg/radare2
- The Official Radare2 Book: https://book.rada.re/
- Kali Linux Tools listing: https://www.kali.org/tools/radare2/
- Radare2 command cheat sheet: https://github.com/radareorg/radare2/wiki/Commands
- "A Beginner's Guide to Radare2" (community wiki tutorials): https://github.com/radareorg/radare2/wiki


### 18.3 Cutter

#### 1. Tool Introduction
